studyMetadata.title: Find Rest for your Soul
studyMetadata.subtitle: Escaping addictive behaviour by finding a better joy
studyMetadata.description: A ten-session Bible study exploring the heart behind addiction — and how the Gospel offers more than any false promise can deliver. Working through Scripture, each session combines close reading with personal reflection.
studyMetadata.author: Rick Creighton
studyMetadata.shareMessage:
Check out this Bible study app — “Find Rest for your Soul: Escaping addictive behaviour by finding a better joy”.

Available soon on Google Play and the App Store.

theme.theme-annotation: Aged iron and rust palette. Inspired by the padlock cover image: dark oxidised metal, warm rust, and muted forest green. Conveys weight, bondage, and the possibility of release. Serious and earthy rather than bright.

elements.text:
David is 43. He runs a small business, has two teenage children, and is an elder at his church. By any measure, his life is full and productive. He doesn’t seem like a man with a problem. But David has a habit. It started eight years ago: a way of decompressing after particularly brutal weeks at work. At the time it felt harmless, even reasonable. He wasn’t hurting anyone. Just unwinding.

Eight years later, the habit is no longer something David chooses. It chooses him. It shows up when he is tired, when a deal falls through, when he and his wife argue, when the elders’ meeting runs long and ends badly, when he feels – as he often does – that he is holding too many things together with not enough help. And increasingly, it shows up for no reason at all: just the pull of it, familiar and reliable, in the quiet of a weekday evening.

David has tried to stop many times. He has prayed, confessed (to God, if not to anyone else), and resolved with complete sincerity that this time will be different. Sometimes it is – for a few weeks, occasionally a few months. But he always goes back. And each time the shame is a little heavier, and the secret a little more sealed, and the gap between the David that people see on Sunday morning and the David who sits alone on Tuesday night grows a little wider.

What David wants, more than anything, is to be free. What he can’t yet see is that freedom won’t come through trying harder. It comes through something far more fundamental: a change in what his heart reaches for. This series is written to help people like David – and people whose stories look very different from David’s – find that change. Maybe you recognise something of yourself in David. Or maybe your struggle looks different – a different behaviour, a different trigger, a different history. Before we go any further, it is worth asking an honest question.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>For from within, out of the human heart, come evil ideas, sexual immorality, theft, murder, <sup>22</sup>adultery, greed, evil, deceit, debauchery, envy, slander, pride, and folly. <sup>23</sup>All these evils come from within and defile a person.

elements.question: What does Jesus identify as the source of all kinds of sins?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus identifies the human heart as the source — not external circumstances, influences, or forces. The list of sins he gives is striking in its breadth: sexual immorality, theft, greed, deceit, envy, pride, and more. All of them, he says, come from within. Evil is not something that happens to us from outside and overwhelms us; it is something that rises from within and expresses what the heart already contains.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says these evils come ’from within’ — what part of the human person does he specifically name as the origin?

elements.question: What difference does it make – if the source of sin is inside us or outside us – for how we see the problem, and find a solution?
elements.sampleAnswer: If the source is outside — in environment, upbringing, other people, stress — then the solution is also external: remove the trigger, change the circumstances, build better defences. But if the source is inside — in the heart — then those external solutions can only manage behaviour at the surface. They can’t reach the root. The implication is that lasting change requires something to happen at the level of the heart itself, not merely at the level of what we’re exposed to or what we do.
elements.questionHint: If a problem comes from inside you, what are the limits of solutions that only work from the outside?

elements.question:
• What do you picture when you hear this word?
  • Does it include you – or does it feel like a word for other people?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Most people picture addiction as something extreme and visible – alcohol dependency, hard drugs, a life in obvious crisis. This makes it easy to conclude: that’s not me.

But the Bible’s account of the problem doesn’t divide people into addicts and non-addicts. It describes a heart that reaches for the wrong things to meet real needs – and that keeps reaching even when the cost is clear. By that account, the dynamic is far more universal than the stereotype suggests.

This series uses the word broadly and deliberately. You don’t need to identify with the clinical definition to find yourself in these chapters.

elements.text: When the Bible talks about the heart, it does not simply mean our emotions. In biblical terms, the heart is the whole of the inner person – the place where we think, feel, desire, decide, and worship. It is the essential self.

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>Guard your heart with all vigilance, for from it are the sources of life.

elements.question: What is the heart’s relationship to our behaviour?
elements.sampleAnswer: The Proverb says that ’from it are the sources of life’ — the heart is the origin point from which everything else flows. Behaviour, decisions, desires, and the direction of a life all spring from what is happening in the heart. That is why it needs to be guarded ’with all vigilance’: because if the heart is disordered, the whole of life that flows from it will be disordered too.
elements.questionHint: The verse says life flows ’from’ the heart — if the heart is the source, what does that tell you about its relationship to everything that comes after it?

elements.text: If the heart is the fountain from which all behaviour flows, then our addictive behaviour is an expression of what the heart wants. External solutions – better habits, stricter accountability, environmental controls – can reduce opportunity and manage behaviour. They cannot change what we love. Only God can do that.

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>Let no one say when he is tempted, "I am being tempted by God," for God cannot be tempted by evil, and he himself tempts no one. <sup>14</sup>But each one is tempted when he is lured and enticed by his own desires.

elements.question: What does James say is the true source of temptation?
elements.sampleAnswer: James says the true source is our own desires — each person is tempted when they are ’lured and enticed’ by what is already within them. He explicitly rules out two alternative explanations: that God is the source of temptation, and (implicitly) that external forces are entirely to blame. The temptation finds its hook because something inside us responds to it. The desire that is lured is already there.
elements.questionHint: James says we are lured by our ’own desires’ — what does the word ’own’ suggest about where responsibility lies?

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>Then when desire conceives, it gives birth to sin, and when sin is full grown, it gives birth to death.

elements.question: What happens when desire is left unchecked?
elements.sampleAnswer: James uses the image of conception and birth: when desire conceives, it gives birth to sin, and when sin is fully grown, it gives birth to death. Unchecked desire follows a developmental arc — it doesn’t stay static. It grows, it acts, and the action has consequences that are ultimately fatal. The process is not instantaneous; it has stages. But each stage leads inevitably to the next if nothing intervenes.
elements.questionHint: James uses the language of birth and growth — what does that developmental image suggest about whether unchecked desire stays the same or changes over time?

elements.question: Where does responsibility lie?
elements.sampleAnswer: With us. James’s whole argument is that we cannot blame God, and the chain he describes — desire, sin, death — begins with something that belongs to us: our own desires. The birth metaphor reinforces this: these are our desires giving birth to our sins. The passage does not deny that circumstances and pressures are real, but it locates the decisive point of responsibility in the person who harbours and acts on the desire.
elements.questionHint: James traces a clear chain from desire to sin to death — at each link in that chain, whose desire, whose sin, and whose death is he describing?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • Does it feel like an ancient problem, or a modern one?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Idolatry tends to conjure images of people bowing to golden statues – a problem belonging to other religions, or other centuries. It can feel like one of the least relevant words in the Bible.

But the biblical concept is more precise and more uncomfortable than that. An idol is anything that takes God’s place at the centre of the heart – anything we trust to give us what only God can truly provide. It doesn’t need a physical form. It just needs our deepest loyalty.

Paul’s claim in Ephesians 5:5 is striking: greed – compulsive wanting – is idolatry. The idol isn’t a statue. It’s the thing you keep returning to, the thing you protect, the thing that promises to make life work.

elements.text:
There’s a more precise way to describe what happens in the heart when we’re caught in addictive behaviour: idolatry. Paul makes a striking claim: sexual sin and compulsive desire are not primarily rule-breaking. They are misplaced worship. Something has taken God’s place at the centre of the heart.

This isn’t a metaphor. It describes something that is really happening. When I return again and again to my addiction – when I find myself thinking about it, planning for it, protecting it, defending it even when I hate myself for doing so – I am relating to it the way a worshipper relates to a god.

Augustine saw this with painful clarity: ‘I was seeking you outside myself, and finding not the God of my heart.’ The longing that drives us to our addictions is not, at root, a longing for the addiction itself. It is a longing for something real – for worth, for love, for rest, for safety, for pleasure, for justice, for cleansing – that God alone can truly provide. We are not seeking too much. We are seeking the right things in the wrong places.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>For you can be confident of this one thing: that no person who is immoral, impure, or greedy (such a person is an idolater) has any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and God.

elements.question:
• Do you think willpower is the answer to habitual sin?
  • Why does it so often fail – even when the desire to change is genuine?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Willpower is the default solution most people reach for. Try harder. Be more disciplined. Make better decisions. And when it fails – as it usually does, eventually – the conclusion is: I didn’t try hard enough.

But willpower operates at the level of behaviour. The problem, as this chapter argues, is at the level of desire. You cannot, by an act of will, change what you want. You can override a desire for a time – but a suppressed desire is a pressurised desire. It will find a way out.

This isn’t an excuse for passivity. It’s a diagnosis that points to the right cure: not more effort, but a reordering of what the heart loves. That is the work of the Gospel, not the work of the will.

elements.question: What is said about someone who is sexually immoral or greedy?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul calls such a person an idolater. The identification is stark and deliberate: sexual immorality, impurity, and greed are not just moral failures — they are, at their root, a form of false worship. The greedy or sexually immoral person has placed something other than God at the centre, trusting it to deliver what only God can provide. Paul also states the consequence: such a person has no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and God.
elements.questionHint: Paul brackets greed and sexual sin with a parenthetical label — what is that label, and why do you think he chooses that particular word for those particular sins?

elements.question: What does it mean, practically, to be an ’idolater’? Can you think of what that looks like in ordinary life?
elements.sampleAnswer: An idolater, in Paul’s sense, is someone who has given their deepest trust and loyalty to something other than God — something they believe will make life work if they can just secure and protect it. In practice, it looks like the thing you can’t stop thinking about, the thing you would sacrifice other goods to keep, the thing that promises relief or satisfaction or worth. It might be money, status, a relationship, a substance, a sexual fantasy, or even personal achievement. The test isn’t whether you enjoy something, but whether it has taken the place that belongs to God.
elements.questionHint: An idol is something that takes God’s place — what practical signs might tell you that something has done that in your own life?

elements.text:
Addictive behaviours work by making real promises about real needs. The needs themselves are not the problem – they are God-given, built into us by design. The problem is where we go to meet them. And the reason we go to our addictions rather than to God is not simply that we are weak or morally deficient. It is that, at some level, we believe the addiction will deliver what God has not.

Knowledge doesn’t automatically produce change. That’s because willpower works on the level of behaviour, while the problem is on the level of desire. We cannot, by an act of will, change what we want. We can override our desires for a time – but a suppressed desire is a pressurised desire. It will find a way out.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus. <sup>2</sup>For the law of the life-giving Spirit in Christ Jesus has set you free from the law of sin and death.

elements.question: What has happened to those who are ’in Christ’?
elements.sampleAnswer: Two things have happened: they are no longer under condemnation, and they have been set free from the law of sin and death by the law of the life-giving Spirit. Both are stated as present realities — ‘there is therefore now.’ The freedom isn’t a future hope; it belongs to those who are in Christ Jesus right now. The agent of that freedom is the Spirit, not personal resolve or moral effort.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’there is therefore now no condemnation’ — what does the word ’now’ tell you about when this reality takes effect for those who are in Christ?

elements.question: What is the difference between trying to free yourself from sin through effort, and being freed by ’the law of the Spirit’?
elements.sampleAnswer: Freedom through effort is self-generated and operates at the level of will and behaviour — it involves overriding or suppressing desires by force of determination. Paul’s ’law of the Spirit’ describes a freedom that comes from outside us, given by God’s Spirit working within us. One is a matter of trying harder; the other is a matter of being liberated. One leaves us as the agent of our own change, dependent on our own strength; the other makes God the agent, with his strength at work in us.
elements.questionHint: Paul contrasts two ’laws’ — one that enslaves and one that liberates. What is the source of each one, and why does that difference matter for where the power to change comes from?

elements.text: The will follows the heart. The heart follows what it worships. Change requires a reordering of worship – a shift in what the heart trusts, treasures, and finds most beautiful. That is not something we can manufacture through effort. It is the work of the Holy Spirit, operating through the Gospel.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>because everyone who has been fathered by God conquers the world. This is the conquering power that has conquered the world: our faith. <sup>5</sup>Now who is the one who conquers the world except the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God?

elements.question: What is the mechanism of victory over patterns of this world?
elements.sampleAnswer: The mechanism is faith — specifically, faith in Jesus as the Son of God. John is emphatic: ‘this is the conquering power that has conquered the world: our faith.’ The victory is not attributed to discipline, determination, or moral effort. It is attributed to faith — to the act of trusting in who Jesus is. The person who overcomes is defined not by their strength of will but by what they believe.
elements.questionHint: John identifies one specific thing as ’the conquering power’ — what is it, and how does that answer the question of what mechanism makes victory possible?

elements.question: What does it mean, in practice, to ’overcome’ through faith rather than through willpower?
elements.sampleAnswer: Overcoming through faith means the battle is fought at the level of what we believe is most true and most real, rather than at the level of clenching harder against a desire. In practice, it looks like reminding ourselves — repeatedly, in the moment of temptation — of who Jesus is and what he has given us, and trusting that he is genuinely better than what we are being drawn toward. Willpower says ’I must not.’ Faith says ’I have something far greater.’ One suppresses; the other displaces.
elements.questionHint: John says the one who overcomes is ’the one who believes’ — what would it look like, in a moment of temptation, to respond with belief rather than with gritted effort?

elements.text: There is hope here. Not the fragile hope of ’maybe this time I’ll manage to hold it together.’ Not the grim hope of white-knuckled endurance. But the solid, Gospel-rooted hope of people who have discovered that God is, in fact, better – and who have found in that discovery a freedom they could not manufacture for themselves.

elements.passageText: <sup>31</sup>Then Jesus said to those Judeans who had believed him, "If you continue to follow my teaching, you are really my disciples <sup>32</sup>and you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free."

elements.question: What does Jesus promise to those who hold to his teaching?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus promises that they will know the truth, and that the truth will set them free. The promise is conditional on continuing in his teaching — it is to those who are genuinely his disciples, not merely initial believers. The freedom promised is something the truth itself produces: it is not freedom achieved by effort, but freedom that comes from knowing something that is real.
elements.questionHint: Jesus connects two things — knowing the truth and being set free. What is the relationship between them, and which one causes the other?

elements.question: What kind of freedom do you think Jesus means here? Is it different from the freedom that willpower tries to achieve?
elements.sampleAnswer: The freedom Jesus means is a freedom produced by truth — by a new understanding of what is real — rather than by suppression. Willpower tries to achieve freedom by overriding desires, holding them down through force. That produces a kind of constrained behaviour, but the desire itself is still there, under pressure. The freedom Jesus describes seems to come from a reorientation at a deeper level: when we truly know the truth about God, ourselves, and what is worth living for, the pull of the old desires loses its power not because we’re holding it down but because something better has displaced it.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says the truth ’will set you free’ — what would be different about a freedom that comes from knowing the truth, compared with a freedom that comes from trying harder?

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>You know that from your empty way of life inherited from your ancestors you were ransomed — not by perishable things like silver or gold, <sup>19</sup>but by precious blood like that of an unblemished and spotless lamb, namely Christ.

elements.question: What have we been redeemed from?
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter says we have been ransomed from an empty way of life — a way of living that was inherited, habitual, and futile. The ransom price was not silver or gold but the precious blood of Christ. The implication is that the old way of life was not just mistaken but captivating — it held us, and a price had to be paid to free us from it. What we were redeemed from was not merely individual wrong acts but a whole inherited orientation of life.
elements.questionHint: Peter describes what we were ransomed from as a ’way of life’ — what does that phrase suggest about the scope of what we needed freeing from?

elements.question: The word ’empty’ is also translated ’futile’ or ’meaningless.’ What does that capture about the cycle of addiction?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘Empty’ or ’futile’ captures something essential: the addictive cycle never delivers what it promises. It produces the sensation of relief or pleasure or connection, but those sensations pass and leave nothing of substance behind. The person returns to the same behaviour, looking for the same thing, and finds the same temporary relief followed by the same emptiness. It is a cycle that goes nowhere — full of activity and yet producing nothing lasting. The word also carries the sense of something inherited and unreflective, a way of living passed on without question.
elements.questionHint: If a way of life is ’empty’ or ’futile,‘ what does that say about whether it succeeds or fails at what it promises to deliver?

elements.text: David’s story – or whatever version of David’s story is yours – does not have to end where it is now. The series ahead is an invitation into a better story. Not an easy story, and not a quick one. But a true one.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Before this study, how would you have explained the root cause of addictive behaviour (in general, or in your own life)? Has anything in this study shifted that explanation?

‘All the explanations we normally reach for (triggers, biology, history, the devil) contain some truth but are incomplete.’ Do you find that convincing? Is there one explanation that you find yourself wanting to hold onto, and why?
What do you make of the idea that addictive behaviour is a form of misdirected worship – that we are seeking real things in the wrong places? Does that reframe anything for you?
‘Willpower fails because it works on the level of behaviour while the problem is at the level of desire.’ Have you seen this in your own life? What did that feel like?
‘God offers more.’ Do you believe that? What makes it hard to believe – especially in the moment of temptation?

elements.text:
Sarah is a family doctor. She is also a mother of three, a church small group leader, and the kind of person other people describe as ’having it all together.’ She is the first to arrive and the last to leave. She never misses a deadline, never drops a ball, never lets anyone down – not professionally, not domestically, not at church.

What nobody sees is that Sarah has not stopped for fifteen years. Never really stopped. Her busyness is not incidental – it is structural. It is the architecture of her life, and she built it carefully, because she discovered early on that when she keeps moving, the voice stays quiet. The voice that says: ‘Is this enough? Are you enough? What would people think if they could really see you?’

When Sarah does stop – when she’s forced to – the anxiety is immediate and physical. So she fills the gap. Another project, another commitment, another reason to be indispensable. Her addiction does not look like an addiction. It looks like virtue. But it is doing the same work as any other: it keeps a deep and scary question at bay.

Most of us are familiar with the desire to be respected – to be thought well of, to know that we matter, to feel that our efforts and presence are valued. This hunger for the approval of others is one of the most powerful and most universal of human drives. And it is not, in itself, wrong. But for many people caught in patterns of compulsive behaviour, this hunger has become something darker: a performance, a project, a relentless attempt to prove something that never quite gets proved.

elements.passageText: <sup>25</sup>The fear of people becomes a snare, but whoever trusts in the LORD will be set on high.

elements.question: What are the effects of living under fear of what others think?
elements.sampleAnswer: The Proverb says it becomes a snare — a trap. Fear of people ensnares because it places your sense of worth and security in the hands of others’ opinions, which can never be permanently secured. You are always performing for a verdict that is never finally given. The contrast the Proverb draws is instructive: the alternative to being snared by human opinion is trusting in the LORD, which leads to being ’set on high’ — a stable, secure position that doesn’t depend on others’ assessment.
elements.questionHint: The Proverb uses the word ’snare’ — what does a snare do to an animal, and how does that image describe what the fear of people does to a person?

elements.question: Can you think of ways – not necessarily dramatic – in which the fear of other people’s opinion shapes your behaviour?
elements.sampleAnswer: It shows up in small, everyday ways: not speaking honestly in a meeting because of what colleagues might think; overstating abilities or achievements; avoiding situations where failure is possible; performing competence or spirituality in public while hiding struggle in private; agreeing with people rather than risking their disapproval; filling every moment with productivity to forestall any sense of being found inadequate. The fear doesn’t have to be dramatic to be controlling.
elements.questionHint: Think about a recent decision you made or avoided making — was concern about what someone would think a factor, even a small one?

elements.text:
The fear of man takes many forms. Sometimes it drives public behaviour – the need to be seen as successful, capable, spiritual, indispensable. Sometimes it drives private behaviour instead – the creation of a secret world in which, whatever failures exist in real life, success is guaranteed. What all of these have in common is that they are, at some level, performances for an imagined audience. They create a theatre in which the verdict is always favourable.

There’s an irony here. The people most visibly caught in the Respect dynamic are those who look least like they have a problem: the high-achieving professional, the effective church leader, the person whose busyness and competence are genuinely admired. Their addiction is invisible precisely because it dresses up as virtue. But drivenness of this kind – where the inability to rest, the terror of being found inadequate, and the compulsive filling of every gap are the engine beneath the achievement – is doing the same work as any other compulsion: it is a flight from an unbearable question.

The point is not that effectiveness and fear-driven compulsion are the same thing, or that every high achiever is hiding a problem. Many people work hard from healthy motivations. The question worth asking is not what you achieve, but what is driving you – and whether you could stop if you chose to.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>For ignoring the righteousness that comes from God, and seeking instead to establish their own righteousness, they did not submit to God’s righteousness.

elements.question: What is true of those who try to make their own righteousness?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says they ignore the righteousness that comes from God and fail to submit to it. By pursuing their own self-generated righteousness, they are simultaneously rejecting the one that God offers. The project of making yourself right — of proving your own adequacy through performance and achievement — is therefore not a neutral activity; it is an active bypassing of the only righteousness that actually works. It is self-sufficiency in the most spiritually costly sense.
elements.questionHint: Paul says those who seek their own righteousness ’did not submit to God’s righteousness’ — what does the word ’submit’ imply about the relationship between self-made and God-given righteousness?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
’Grace’ sounds like God being lenient – turning a blind eye, or grading on a curve. But grace isn’t God lowering his standards. It’s God acting in our favour when we had no right to expect it and nothing to offer in return.

It’s not a reward for the almost-good-enough. It’s a rescue for the lost. The chapter will describe ’counterfeit grace’ – the false acceptance that addiction offers. The contrast only lands if we understand what real grace actually is: entirely unearned, and entirely sufficient.

elements.question: What is the difference between seeking approval through performance, and receiving a righteousness from God?
elements.sampleAnswer: Performance-based approval is earned, provisional, and always at risk of being withdrawn if the performance falters. It places the burden of proof entirely on the person performing, and the verdict is never secure — there is always more to prove. Righteousness from God, by contrast, is given rather than earned: it comes through Christ’s faithfulness, not our own. It is not provisional — it is not subject to revision on the basis of future failures. The fundamental difference is that one depends on us, and the other depends on God.
elements.questionHint: Think about what the word ’establishing’ implies — what does it mean to try to establish your own righteousness, and how does that differ from receiving something that has already been established for you?

elements.text:
Where the Respect dynamic drives people toward performance and achievement, the Rescue dynamic is experienced by those who have, in some sense, given up on the project of proving themselves. The self-loathing is too deep, the sense of failure too pervasive. What they are looking for is not admiration – it is simply acceptance. Not a verdict of ’excellent,‘ but a verdict of ’OK.’

And the addiction offers exactly that. It does not judge. It is always available, always welcoming. For the person drowning in shame, it feels, in the moment, like grace. What it is, of course, is a counterfeit grace. And like all counterfeits, it is recognisable by what it fails to do: it offers the feeling of acceptance without any of its substance. It receives us as we are and leaves us exactly as we are.

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>For while we were still helpless, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly. <sup>7</sup>(For rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though for a good person perhaps someone might possibly dare to die.) <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear these words?
  • What do you think they really mean?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
’Justified’ and ’righteous’ are sometimes read as meaning ’good enough’ or ’morally improved’ – or even as meaning ’self-righteous.’ But in Scripture, to be justified means to be declared right with God – not because you’ve become righteous, but because Christ’s righteousness has been credited to you.

It’s a legal verdict, not a character assessment. God doesn’t declare you righteous because you are. He makes you righteous by declaring it – on the basis of what Christ has done, not what you have done.

This matters enormously for the Respect and Rescue dynamics. Justification means the project of self-justification is simply over. There is nothing left to prove.

elements.question: What does Paul say we were like when Christ died for us?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says we were helpless and ungodly (verse 6), and sinners (verse 8). These are not complimentary terms — they describe people with no claim on God’s favour, no strength of their own to improve their situation, and no record of righteousness to commend them. The three words together present the full picture of our condition: morally failing, spiritually unable, and deserving of nothing.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses three different words to describe our condition — ‘helpless,‘ ‘ungodly,‘ and ’sinners.’ What does each word add to the picture of what we were like when Christ acted?

elements.question: What does it tell us about God’s love that he acted ’at just the right time’, before we had changed, or proved ourselves?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that God’s love is unconditional and entirely unprompted by anything in us. He did not wait for us to become better, to prove ourselves worthy, or to show sufficient remorse. He acted while we were still in our worst condition. This means his love is not a response to our goodness; it is the cause of our salvation. For anyone caught in the cycle of feeling they need to get themselves sorted before God would want them, this is important: the timing of God’s action was precisely when we were least deserving.
elements.questionHint: Paul emphasises that Christ died ’while we were still’ in that condition — what does the word ’still’ tell you about whether God was waiting for something to change in us first?

elements.text:
The Gospel’s answer to both the Respect dynamic and the Rescue dynamic begins with one of its most fundamental truths: justification. God has pronounced a verdict over everyone who trusts in Christ: righteous. Not ’acceptable given the circumstances.’ Not ’forgiven, but on probation.’ Righteous – the same verdict he pronounces over his own Son.

This verdict is not earned. It is not provisional. It will not be revised in the light of future failures. It means that the project of self-justification, the endless performance for an imagined audience, is simply over. There is nothing left to prove.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.

elements.question: What is now true of those who are in Christ Jesus?
elements.sampleAnswer: There is no condemnation for them — not reduced condemnation, not condemnation held in suspension pending future behaviour, but none at all. The verdict is absolute and present tense: ‘there is therefore now.’ For anyone whose compulsive behaviour is driven by shame and a sense that they are fundamentally not enough, this is the most direct possible Gospel answer: the ultimate court has already passed its verdict, and it is entirely favourable.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’no condemnation’ — not ’less condemnation’ or ’conditional on future improvement.’ What does the absolute nature of that statement mean for how secure the position is?

elements.question: What would it mean, practically, to live without condemnation – not just in theory, but as a felt reality?
elements.sampleAnswer: It would mean that the voice that drives compulsive behaviour — ‘you are not enough, you need to prove yourself, you need this thing to feel OK’ — has lost its authority, because the verdict it claims to be delivering has already been settled. Practically, it might look like being able to rest without anxiety; being honest about failure without the fear of total exposure; making decisions from a settled sense of worth rather than from the need to earn or protect it. The gap between David on Sunday morning and David on Tuesday night would begin to close — not because the Tuesday behaviour has been suppressed, but because the shame driving it has been addressed at its root.
elements.questionHint: If condemnation is the voice that says ’you are guilty, you are not enough’ — what would the daily experience of no condemnation look and feel like in ordinary life?

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>But now apart from the law the righteousness of God (which is attested by the law and the prophets) has been disclosed — <sup>22</sup>namely, the righteousness of God through the faithfulness of Jesus Christ for all who believe. For there is no distinction, <sup>23</sup>for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. <sup>24</sup>But they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.

elements.question: What is the basis on which God declares us righteous?
elements.sampleAnswer: The basis is the faithfulness of Jesus Christ, received through faith. Paul says it is ’apart from the law’ — not through obedience to God’s commands — and it comes ’freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.’ Three things are clear: it is free (not earned), it comes from God’s grace (not our merit), and it is grounded in what Christ has done (the redemption), not in what we have done.
elements.questionHint: Paul says we are justified ’freely by his grace’ — what do those two words together (‘freely’ and ’grace’) rule out as a possible basis for the declaration?

elements.question: Why is it significant that this righteousness comes ’apart from the law’ – that is, apart from our performance?
elements.sampleAnswer: It means our standing before God is not a function of how well we perform. If it were, then the person who performs well would have more standing than the person who doesn’t, and the project of self-justification would have a point. But Paul says there is no distinction — all have sinned and fall short, and all are justified by the same means: God’s grace in Christ, entirely apart from performance. This removes the ladder of achievement entirely. No one can climb their way to a better position, but equally, no one is excluded because their performance is too poor.
elements.questionHint: If righteousness came through the law — that is, through performance — what would that mean for the person whose performance is poor? And what would it mean for the person whose performance is good?

elements.text: But justification, profound as it is, is not the whole of the Gospel’s answer. For the person whose deepest hunger is not for a favourable verdict but for unconditional love – for the person who needs to know not just that they are acquitted but that they are wanted – God offers something even more intimate.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>More than that, I now regard all things as liabilities compared to the far greater value of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord, for whom I have suffered the loss of all things — indeed, I regard them as dung! — that I may gain Christ, <sup>9</sup>and be found in him, not because I have my own righteousness derived from the law, but because I have the righteousness that comes by way of Christ’s faithfulness — a righteousness from God that is in fact based on Christ’s faithfulness.

elements.question: What is Paul’s new basis for confidence after meeting Christ?
elements.sampleAnswer: His new basis is not his own righteousness — his credentials, his zeal, his accomplishments under the law — but the righteousness that comes through Christ’s faithfulness, which is a righteousness from God. He wants to ’be found in him,‘ meaning his identity and standing are now located in Christ rather than in his own track record. His confidence rests entirely on what Christ has done, not on what he has achieved.
elements.questionHint: Paul contrasts ’my own righteousness’ with ’the righteousness that comes by way of Christ’s faithfulness’ — in each case, who is the source, and why does that matter for how secure the confidence is?

elements.question: What has Paul given up, and what has he gained?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul has given up everything he previously counted as gain — his religious credentials, his status as a Pharisee, his blameless observance of the law, his reputation. He counts all of it as loss, and goes further: he regards it as ’dung,‘ a word chosen for its sharpness. What he has gained is Christ himself — and with Christ, a righteousness that is not his own but God’s, which far exceeds anything his former record could have produced.
elements.questionHint: Paul says he ’suffered the loss of all things’ to gain Christ — what does the strength of that language (‘suffered,‘ ‘loss,‘ ‘all things’) suggest about how significant his former credentials were to him, and therefore how significant the exchange was?

elements.question: What things can you see in your own life that work the way Paul’s ’own righteousness’ did for him?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s ’own righteousness’ was the accumulation of things he could point to that made him feel he was enough — his heritage, his achievements, his religious performance. In our own lives, the equivalent might be: professional success or reputation; being seen as a good parent, spouse, or friend; length of time as a Christian; theological knowledge; ministry involvement; moral record. These are not wrong in themselves. The question is whether we are using them the way Paul used his credentials — as a foundation for confidence and worth — rather than holding them loosely as gifts.
elements.questionHint: Paul’s ’own righteousness’ was the list of things he could point to that made him feel secure and sufficient — what would be on your equivalent list, the things you quietly rely on to feel that you are OK?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>But to all who have received him — those who believe in his name — he has given the right to become God’s children.

elements.question: What name does John say we are given the right to be called?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s children. John says that to all who received Jesus and believed in his name, he gave the right to become God’s children. The word ’right’ is significant — this is not a tentative possibility or a metaphor for approval; it is a legal status conferred. To be a child of God is to have a specific, defined, permanent relationship to the Father.
elements.questionHint: John uses the word ’right’ — not ’opportunity’ or ’chance.’ What does being given a ’right’ to something suggest about how secure and settled that status is?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>See what sort of love the Father has given to us: that we should be called God’s children — and indeed we are! For this reason the world does not know us: because it did not know him.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this phrase?
  • Does it sound like a good thing or a threatening one?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The fear of the Lord tends to sound like cowering before an unpredictable deity – the kind of fear that paralyses rather than frees. It can seem like an Old Testament concept that the New Testament softens or replaces.

But the Bible presents the fear of the Lord as a deeply positive thing: a right orientation toward a God who is truly great. It is reverence, awe, and trust – the response of a creature who has grasped how glorious God actually is.

Crucially, it is also liberating. Proverbs says the fear of the Lord leads to life, and brings rest. This chapter’s argument is that the antidote to the exhausting fear of other people’s opinions is not the absence of all fear – it is a bigger, truer fear that puts everything else in its proper place.

elements.question: What does the Father’s love look like – what word describes it?
elements.sampleAnswer: John calls it an extraordinary kind of love: ‘See what sort of love the Father has given to us.’ The exclamation invites wonder — as if the love is so remarkable it requires a double take. It results in something that should be astonishing: that we should be called God’s children. John adds ’and indeed we are!’ — not just in name or aspiration, but in reality. The love is of such a quality that it produces an actual, present identity as children of God.
elements.questionHint: John doesn’t just say the Father loves us — he says ’see what sort of love.’ What does inviting us to look at the quality or kind of love suggest about what makes it unusual?

elements.question: Does the intensity of that language surprise you? What does it tell you about how God feels about his children?
elements.sampleAnswer: The intensity should surprise us, because it is far more than the minimum required. God could have said ’you are forgiven’ or ’you are acceptable.’ Instead John describes a love so remarkable that it needs to be pointed at. The language suggests not a managed approval but a delighted affection — God does not merely tolerate his children, he names them as his own with a love that John finds almost too much to take in. For anyone whose compulsive behaviour is driven by the fear of not being wanted or accepted, this is the direct counter-word.
elements.questionHint: John seems almost overwhelmed — he says ’see what sort of love.’ What does that emotional register suggest about the difference between merely being tolerated and being genuinely loved?

elements.text: The parable Jesus tells in Luke 15 captures this with extraordinary precision. The father in the story does not wait for his son to make his case. He does not receive him cautiously, on probation. He sees him while he is still a long way off – and he runs.

elements.passageText: <sup>20</sup>So he got up and went to his father. But while he was still a long way from home his father saw him, and his heart went out to him; he ran and hugged his son and kissed him. <sup>21</sup>Then his son said to him, "Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you; I am no longer worthy to be called your son." <sup>22</sup>But the father said to his slaves, "Hurry! Bring the best robe, and put it on him! Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet! <sup>23</sup>Bring the fattened calf and kill it! Let us eat and celebrate, <sup>24</sup>because this son of mine was dead, and is alive again — he was lost and is found!" So they began to celebrate.

elements.question: What does the father do when he sees his son returning?
elements.sampleAnswer: He sees the son while he is still ’a long way from home’ — he was watching for him. His heart goes out to him. He runs — the detail that would have struck a first-century audience most forcefully, since dignified elderly men did not run. He hugs and kisses him, before the son has said a word. The entire sequence happens before any explanation, apology, or promise of reform is offered. The welcome is initiated by the father, not earned by the son.
elements.questionHint: The father takes several actions before the son even speaks — can you list them in order, and notice what each one says about who is driving the reunion?

elements.question: What do the robe, the ring, and the feast tell us about the nature of the father’s welcome?
elements.sampleAnswer: Each item communicates full restoration of status. The best robe covers the son’s rags and publicly marks him as honoured. The ring is a sign of authority and belonging — he is not a servant, he is family. The sandals distinguish him from a slave. And the feast is not a quiet, private affair but a public celebration: ‘let us eat and celebrate.’ The father is not quietly forgiving his son in private while keeping up appearances. He is throwing a party. The welcome is extravagant, public, and complete — not grudging, not minimal, not provisional.
elements.questionHint: Each gift the father gives — the robe, the ring, the sandals — was a culturally specific signal. What kind of person in that world would have received such things, and what does that tell you about the son’s new status?

elements.question: Are these the actions of someone who is tolerating the son’s return, or something more?
elements.sampleAnswer: Clearly something more — and dramatically more. Someone tolerating a return does not run to meet the person while they are still far off. They do not hug and kiss before hearing any explanation. They do not throw a party. Every detail of the father’s response communicates not reluctant acceptance but joyful, wholehearted welcome. His words to the servants — ‘this son of mine was dead, and is alive again; he was lost and is found’ — express delight, not relief that a difficult situation has been managed.
elements.questionHint: Think about what the opposite of tolerance would look like in someone receiving back a person who had wronged them deeply — does the father’s behaviour look more like tolerance or its opposite?

elements.text: The biblical antidote to the fear of man is not the absence of all fear. It is a different, greater, more liberating fear – the fear of the Lord.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>The LORD shows his faithful followers the way they should live. <sup>13</sup>They experience his favor; their descendants inherit the land. <sup>14</sup>The LORD’s loyal followers receive his guidance, and he reveals his covenantal demands to them.

elements.question: What is given to the person who fears the Lord?
elements.sampleAnswer: According to this Psalm, those who fear the Lord are shown the way to live, they experience his favour, their descendants inherit the land, and they receive his guidance — he ’reveals his covenantal demands to them,‘ meaning he lets them into his confidence, sharing what he asks of those in a relationship with him. The picture is of someone who is guided, blessed, and included: not at arm’s length but in close relationship with God.
elements.questionHint: The Psalm lists several things given to the person who fears the Lord — what kind of relationship does that list describe, close and guiding or distant and demanding?

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>Fearing the LORD leads to life, and one who does so will live satisfied; he will not be afflicted by calamity.

elements.question: What does the fear of the Lord replace?
elements.sampleAnswer: It replaces the restless, insatiable hunger that drives so much compulsive behaviour. The Proverb says that the one who fears the Lord ’will live satisfied’ — and satisfaction is precisely what the fear of man and the patterns that accompany it never produce. The fear of people, the need for approval, the compulsive behaviours that medicate shame — all of these are forms of dissatisfaction, always needing more. The fear of the Lord leads to life and satisfaction: not the temporary relief of the addiction, but genuine, lasting contentment.
elements.questionHint: The Proverb says the person who fears the Lord ’will live satisfied’ — what is the opposite of satisfied, and which of the patterns described in this chapter does that opposite describe?

elements.question: What does it mean for the fear of the Lord to bring ’rest’? How is that different from the restlessness that the fear of man produces?
elements.sampleAnswer: The fear of man produces restlessness because the verdict it seeks can never be permanently secured — there is always another audience to perform for, another standard to meet, another way to fall short. Sarah in the story can never stop because the voice never quiets. The rest that comes from fearing the Lord is different in kind: it comes from a verdict that has already been given by the only judge whose opinion ultimately matters. When God is the audience and his verdict is settled and favourable, the exhausting performance for every other audience loses its compulsive urgency. Rest becomes possible.
elements.questionHint: Sarah can’t stop because the voice never goes quiet — what would need to change for the voice to lose its power, and how does fearing the Lord rather than people address that?

elements.text: When we begin to grasp how glorious God is – and that this God regards us, in Christ, with delight – the opinion of others begins to lose its tyrannical power. Not because other people stop mattering, but because their verdict is no longer the one that counts. The one verdict that truly matters has already been given, in Christ, and it is entirely favourable. We are freed from the exhausting project of self-justification, not because we have finally proved ourselves adequate, but because someone adequate has stood in our place.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Which dynamic do you find yourself identifying with more – Respect (the performing heart) or Rescue (the hunger for unconditional acceptance)? Or is it both?
Sarah’s addiction is work – and it looks like virtue from the outside. What other compulsive behaviours seem respectable?
What would it mean practically to live knowing the verdict of justification is true – so you have nothing left to prove? What in your life would change?
The father in Luke 15 runs toward his son while he is ’still a long way off.’ What does that say to someone who feels they need to get themselves sorted before they can come to God?

The chapter describes counterfeit grace – the acceptance the addiction offers – as something that ’receives us as we are and leaves us exactly as we are.’ How is that different from what God’s grace does?

elements.text:
Marcus is 31. He is thoughtful, well-liked, and – by his own account – terrible at relationships. Not in an obvious way. He is warm, funny, interested in people. He has many acquaintances and a few people he calls friends. But genuine intimacy – where someone knows the full truth of you and stays anyway – has always felt just out of reach.

He is not sure exactly when porn became a habit. It was gradual. What he is sure of is what it gave him, in the early days at least: a feeling of being wanted. Of being, in some imagined way, chosen. It sounds absurd when he says it out loud. But the feeling was real.

What Marcus could not see – what took him years to begin to understand – is that the thing he was using to medicate his loneliness was making the loneliness worse. Every hour spent in that private world was an hour not spent building the real relationships he actually needed. The false intimacy was crowding out the real thing, and the gap between his outer sociability and his inner isolation was growing wider every year.

The longing to be known and wanted – to matter deeply to at least one other person – is one of the most fundamental of human desires. And it is entirely right that it should be. It is not a weakness or a neediness to be overcome. It is a reflection of how we are made.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>The LORD God said, "It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make a companion for him who corresponds to him."

elements.question: What does God say about the man he created, before the Fall?
elements.sampleAnswer: God says ’It is not good for the man to be alone.’ This is striking because it is said in an unfallen world — before sin, before the curse, when everything else God had made was declared good. Aloneness is the one thing God identifies as not good in the original creation. He then acts to address it by making a companion who ’corresponds to’ the man — someone fitting and complementary, not just any company.
elements.questionHint: This statement is made before sin enters the world — what does that tell you about whether the need for companionship is a consequence of the Fall or something built into human nature from the beginning?

elements.question: What does it tell us that God himself declares aloneness ’not good’ – the one thing in creation that is a problem before sin?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that the desire for relationship is not a weakness, a flaw, or a consequence of the Fall — it is a God-designed feature of human nature. God himself identifies its absence as a problem and moves to solve it. This means that when someone feels the deep hunger for genuine connection, they are not experiencing something shameful or spiritually immature. They are experiencing something built into them by their Creator. The question is where they go to meet that hunger — and whether the substitutes they use actually satisfy it.
elements.questionHint: Every other thing in the creation account is declared ’good’ — what is the significance of the one exception being not a sin but a form of absence or isolation?

elements.text:
If the longing for relationship is God-given and good, why does it so often drive us toward things that harm us? Part of the answer is that genuine intimacy is costly. To be truly known requires vulnerability – and vulnerability means risk. The risk of exposure, of judgment, of rejection. The risk that if someone saw the full truth of us, they might not stay.

One of the things that addictive behaviour offers – across a wide range of different behaviours – is a simulation of intimacy without the risk. The warmth of alcohol in a social setting lowers inhibition and creates a feeling of connection without requiring the hard work of genuine relationship. Pornography offers the feeling of being desired and welcomed, at zero relational risk.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>There is no fear in love, but perfect love drives out fear, because fear has to do with punishment. The one who fears punishment has not been perfected in love.

elements.question: What does John say casts out fear?
elements.sampleAnswer: Perfect love casts out fear. John says there is no fear in love — they are incompatible. When love is ’perfect’ (complete, mature, fully received), it drives the fear out. The fear John has in mind is specifically the fear connected with punishment or judgment — the fear that we will be found wanting and condemned. When we are fully persuaded that we are loved and not under threat, that fear has no ground to stand on.
elements.questionHint: John says perfect love ’drives out’ fear — that’s an active image, not a passive one. What does it suggest about the relationship between receiving love and the diminishment of fear?

elements.question: What kind of fear do you think John has in mind here? And how might this verse apply to the fear of intimacy – the fear of being truly known and possibly rejected?
elements.sampleAnswer: John specifies that the fear has to do with punishment — the fear of judgment, of being found unacceptable and condemned. Applied to the fear of intimacy, the connection is close: the fear of being truly known is ultimately the fear that if someone saw all of us — our failures, our ugliness, our hidden life — they would find us unacceptable and leave. That is a form of the same fear: exposure leading to judgment leading to rejection. John’s answer is that perfect love — love that has no condemnation in it, love that has already seen the worst and stayed — is what casts that fear out. The person who genuinely knows themselves to be loved without condition does not need to hide.
elements.questionHint: John says fear has to do with ’punishment’ — how is the fear of being truly known and rejected by another person a version of that same fear?

elements.text:
There is a specific version of the relationship dynamic that is worth naming directly. For many people, the appeal of pornography – and of some other addictive behaviours – is not simply that it offers intimacy, but that it offers intimacy on their own terms. It is relational experience without vulnerability, without negotiation, without the possibility of rejection or disappointment. What this amounts to is a desire for sovereignty over our relational world.

One person described the dynamic with unusual honesty: ‘For me, porn is all about the illusion of controlling others. I felt so out of control in my life that this was something I could control. There is no vulnerability, no risk, and therefore, no growth.’ The last phrase is telling: no growth. The controlled, risk-free substitute for intimacy actively prevents the development of the capacity for real relationship. We practise cowardice and wonder why we never become brave.

elements.passageText: <sup>25</sup>They exchanged the truth of God for a lie and worshiped and served the creation rather than the Creator, who is blessed forever! Amen.

elements.question: What happens to someone who exchanges the truth of God for a lie, and worships created things?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says they worship and serve the creation rather than the Creator. In the broader context of Romans 1, he goes on to describe how God ’gives them over’ to the consequences of this exchange — to disordered desires and a disordered life. The lie they have believed is that created things can give them what only God can. And built into created things is the incapacity to fulfil that role: they will always eventually disappoint or fail, because they were never designed to bear the weight of ultimate meaning, worth, or satisfaction.
elements.questionHint: Paul says they ’exchanged’ truth for a lie — what does the language of exchange suggest about what they expected to gain, and what they actually ended up with?

elements.question: How does the desire for control in relationships reflect a deeper refusal to trust God with the parts of our lives that feel most uncertain?
elements.sampleAnswer: Worshipping created things rather than the Creator is, at its core, a bid for self-sufficiency — the rejection of dependence on God in favour of something we can manage or control. The desire for control in relationships follows the same logic: real relationship requires trusting God with the outcome, including the possibility of rejection or disappointment. The controlled substitute offers the relational experience without that dependence. It is, in miniature, the same exchange Paul describes: the truth of a God-governed relational world traded for the lie of a self-governed one.
elements.questionHint: Paul says they ’served’ the creation — what does serving something involve, and how does the desire to control your relational world actually end up serving the addiction rather than freeing you?

elements.text: There is a further cruelty in the addiction-as-relationship dynamic: it makes the original problem worse. The person who turns to pornography because they are lonely does not become less lonely. They become more so – because the time and emotional energy spent in the false world is time and energy not invested in real relationships. And the shame that follows each episode creates another layer of hiddenness, another reason not to let anyone too close. The very thing used to medicate the isolation deepens it.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>Therefore encourage one another and build up each other, just as you are in fact doing.

elements.question: What is a better alternative to this ’vicious circle’?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s call to ’encourage one another and build up each other’ describes the opposite pattern to the vicious circle of isolation and shame. Where the addiction cycle involves withdrawal, hiddenness, and deepening isolation, genuine Christian community involves mutual encouragement — the practice of speaking into each other’s lives in ways that strengthen rather than deplete. The alternative to the false intimacy of addiction is not simply the removal of the behaviour, but investment in the real relationships that the behaviour was substituting for.
elements.questionHint: The vicious circle involves turning away from real relationships and deeper into isolation — what does ’building up each other’ require that the addictive pattern avoids?

elements.question: What does it look like practically to ’build each other up’ for someone trapped in the cycle of shame and isolation?
elements.sampleAnswer: For someone in the shame and isolation cycle, being ’built up’ might begin simply with being known and not rejected — someone being told their struggle with honesty and receiving acceptance rather than judgment. From there it might include someone actively checking in, praying with them, reminding them of Gospel truths about their worth and standing before God, and being present through failure without withdrawing. Building up is the opposite of the pattern the addiction creates: instead of hiding, being seen; instead of performing, being known; instead of isolation, belonging.
elements.questionHint: Think about what shame and isolation specifically require in order to be broken — what would someone in that cycle need to receive from another person to begin to come out of it?

elements.text: The Gospel’s answer to the relationship dynamic is not, primarily, a set of instructions about how to build better human relationships – though those matter. It is, first, an announcement about a relationship that already exists, and that nothing can destroy.

elements.passageText: <sup>35</sup>Who will separate us from the love of Christ? Will trouble, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword? <sup>36</sup>As it is written, "For your sake we encounter death all day long; we were considered as sheep to be slaughtered." <sup>37</sup>No, in all these things we have complete victory through the one who loved us! <sup>38</sup>For I am convinced that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor heavenly rulers, nor things that are present, nor things to come, nor powers, <sup>39</sup>nor height, nor depth, nor anything else in creation will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.

elements.question: What can separate us from the love of Christ?
elements.sampleAnswer: Nothing. Paul’s answer, after running through the most comprehensive possible list of threats — trouble, distress, persecution, famine, danger, death, life, angels, present and future, height and depth, anything else in creation — is that none of it can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus. The structure of the passage is itself rhetorical: it asks who could separate us, lists every conceivable candidate, and then answers: not one of them.
elements.questionHint: Paul asks ’who will separate us?’ and then lists many possible answers before giving his conclusion — what is his conclusion, and how does the exhaustiveness of the list make the conclusion stronger?

elements.question: Re-read Paul’s list of threats. How comprehensive is the list? What does it say about the security of this relationship?
elements.sampleAnswer: The list is extraordinarily comprehensive. Paul covers external suffering (trouble, distress, persecution, famine, danger, sword), the poles of existence (death and life), spiritual powers (angels, heavenly rulers), time itself (things present and things to come), and spatial dimensions (height and depth) — and then adds ’nor anything else in creation’ to catch whatever he might have missed. It is a deliberately exhaustive catalogue designed to establish one thing: that the security of this relationship is total. If not one item on that list can break it, then it cannot be broken.
elements.questionHint: Paul ends with ’nor anything else in creation’ — why do you think he adds that final catch-all phrase, and what does it tell us about how confident he wants his readers to be?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>My commandment is this — to love one another just as I have loved you. <sup>13</sup>No one has greater love than this — that one lays down his life for his friends. <sup>14</sup>You are my friends if you do what I command you. <sup>15</sup>I no longer call you slaves, because the slave does not understand what his master is doing. But I have called you friends, because I have revealed to you everything I heard from my Father.

elements.question: What sort of relationship do Jesus’ disciples have with him?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus calls them friends. He distinguishes this explicitly from the relationship of a slave to a master — a slave does not know what the master is doing, is not let into the master’s confidence, does not share in the master’s purposes. A friend is different: Jesus has revealed to them everything he heard from the Father. The relationship is characterised by disclosure and intimacy — being brought into the inner circle of what God is doing — rather than by mere obedience to commands from a distant authority.
elements.questionHint: Jesus contrasts ’slave’ with ’friend’ — what is the key difference he identifies between the two, and what does that difference reveal about the kind of relationship he has with his disciples?

elements.question: What is the difference between a servant and a friend? What does it mean that Jesus calls us friends?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus defines the difference himself: a servant doesn’t know what their master is doing. Friendship, by contrast, involves being let in — sharing knowledge, being trusted with what matters. The slave obeys without understanding; the friend is included. When Jesus calls his disciples friends, he is saying that they are not merely recipients of commands but participants in his purposes, people he has chosen to share himself with. For someone whose deepest hunger is to be genuinely known and chosen — not just useful to someone — this is directly addressed: Jesus has called us not employees but friends.
elements.questionHint: Think about what makes a friendship different from a working relationship — is it just closeness, or does it involve a particular kind of knowledge and disclosure? What does Jesus say specifically?

elements.text:
This is what the Gospel offers in place of the controlled, risk-free simulation of intimacy: a relationship in which we are fully known – God knows our worst, without exception – and fully loved. Not tolerated. Not managed. Loved, with the same love with which the Father loves the Son.

And this is not merely a private, interior reality. It is embodied and extended in the life of the Church. The same God who calls us into relationship with himself calls us into relationship with one another – into a community of people who are learning, however imperfectly, to bear one another’s burdens and to love as Christ loved.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>So confess your sins to one another and pray for one another so that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person has great effectiveness.

elements.question: What practice belongs to genuine Christian community?
elements.sampleAnswer: Mutual confession of sins and mutual prayer. James puts both together: confession to one another and prayer for one another, with the result that healing can occur. The practice is bidirectional — not one person confessing to a leader, but members of a community being honest with each other. And the stated outcome is healing, which places this practice in the context of the whole chapter’s concern with suffering and restoration.
elements.questionHint: James combines confession and prayer as a pair — what does that combination suggest about what genuine Christian community is supposed to look and feel like?

elements.question: Why do you think confession to another person – not just to God – is part of what James calls for? What does it add?
elements.sampleAnswer: Confession to God is essential, but confession to another person adds something different: the actual experience of being known by another human being and not rejected. God already knows everything; we are not informing him of anything new. But when we confess to another person, we expose what is real in us to another human witness — and their response, if they receive us with grace rather than judgment, enacts in a visible, embodied way the truth that we are accepted. The shame of the secret is broken not just vertically, before God, but horizontally, in community. The thing that was hidden becomes known, and the relationship survives. That survival is healing.
elements.questionHint: God already knows our sins before we confess them — so what does confessing to another human being add that confessing only to God doesn’t?

elements.text: Genuine intimacy requires vulnerability – and vulnerability means risk. The Gospel does not remove that risk. People may still reject us. Relationships will still disappoint us. The Church is full of imperfect people who sometimes handle one another’s honesty badly. But if God is sovereign over our relational lives – able to use even rejection and disappointment to shape us into the likeness of his Son – then the risk of genuine intimacy is a risk worth taking. The controlled, consequence-free world of the addiction is not safety. It is a smaller life.

elements.passageText: <sup>28</sup>And we know that all things work together for good for those who love God, who are called according to his purpose, <sup>29</sup>because those whom he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, that his Son would be the firstborn among many brothers and sisters.

elements.question: What can God do with the painful and difficult events of our lives?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says God works all things together for good — not that all things are good in themselves, but that he is able to work through them toward a good end. Verse 29 defines what that good end is: being conformed to the image of his Son. So the painful events of our lives — including relational rejection, disappointment, and failure — are things God is able to use in the process of shaping us into the likeness of Christ. They are not wasted, even when they hurt.
elements.questionHint: Paul says God works ’all things’ for good — does that mean all things are good, or something different? What does verse 29 tell us about what the ’good’ is that he is working toward?

elements.question: If we trust God to be working even through failures and disappointments, what difference does that make to the risk of genuine relationship?
elements.sampleAnswer: It changes the nature of the risk without removing it. If a relationship fails or disappoints, that is genuinely painful — but it is not meaningless or wasted if God is able to work through it. The fear that makes people reach for risk-free substitutes is partly the fear that a bad outcome would be purely and finally harmful. But if God is sovereign over even the painful outcomes — able to use them for our growth and his purposes — then the worst case has been reframed. The risk of genuine relationship becomes a risk worth taking, not because the pain won’t be real, but because the pain won’t be the end of the story.
elements.questionHint: The reason people avoid vulnerable relationship is often the fear of what will happen if it goes wrong — how does Paul’s claim about God working ’all things’ for good address exactly that fear?

elements.text: Trusting God does not make life risk-free. But it changes the nature of the risk. If God is sovereign over our relational lives – if he is able to use even rejection and disappointment to shape us into the likeness of his Son – then the risk of genuine intimacy is a risk worth taking. The controlled, consequence-free world of the addiction is not safety. It is a smaller life.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Marcus’s story describes porn as something that offered him a feeling of being wanted, of being chosen. Can you identify ways in which other addictive behaviours might offer a similar feeling – a simulation of connection or belonging?
The chapter identifies a ’desire for sovereignty’ over our relational world – intimacy on our own terms, without risk. Where else in life do you notice this desire for control operating in you?

Romans 8:38-39 lists everything that cannot separate us from the love of God. When you read that list, does it feel like good news – or does part of you find it hard to apply to yourself personally? Why might that be?

Jesus calls his disciples friends rather than servants, because he has made known to them everything he has received from the Father. What would it look like to approach your relationship with God as a friendship – characterised by honesty, by being known, rather than by performance?

James 5:16 calls us to confess our sins to one another. Is there someone in your life with whom you have that kind of honesty? If not, what has prevented it – and what would it take to move toward it?

elements.text:
Two people. Two very different stories. Anne is fifty-six. Three years ago her husband died after a long illness. The grief was – still is – enormous. What surprised her was how quickly she found that a glass of wine in the evening took the edge off it. Not removed it. Just made it bearable. Then two glasses. Then the bottle was open anyway. Her GP, when she finally mentioned it, was kind but clear: what had started as self-medication had become dependency. Anne does not think of herself as someone with a drinking problem. She thinks of herself as someone with a grief problem who found something that helped – until it didn’t.

Daniel is twenty-seven. His life is, by most measures, fine. Good job, good friends, no particular crisis. But most evenings, after dinner, he picks up his phone. He is not sure exactly when this became the thing he does – it just is, now. An hour on social media, maybe more. He is not enjoying it, exactly. He is barely aware of it. But the thought of stopping, of sitting in a quiet room with nothing in his hands, produces a faint but real anxiety that he has never quite examined. The phone is not a pleasure. It is a place. And without it, the evenings feel formless and strange.

Anne and Daniel are looking for different things. Anne wants the pain to stop. Daniel wants the emptiness to be filled. But both have found, in their different ways, a behaviour that answers a need – and both are now more bound to it than they intended to be.

The longing for relief is real and God-given. God made us as creatures who feel deeply. Emotional pain – grief, anxiety, depression, fear – is not a sign of weakness or insufficient faith. It is part of what it means to be human and mortal in a broken world. The desire for relief from suffering is entirely natural, and the Bible does not pretend otherwise.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Have mercy on me, LORD, for I am in distress; my eyes grow dim from grief; my whole body wastes away. <sup>10</sup>For my life nears its end in pain; my years draw to a close with a groan; my strength fails because of my sin, and my bones become brittle.

elements.question: How does the Psalmist describe his distress in this passage?
elements.sampleAnswer: The description is physical and total: his eyes grow dim from grief; his whole body wastes away; his life nears its end in pain; his years draw to a close with a groan; his strength fails; his bones become brittle. The suffering is not vague or spiritual in an abstract sense — it is embodied, consuming, and pervasive. The Psalmist is not managing his distress or describing it at a distance; he is in it, and he describes it with stark honesty.
elements.questionHint: The Psalmist describes his distress using both emotional and physical language — what does the use of bodily images suggest about the depth and kind of suffering he is experiencing?
