elements.question: What is significant about the fact that the Psalmist brings this level of raw pain directly to God (rather than managing it, minimising it, or keeping it private)?
elements.sampleAnswer: It means that this level of suffering is considered legitimate material for prayer. The Psalmist does not tidy up his pain before bringing it to God; he does not apologise for feeling this bad. He brings it raw and whole: ‘have mercy on me, for I am in distress.’ This stands against the instinct to manage or suppress pain privately, or to reach for something that numbs it. Bringing it to God — honestly, directly, without pretence — is itself presented as the faithful response to suffering. The Psalm models what it looks like to run toward God with pain rather than away from it.
elements.questionHint: Compare the Psalmist’s approach with Anne’s in the introduction — what is the difference between what each one does with their pain, and where each one takes it?

elements.text: For many people, the addictive behaviour is not primarily about pleasure. It is about the temporary suspension of pain. The fact that an addictive behaviour began as a response to genuine pain does not make it sinful in a simple or straightforward sense. It makes it human. What the Gospel offers is not judgment of the person in pain who reached for the nearest thing that helped – it is a better and truer form of the relief they were looking for.

elements.passageText: <sup>28</sup>"Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. <sup>29</sup>Take my yoke on you and learn from me, because I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. <sup>30</sup>For my yoke is easy to bear, and my load is not hard to carry."

elements.question: What is promised to those who are weary and burdened?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus promises rest — twice, and in two slightly different forms. First: ‘I will give you rest’ — something Jesus bestows, a gift. Second: ‘you will find rest for your souls’ — something discovered in the process of coming to him and learning from him. The rest is not merely the absence of labour but something positive: a settled, soul-deep peace that comes from being in relationship with one who is ’gentle and humble in heart.’ It is the rest that comes from laying down a burden at the feet of someone who can actually bear it.
elements.questionHint: Jesus uses the word ’rest’ twice — once as something he gives and once as something we find. What is the difference, and what does each one tell us about how the rest is obtained?

elements.question: Jesus speaks of a ’yoke’ – a working image, not a resting one. What does it tell us that the rest Jesus offers is found in coming to him and learning from him, not the absence of all demands?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that Jesus’ rest is not the rest of inactivity or the numbing of experience, but the rest of being yoked to the right thing. A yoke is a working implement — it harnesses two animals together so they can pull in the same direction. To take Jesus’ yoke is to be joined to him, working alongside him, under his instruction. The rest comes not from the absence of effort or demand, but from the difference in the burden: ‘my yoke is easy to bear, and my load is not hard to carry.’ The relief Jesus offers is not the anaesthetic that numbs pain while leaving the person alone; it is the relief of being accompanied and helped to carry what has to be carried.
elements.questionHint: What is the difference between rest that comes from stopping all activity, and rest that comes from being yoked to someone stronger who shares the load with you?

elements.text: The relief that addiction provides is real, but it is structurally temporary – and it comes at a cost that compounds over time. There is also a deeper cost: the anaesthetic keeps us from bringing our pain to God. The addiction becomes a rival to God – not because it is more appealing, but because it is more immediately accessible, and it works faster.

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>And God will exalt you in due time, if you humble yourselves under his mighty hand <sup>7</sup>by casting all your cares on him because he cares for you.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • Is it the same as complaining – or something different?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Lament tends to sound like complaint, self-pity, or a failure of faith – as if the spiritually mature person should endure suffering quietly and without protest. Many Christians treat it as something to be moved past as quickly as possible.

But nearly a third of the Psalms are laments – cries of pain, protest, and confusion directed honestly at God. The Bible does not treat this as a failure of faith. It treats it as one of the deepest expressions of it: the person who laments believes that God is there, that God hears, and that God is the right address for even the darkest human experience.

Lament is not the same as escape. Escape turns away from God along with the pain. Lament turns toward God precisely in and through the pain. That distinction is one of the most important things this chapter has to say.

elements.question: What does Peter tell us to do with our anxiety?
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter says to cast all our cares on God — the word ’cast’ is active and deliberate, like throwing something. It is not a passive drifting into God’s presence but an intentional act of transfer: taking what we are carrying and placing it on him. And this is possible because he cares for us — the reason for the casting is the character of the one we are casting onto.
elements.questionHint: Peter uses the word ’cast’ — that’s an active verb, not a passive one. What does the image of casting suggest about what we are supposed to do with our anxiety, and how deliberately?

elements.question: What is the reason Peter gives for why we can do this? Why might that be easy to forget in a moment of acute pain?
elements.sampleAnswer: The reason is that he cares for us. Not that he is powerful enough to help (though he is), but that he is personally concerned about us — the word is warm and relational. In a moment of acute pain, this is exactly what becomes hardest to believe. Pain has a way of making God feel absent, indifferent, or far away. The thing that gives Peter’s instruction its power — ‘he cares for you’ — is also the thing that suffering most readily obscures. Which is why the instruction needs to be held beforehand, not discovered in the middle of the crisis.
elements.questionHint: The reason Peter gives for casting cares on God is not God’s power but something more personal — what is it, and why might that particular reason be the one that acute pain makes hardest to feel?

elements.text: One of the most important – and most neglected – resources the Bible offers to people in pain is the practice of lament. Lament is the deliberate, honest bringing of our full pain into the presence of God. It is not a failure of faith. It is one of the deepest expressions of it: the person who laments believes that God is there, that God hears, and that God is the right address for even the darkest human experience.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>My God, my God, why have you abandoned me? I groan in prayer, but help seems far away. <sup>2</sup>My God, I cry out during the day, but you do not answer, and during the night my prayers do not let up.

elements.question: How does the Psalmist address God at the start of this lament?
elements.sampleAnswer: He addresses God as ’My God, my God’ — twice, with intense personal possession — and then immediately asks why God has abandoned him. The opening is simultaneously an act of faith (he is addressing God, not turning away from him) and a cry of desolation (he experiences himself as forsaken). The address ’my God’ is significant: even in the experience of abandonment, he does not stop speaking to God or stop claiming him as his own. The lament is directed at God, not away from him.
elements.questionHint: The Psalmist says ’My God’ even while accusing God of abandoning him — what does that combination tell you about whether lament is an expression of faith or a departure from it?

elements.question: What is striking about the fact that Jesus quotes this psalm from the cross? What does it tell us about God’s relationship to human suffering?
elements.sampleAnswer: It is striking because it means that God in human flesh prayed this prayer — used this exact language of desolation, of being abandoned, of unanswered cries. The God who receives our laments has himself lamented. He is not a distant observer of suffering who receives our pain from a position of untouched comfort; he has entered fully into the experience of it. This means that when someone in the depths of addiction-driven shame or grief brings their pain to God, they are bringing it to someone who knows it from the inside. The ’my God’ at the opening of that lament was, on the cross, Jesus’ own cry.
elements.questionHint: Jesus chose to quote this specific psalm at the moment of greatest suffering — what does his use of a lament psalm tell you about what lament is for, and whether God is a stranger to that kind of prayer?

elements.text:
Relief and Rhythm are different experiences of the same problem: time. Relief is about a present moment that is unbearable – and the desperate need to escape it. Rhythm is about time that is formless and empty – and the need to fill it with something that makes it feel inhabited.

Humans are not designed for formlessness. We need rhythm – regular patterns of activity and rest, ritual and routine, that shape our days and weeks. We need a sense of belonging. These needs are not trivial. They are built into the fabric of creation.

elements.passageText: 3God said, "Let there be light." And there was light! 4God saw that the light was good, so God separated the light from the darkness. 5God called the light "day" and the darkness "night." There was evening, and there was morning, marking the first day. … 2By the seventh day God finished the work that he had been doing, and he ceased on the seventh day all the work that he had been doing. 3God blessed the seventh day and made it holy because on it he ceased all the work that he had been doing in creation.

elements.question: What pattern does God establish at the beginning of Genesis?
elements.sampleAnswer: God establishes the rhythm of day and night — marking time into distinct, alternating units — and then the rhythm of work and rest, culminating in the seventh day on which he ceases from his creative work. Creation is structured as a series of rhythms: light and darkness, evening and morning, labour and sabbath. These are not incidental features of the creation narrative; they are built into its fabric from the beginning, established by God’s own activity.
elements.questionHint: God divides the first day into two distinct parts and then marks the seventh day as set apart for rest — what kind of structure for human life is being established in these opening patterns?

elements.question: What does the rhythm of work and rest, built into the structure of creation and then commanded in the fourth commandment, tell us about what human beings need in order to flourish?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that human beings are not designed for either constant work or formless unstructured time. We need both meaningful activity and genuine rest, in a patterned alternation. The fact that this rhythm is built into creation itself — not imposed as a religious rule but woven into the fabric of how things are — suggests it is not optional but necessary for flourishing. Daniel’s anxiety at the thought of an unstructured evening reflects this: the formlessness itself is uncomfortable because we were not made for it. The solution is not filling the gap with any available behaviour; it is replacing it with rhythms that are genuinely life-giving.
elements.questionHint: The pattern God establishes is not just ’rest’ but a regular alternation between work and rest — what does the alternation itself, rather than just the rest, tell us about what human beings need?

elements.text: For some people, the addictive behaviour is not driven by pain or loneliness. It is driven by something quieter: the need for structure, habit, and a sense of identity. Addiction fills the formless hours. It confers an identity: ‘I am the kind of person who does this.’ This is why recovery is hard even when the desire to be free is real. To remove the behaviour without replacing the rhythm or the identity it provided leaves a vacuum – which won’t stay empty for long.

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>You were taught with reference to your former way of life to lay aside the old man who is being corrupted in accordance with deceitful desires, <sup>23</sup>to be renewed in the spirit of your mind, <sup>24</sup>and to put on the new man who has been created in God’s image — in righteousness and holiness that comes from truth.

elements.question: What should we do with the ’old self’ and the ’new self’?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says we should lay aside the old self and put on the new self. Both are active, deliberate actions — not passive hopes or slow drifts. The old self is to be stripped off like clothing; the new self, created in God’s image in righteousness and holiness, is to be put on. Between those two actions, Paul places the renewal of the mind — suggesting that the active change of behaviour flows from an interior transformation in how we think.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses clothing language — laying aside and putting on. What does that suggest about whether change is passive or active, and whether it happens all at once or requires ongoing, deliberate choice?

elements.question: What does it tell us that Paul speaks of ’putting on’ a new self – an active, deliberate action – rather than simply having the old one removed?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that change involves active participation, not just the removal of the harmful behaviour. ’Putting on’ requires an intentional reaching for something — a new identity, new habits, a new way of living — not just the stopping of the old. This is directly relevant to the Rhythm dynamic: simply removing the addictive behaviour leaves an empty space. Paul’s language suggests that the work is not finished with the removal; it includes the deliberate adoption of what replaces it. The new self has to be actively clothed, not merely waited for.
elements.questionHint: If someone took off a coat in the cold, simply removing it wouldn’t solve the problem — they’d need to put something else on. How does that image apply to what Paul is saying about change?

elements.passageText: 2"Absolutely pointless!" says the Teacher, "Absolutely pointless! Everything is pointless." 3What benefit do people get from all the effort which they expend on earth? … 10I did not restrain myself from getting whatever I wanted; I did not deny myself anything that would bring me pleasure. So all my accomplishments gave me joy; this was my reward for all my effort. 11Yet when I reflected on everything I had accomplished and on all the effort that I had expended to accomplish it, I concluded: "Everything is futile — like chasing the wind! There is nothing of lasting benefit under the sun."

elements.question: What does Solomon say about the experience of a life lived ’under the sun’ – without reference to God?
elements.sampleAnswer: Solomon says it is ’absolutely pointless’ — a phrase he repeats for emphasis. The phrase ’under the sun’ describes life viewed and lived purely within the horizon of the material world, without reference to God or eternity. From inside that horizon, even the most intensive pursuit of pleasure and accomplishment yields the same verdict: futile, like chasing the wind. There is nothing of lasting benefit. The effort expended does not produce a proportionate return of lasting meaning.
elements.questionHint: Solomon had access to more pleasure and achievement than almost anyone — what makes his conclusion particularly compelling as evidence about what those things can and cannot provide?

elements.question: Solomon had access to every pleasure and achievement. What does his conclusion tell us about what those things can and cannot provide?
elements.sampleAnswer: Solomon’s extreme example is deliberately chosen: if anyone could have found lasting satisfaction in pleasure and achievement, it was him. He restrained himself from nothing. He got everything. And his conclusion after reflection is not that he found it wanting in one or two areas but that it was completely futile — ‘like chasing the wind.’ The things themselves brought temporary pleasure (he acknowledges that — ‘this was my reward for all my effort’), but nothing of lasting benefit. They could not produce meaning, and they could not satisfy the deeper hunger that drove the pursuit.
elements.questionHint: Solomon says the accomplishments ’gave me joy’ in the moment — so the problem wasn’t that they were unpleasant. What was the problem, and what does the phrase ’no lasting benefit’ point to?

elements.text: The Gospel doesn’t simply take things away. It replaces them – with something better, something that can bear the weight we have been placing on our habits and our addictions.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people of his own, so that you may proclaim the virtues of the one who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light. <sup>10</sup>You once were not a people, but now you are God’s people. You were shown no mercy, but now you have received mercy.

elements.question: What identity do those who belong to Christ have?
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter gives four descriptions: a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people of God’s own. Each carries enormous weight. ’Chosen’ speaks to election and purpose — not accidental, deliberately selected. ’Royal priesthood’ confers dignity and function — access to God and a mediating role. ’Holy nation’ marks a distinct identity and belonging. ’People of his own’ is perhaps the most intimate: possessed by God, belonging to him. And all of this is contrasted with their former state: once not a people, now God’s people; once shown no mercy, now recipients of mercy.
elements.questionHint: Peter gives four descriptions of who believers are — try reading each one and asking what it says about their worth, their belonging, and their purpose. What does the cumulative effect of all four together tell you?

elements.question: Read through the four descriptions Peter uses. What does each add to a person’s sense of who they are and where they belong?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘Chosen race’ provides the sense of being deliberately selected rather than overlooked or rejected — countering the experience of not being wanted. ’Royal priesthood’ gives dignity and function — not just status but purpose, a role that matters. ’Holy nation’ provides belonging to a community with a distinct identity — not isolated but part of something larger. ’People of his own’ is the most personal: owned, valued, specifically his. Together they address the kind of identity vacuum that addiction fills: the need to be something, to belong somewhere, to matter to someone.
elements.questionHint: Think about what it would feel like to be defined by each of these four descriptions — chosen, royal, holy, God’s own. Which of Daniel’s or Anne’s needs does each one speak to?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Therefore, if you have been raised with Christ, keep seeking the things above, where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God. <sup>2</sup>Keep thinking about things above, not things on the earth, <sup>3</sup>for you have died and your life is hidden with Christ in God. <sup>4</sup>When Christ (who is your life) appears, then you too will be revealed in glory with him.

elements.question: What should we ’set our minds on,‘ and why?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says to seek and think about the things above — the realities of where Christ is, at the right hand of God. The reason is grounded in identity: ‘your life is hidden with Christ in God.’ Our real life, our truest self, is not located in the material and temporal things of this earth but in Christ. Setting our minds on things above is therefore not escapism; it is attending to the most real thing about us. And the future orientation is significant: ‘when Christ appears, you too will be revealed in glory with him.’ What we are now, hidden, will one day be fully manifest.
elements.questionHint: Paul doesn’t just say ’think about heaven’ — he grounds the instruction in a statement about where our life actually is. What is that statement, and how does it explain why thinking about ’things above’ is the right response?

elements.question: How does knowing that your ’real life is hidden with Christ in God’ change the way you approach moments that need filling?
elements.sampleAnswer: If our real life is hidden with Christ — if our truest identity and deepest satisfaction are located there — then the formless moments that Daniel fills with his phone are not a problem requiring an immediate solution. They are, potentially, a space. A space for prayer, for reflection, for attention to the things that are most real. The anxiety that formlessness produces comes from the sense that if we’re not doing something, we are nothing. But if our life is hidden with Christ, our worth and identity are secure even in the quiet. The empty evening is not a threat; it is an invitation.
elements.questionHint: Daniel’s anxiety about unstructured time comes from a sense that it must be filled. How does Paul’s claim that our ’real life is hidden with Christ’ reframe what that empty time actually is?

elements.text: The Christian life is not a life emptied of ritual and habit – it is a life filled with better ones. Prayer, Scripture, worship, sabbath, service, the regular gathering of the community around Word and Table: these aren’t just religious duties. They are the practices through which God forms us into the people we were made to be. They provide rhythm – daily, weekly, seasonal – that is oriented toward something real and lasting, rather than toward the next fix.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Anne’s story is about using alcohol to cope with genuine grief. Daniel’s is about using social media to fill a formless evening. Which dynamic – Relief or Rhythm – do you find yourself identifying with more?
Addiction as an ’anaesthetic’ that keeps us from bringing our pain to God. Have you experienced a behaviour that reduced your need to seek God? What did that look like?
Psalm 22:1-2 – raw pain and apparent abandonment – is what Jesus quotes from the cross. What does it mean to you that God in human flesh experienced that degree of suffering?

Recovery from a Rhythm-based addiction requires not just removing the behaviour but replacing the identity and structure it gave. What rhythms and habits – in your own life, or in the life of your church – could do that replacing?

Ecclesiastes describes the emptiness of a life organised around pleasure and achievement. Have you experienced that emptiness – the moment after getting something you wanted, when it turns out not to be enough? What did it point you to?

elements.text:
Ryan is 38. He works long hours in a demanding job, has two young children, a mortgage, and a marriage that is – his word – ‘strained.’ He’s not a bad husband or father. By most measures he’s responsible. But every evening, after the children are in bed, he disappears.

Not literally – he’s in the spare room, in front of a screen, playing an online game he has played for years. In there, the rules are clear, the objectives are defined, and progress is measurable. Nobody needs anything from him that he can’t provide. Nobody is disappointed in him. The pressure – the relentless, accumulating pressure of being a father and a husband and an employee and a functioning adult – simply does not exist in there.

His wife has stopped asking when he is coming to bed. She already knows. Ryan does not think of himself as having a problem. He thinks of himself as having a way of coping. The distinction, he is beginning to suspect, may be less clear than he thought.

The longing for a place of safety – somewhere the demands of the world cannot reach us, somewhere we can simply be without having to perform or provide or protect – is a normal human desire. And in itself, it’s entirely right. God made us as finite creatures. We were never designed to carry unlimited weight without rest. The Psalms are full of refuge language. David turned again and again to the same image: God as rock, fortress, stronghold, hiding place.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>I love you, LORD, my source of strength! <sup>2</sup>The LORD is my high ridge, my stronghold, my deliverer. My God is my rocky summit where I take shelter, my shield, the horn that saves me, and my refuge. <sup>3</sup>I called to the LORD, who is worthy of praise, and I was saved from my enemies.

elements.question: What does David say about God in this opening to Psalm 18?
elements.sampleAnswer: David opens with a declaration of love — ‘I love you, LORD’ — and then immediately moves into a cascade of images for what God is to him: strength, high ridge, stronghold, deliverer, rocky summit, shelter, shield, horn of salvation, and refuge. He then adds the testimony that this isn’t theoretical — he called to God and was actually saved from his enemies. The passage presents God as someone David has personally tested and found reliable, not merely a theological concept.
elements.questionHint: David doesn’t just describe God in abstract terms — he describes what God has been ’to him’ personally. What does the personal language (‘my strength,‘ ‘my deliverer,‘ ‘my refuge’) tell you about the kind of relationship David has with God?

elements.question: What do you notice about the number and variety of images? What does that tell us about what David is reaching for?
elements.sampleAnswer: David uses at least seven distinct images in just three verses — strength, high ridge, stronghold, deliverer, rocky summit, shield, horn, refuge. The sheer accumulation suggests that no single image is sufficient on its own. Each captures something true but partial: strength speaks to power, the ridge and summit speak to elevation and perspective, the shield speaks to protection in battle, refuge speaks to shelter from pursuit. David is piling up images because what he’s trying to describe — the totality of what God is to him — exceeds what any one word or picture can hold.
elements.questionHint: If one word were enough, David would have used one word — so why might he need seven? What does reaching for image after image suggest about the size of what he’s trying to describe?

elements.text: Before we say anything critical about the search for refuge, we need to see that the need is real, and legitimate. God does not call us to be invulnerable. The fourth commandment isn’t a concession to human weakness. It is a recognition of human design. We are creatures, not the Creator. We need to stop.

elements.passageText: 35Then Jesus got up early in the morning when it was still very dark, departed, and went out to a deserted place, and there he spent time in prayer. … 31He said to them, "Come with me privately to an isolated place and rest a while" (for many were coming and going, and there was no time to eat). 32So they went away by themselves in a boat to an isolated place.

elements.question: What does Jesus do when the pressures of ministry are intense?
elements.sampleAnswer: When the pressures of ministry are intense, Jesus withdraws. He gets up before dawn to go to a deserted place and pray. When the demands on him and his disciples have become so relentless that there’s no time even to eat, he doesn’t push through — he actively invites the disciples to come away with him to a quiet place to rest. In both cases, the response to pressure is not more effort but deliberate withdrawal to a place of quiet and restoration.
elements.questionHint: Jesus doesn’t respond to intense pressure by working harder — what does he do instead, and where does he go? What does the direction he turns in tell you about where he finds restoration?

elements.question: What does it say about the nature of rest and refuge that Jesus – fully divine, fully human – needed and sought it regularly?
elements.sampleAnswer: If anyone could have sustained relentless engagement without rest, it would be Jesus — and yet he didn’t. He regularly withdrew. This tells us that the need for rest and refuge is not a symptom of weakness or failure; it is a feature of being human. Jesus took on full humanity, and part of what that meant was genuine tiredness, genuine need for restoration, and a genuine practice of withdrawal. If the Son of God needed to retreat to quiet and prayer under pressure, then the person who feels they have no right to rest — or who feels guilty for needing it — is holding themselves to a standard that Jesus himself did not claim.
elements.questionHint: If rest were a sign of weakness or insufficient faith, we might expect Jesus — who is fully divine — to have no need of it. What does the fact that he actively sought it tell you about the relationship between rest and human design?

elements.text:
The desire for refuge is not the problem. The problem comes when we seek it in the wrong place. Ryan’s story is like many others. The common thread across different addictions is the creation of a private world from which the real world is locked out.

For people who carry high levels of public responsibility – leaders, clergy, parents, carers, medical professionals – the appeal of a space of zero responsibility is especially powerful. This is not a character flaw. It is a predictable consequence of carrying more than any person is designed to carry alone. But escape merely defers the weight rather than addressing it.

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>If you faint in the day of trouble, your strength is small!

elements.question: What does avoidance reveal – and what does it cost?
elements.sampleAnswer: The proverb is blunt: fainting in the day of trouble reveals that the strength was never really there. Avoidance, in this light, is not neutral — it is diagnostic. Each time we retreat from difficulty into an escape rather than facing it, we are both revealing and reinforcing a smallness of strength. The cost is twofold: practically, the problem remains unaddressed and typically grows; personally, the capacity to face difficulty is not built but eroded. Avoidance may feel like self-preservation, but it is closer to the opposite.
elements.questionHint: The proverb doesn’t say fainting in trouble is understandable — it says it reveals something about the state of your strength. What does consistently avoiding difficulty do to your capacity to face it in the future?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>So let the one who thinks he is standing be careful that he does not fall. <sup>13</sup>No trial has overtaken you that is not faced by others. And God is faithful: He will not let you be tried beyond what you are able to bear, but with the trial will also provide a way out so that you may be able to endure it.

elements.question: What does Paul say to those who think they stand secure?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says: be careful you don’t fall. The warning is specifically directed at those who are confident in their own stability — which is precisely the condition most likely to precede a fall. The person who thinks they’ve got it handled, who has moved past vulnerability, who no longer feels the need to stay alert — that person is, in Paul’s view, in the most danger. Self-assurance about one’s spiritual resilience is not a sign of strength; it’s a sign that vigilance has been relaxed.
elements.questionHint: Paul doesn’t warn the person who feels fragile — he warns the person who feels secure. Why might confidence in your own standing be more spiritually dangerous than a sense of vulnerability?

elements.question: What is the encouragement in verse 13 – and what is its condition? How does God provide a ’way out’ of temptation?
elements.sampleAnswer: The encouragement is that no temptation we face is uniquely ours — others have faced it and come through — and that God’s faithfulness means he won’t allow us to be pushed beyond what we can bear. The condition implicit in the verse is that we are looking for the way out rather than accommodating the temptation. The ’way out’ Paul describes is not an escape from the trial but the capacity to endure it — God provides the means to stand, not necessarily the removal of the pressure. That way out may be as ordinary as another person, a moment of clarity, or a remembered truth — but Paul’s point is that it is always there, provided by a faithful God.
elements.questionHint: Paul says God provides ’a way out’ — but notice he says it comes ’along with’ the temptation, not instead of it. What does that tell you about whether the way out is the removal of difficulty, or something provided within it?

elements.text: There is a pattern that most people caught in the Refuge dynamic will recognise. The pressure becomes unbearable; we turn to the refuge for relief; the release is followed by guilt and shame and practical consequences; those consequences add to the pressure; the cycle repeats. The hiding place becomes a trap. Nothing is truly hidden: God sees what we do in the spare room as clearly as what we do on Sunday.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>For God will evaluate every deed, including every secret thing, whether good or evil.

elements.question: What is said about the hidden life – about things done in secret?
elements.sampleAnswer: The verse states plainly that God will evaluate every deed — with specific emphasis on ’every secret thing,‘ whether good or evil. Nothing is excluded from his scrutiny on the basis of being hidden from other people. The private world we construct — the one nobody else sees — is fully visible to God. This is not presented primarily as a threat but as a statement of reality: secrecy from other people is not the same as hiddenness from God.
elements.questionHint: The verse specifically mentions ’every secret thing’ as though anticipating the assumption that hidden deeds might somehow escape evaluation — what does including that phrase tell you about whether privacy offers any real concealment from God?

elements.question: Nothing is truly hidden. God sees what we do in the spare room as clearly as what we do on Sunday. How does knowing that change the way you think about the private worlds you build?
elements.sampleAnswer: Knowing that God sees the private world as clearly as the public one dismantles the psychological logic of compartmentalisation — the sense that what happens in the spare room is separate from who you are on Sunday. If there is no genuine separation between the two in God’s eyes, then the private world can’t really serve its function as an escape from accountability. For some people, this knowledge is primarily alarming; for others, it can actually be releasing — because it means there is no performance required, no need to maintain the gap between the two selves. God already knows everything, which means there is nothing left to hide.
elements.questionHint: The ability to keep a private world depends on the belief that it is genuinely separate from everything else — what happens to the appeal of that private world once you accept that God sees it as clearly as he sees your public life?

elements.text: The God of the Bible does not simply tell us to stop hiding. He offers us somewhere better to hide.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>He reached down from above and grabbed me; he pulled me out of the surging water. <sup>17</sup>He rescued me from my powerful enemy, and from those who hate me, for they were too strong for me. <sup>18</sup>They confronted me in my day of calamity, but the LORD was my support. <sup>19</sup>He led me out into an open place; he rescued me because he was pleased with me.

elements.question: What does the physicality of David’s language tell us – ‘reached down,‘ ‘took hold,‘ ‘drew out’? What kind of God is being described here?
elements.sampleAnswer: The physicality is striking — God ’reached down,‘ ‘grabbed,‘ ‘pulled out.’ These are not the actions of a distant deity who issues instructions from afar. They are the actions of someone who descends to where you are, makes physical contact, and exerts real force on your behalf. David also says explicitly that his enemies ’were too strong for me’ — he was not rescued because he was strong enough; he was rescued because someone stronger intervened. The God being described here is active, engaged, and willing to come down into the chaos rather than waiting for David to climb out of it himself.
elements.questionHint: David doesn’t describe himself climbing out of the water — he describes someone reaching down and pulling him out. What does that direction of movement — downward, toward him — tell you about the kind of rescue God offers?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Those who live in the shelter of the Most High will find rest in the shadow of the Almighty. <sup>2</sup>This I declare about the LORD: He alone is my refuge, my place of safety; he is my God, and I trust him. <sup>3</sup>For he will rescue you from every trap and protect you from deadly disease. <sup>4</sup>He will cover you with his feathers. He will shelter you with his wings. His faithful promises are your armor and protection.

elements.question: What does God promise to those who dwell in his shelter?
elements.sampleAnswer: To those who dwell in God’s shelter, the psalm promises rest, rescue from traps, protection from disease, covering, and shelter under his wings. His faithfulness itself is described as armour and protection. The scope is comprehensive — physical danger, hidden traps, and disease are all included. Crucially, the promise is specifically to those who ’dwell’ or ’live’ in his shelter — it’s a promise linked to ongoing, habitual abiding rather than an occasional visit.
elements.questionHint: The promise isn’t made to those who visit God’s shelter occasionally — it’s made to those who ’dwell’ there. What might the difference between visiting and dwelling tell you about the kind of relationship with God that produces this kind of protection?

elements.question: The images here (shelter, shadow, wings, shield) are images of protection, not just comfort. What would it mean to inhabit these images as a lived reality, rather than simply as poetry?
elements.sampleAnswer: To inhabit these images as a lived reality would mean genuinely orienting oneself under God’s protection when under pressure — not as a poetic thought but as a practical act of trust. The shadow of the Almighty is real shelter only if you actually place yourself in it. The wings of God are real covering only if you actually bring what you’re carrying to him rather than carrying it elsewhere. To live inside these images would mean, practically, bringing fear to prayer rather than to a screen; bringing shame to confession rather than concealing it; bringing exhaustion to rest in God rather than to escape. The images aren’t decorative — they are invitations.
elements.questionHint: A shadow only shelters you if you actually stand in it — so what would it mean in practical, everyday terms to ’stand in’ the shadow of the Almighty rather than simply admiring the image?

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>And God will exalt you in due time, if you humble yourselves under his mighty hand <sup>7</sup>by casting all your cares on him because he cares for you.

elements.question: What does Peter tell us to do with our anxiety, and why?
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter tells us to cast all our anxiety onto God — and the reason he gives is simply that God cares for us. The command is total (‘all your cares’) and the motivation is relational rather than transactional. We don’t cast our cares on God because he’s obligated to take them, or because we’ve earned his attention — but because he genuinely cares. The action Peter describes is also set within a call to humility: it is precisely by humbling ourselves under God’s hand that we lay down the cares. The person who insists on managing everything themselves is, in Peter’s framing, exercising a form of pride.
elements.questionHint: Peter gives a reason for casting your cares on God — he ’cares for you.’ Why does the motivation matter? How is casting your anxiety onto someone who genuinely cares different from dumping it on someone out of convenience?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • Is it mainly about Sunday church attendance – or something more?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Sabbath is often reduced to a day of the week, or to the question of whether Christians should attend church on Sundays. For many people it feels like an Old Testament regulation that has little to say to modern life.

But the sabbath commandment in Exodus roots the practice in creation itself: God worked and then stopped, and built that rhythm into the fabric of the world. In Deuteronomy, it is also rooted in redemption: you were slaves, and slaves cannot stop – but you are free, so stop. Sabbath is both a creational norm and a gospel declaration.

This chapter’s argument is that the person with no genuine sabbath – no regular, protected space of rest oriented toward God – is the person most vulnerable to counterfeit refuges. Sabbath is not a religious obligation. It is God’s own provision for a need he built into us.

elements.question: The word for ’cast’ is vivid – throwing, not gently handing over. What does that say about the kind of trust Peter describes?
elements.sampleAnswer: Throwing is not tentative — it’s a decisive, whole-body action. You don’t throw something while still gripping it; you release it completely. The image suggests that the kind of trust Peter is describing isn’t the cautious half-surrender of someone who is willing to let God be involved but wants to stay in control of the outcome. It’s the decisive action of someone who actually lets go. This is not something you can do gradually or in stages — you either throw or you don’t. The forcefulness of the word is the point.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between gently placing something down while keeping your hand on it, and throwing it — what does the physical completeness of throwing tell you about what genuine trust actually requires?

elements.text: Both escape and lament involve turning away from the pressure of the present moment. But they turn in opposite directions. Escape turns away from God as well as from the pressure. Lament turns toward God precisely in the pressure – it seeks relief through God, by bringing the weight honestly into his presence.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>With my own voice I cry out to the LORD; with my own voice I passionately plead with the LORD for mercy. <sup>2</sup>I pour out my lament before him; I tell him about my trouble. <sup>3</sup>Even when my spirit is faint within me, you know my way.

elements.question: How does David bring his distress to God?
elements.sampleAnswer: David brings his distress to God with his own voice — crying out, passionately pleading, pouring out his lament, telling God directly about his trouble. There is nothing polished or dignified about it. He doesn’t manage his presentation or soften the edges — he pours it out. And yet even at his lowest point (‘even when my spirit is faint within me’), he anchors himself in the knowledge that God knows his way. The distress is brought in full, unedited, and the act of bringing it is itself a form of trust.
elements.questionHint: David uses active, physical verbs — crying out, pouring out, telling. What does the rawness of his language suggest about whether you need to tidy up your distress before bringing it to God?

elements.question: What is the difference between the relief David finds here and the relief that escape from pressure offers?
elements.sampleAnswer: Escape offers relief by removing you from the pressure — but it leaves the pressure intact and returns you to it, usually heavier than before, with the added weight of guilt. David’s lament moves in the opposite direction: rather than turning away from the pressure, he turns into it — bringing it directly before God. The relief he finds doesn’t come from the pressure disappearing but from the weight being shared with someone who ’knows his way’ even when his spirit is faint. The difference is the direction: escape turns its back on God as well as on the problem; lament takes both the problem and the self directly to God.
elements.questionHint: Escape works by creating distance from the pressure — lament works differently. Where does David take the pressure, and what is the difference between pressure that you’ve run away from and pressure that you’ve brought honestly to God?

elements.text: Sabbath is, among other things, God’s own provision for the human need for refuge. It is a regular, recurring, built-in permission to stop. To lay down the weight. To exist, for a defined and protected period, as a creature rather than a performer.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>"Remember the Sabbath day to set it apart as holy. <sup>9</sup>For six days you may labor and do all your work, <sup>10</sup>but the seventh day is a Sabbath to the LORD your God; on it you shall not do any work, you, or your son, or your daughter, or your male servant, or your female servant, or your cattle, or the resident foreigner who is in your gates. <sup>11</sup>For in six days the LORD made the heavens and the earth and the sea and all that is in them, and he rested on the seventh day; therefore the LORD blessed the Sabbath day and set it apart as holy.

elements.question: What reason does God give for the sabbath commandment?
elements.sampleAnswer: In Exodus, the reason given is creational: God himself worked for six days and rested on the seventh, and blessed and set apart that day of rest. The Sabbath is therefore not an arbitrary regulation — it is a participation in the pattern that God himself established at creation. Human beings are being invited to rest because God rested; the rhythm of work and rest is built into the structure of the world itself, not imposed on it from outside.
elements.questionHint: God doesn’t just command the Sabbath — he gives a reason beginning with ’for.’ What pattern does he point to as the basis for the command, and what does grounding Sabbath in creation tell you about whether rest is optional for human beings?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>"Be careful to observe the Sabbath day just as the LORD your God has commanded you. <sup>13</sup>You are to work and do all your tasks in six days, <sup>14</sup>but the seventh day is the Sabbath of the LORD your God. On that day you must not do any work, neither you, your son, your daughter, your male slave, your female slave, your ox, your donkey, any other animal, or the foreigner who lives with you, so that your male and female slaves may rest as well as you. <sup>15</sup>Recall that you were slaves in Egypt and that the LORD your God brought you out of there by strength and power. That is why the LORD your God has commanded you to observe the Sabbath day.

elements.question: And what additional reason does he give in Deuteronomy?
elements.sampleAnswer: In Deuteronomy, the additional reason is redemptive: remember that you were slaves in Egypt, and God brought you out. Slaves cannot stop — they work because they have no choice. But you are free. The Sabbath is therefore not just a creational pattern to follow; it is a weekly declaration of freedom. To stop is to embody the reality of your liberation. The person who cannot stop — who is driven by relentless pressure and never rests — is living, in practice, like a slave, even if they’re technically free.
elements.questionHint: In Deuteronomy, God says ’remember that you were slaves’ as the reason for the Sabbath — what is it about slavery that makes stopping impossible, and what does that imply about the person who is never able to rest?

elements.question: What do these two reasons – creation and redemption – together tell us about the nature of sabbath rest? What is it a reminder of, and what does it free us from?
elements.sampleAnswer: Together, the two reasons tell us that sabbath rest is simultaneously a creational and a gospel act. As a creational act, it acknowledges our true nature — we are creatures, not the Creator; we were made within a rhythm of work and rest, not designed for relentless effort. As a gospel act, it enacts our freedom — we are not slaves, we are redeemed people who are free to stop. Sabbath is therefore a reminder of both who God is (the Creator who himself rested) and who we are (freed people, not driven ones). It frees us from the performance identity — the belief that our worth is tied to our productivity, and that stopping is a failure.
elements.questionHint: Creation says ’you were made for rest’; redemption says ’you are free to rest.’ What does combining those two reasons tell you about what kind of person would find it impossible to keep the Sabbath — and what might that impossibility reveal about them?

elements.text: The person who has no genuine sabbath – no regular, protected space of rest and refuge in God – is the person most vulnerable to the appeal of a counterfeit refuge. Ryan is spending his evenings in the spare room not because he is weak, but because he has nowhere legitimate to put the weight down. The answer is not simply to remove the game. It is to build, in its place, a genuine practice of rest – rest that is oriented toward God, that acknowledges creatureliness, and that does not need to hide from anything.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Ryan’s escape is a video game. But the Refuge dynamic works across many different behaviours. What ways – perhaps quite ordinary and socially acceptable ones – do you seek refuge from pressure? What does the refuge offer you?

Rest is different from escape – both involve stopping, but they are oriented differently. How would you describe the difference in your own experience? Can you think of a time when you genuinely rested, rather than escaped?

Psalm 18:16-19 describes God reaching down, taking hold, and drawing out. Does that image of God feel real to you – or distant? What shapes your sense of whether God is actually present in your moments of greatest pressure?

People in high-responsibility roles are particularly vulnerable to the Refuge dynamic. Do you recognise that in yourself or in others? What makes it hard for responsible people to admit they are struggling?
What would a genuine practice of sabbath – real, protected, God-oriented rest – look like in your life? What would need to change to make it possible?

elements.text:
Two people. Two familiar moments. James has just finished a brutal month at work: project done, deadline met, client satisfied. He’s exhausted in the way that only sustained, high-stakes effort produces. On the way home, almost without deciding, he stops to buy beer. He doesn’t drink much, normally. But tonight he’s earned it. He deserves something. The bottle is open before he has taken his coat off.

Claire has had the children all week while her husband was away. It’s been relentless – school runs, packed lunches, homework, arguments, washing that never ends. On Friday evening, after the children are in bed, she opens her laptop. She tells herself she is just browsing. An hour later she has spent more than she intended to on things she does not particularly need. Briefly, she feels much better. Then she feels worse. She closes the laptop and tells herself she won’t do it again.

Neither James nor Claire thinks of themselves as having an addiction. What they have, they would say, is a way of rewarding themselves after hard work. The question worth asking is not whether the reward is reasonable. It is whether it is real.

elements.question:
• What do you think Christianity teaches about pleasure?
  • Does following Jesus mean wanting less?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Asceticism is the belief that physical pleasure is spiritually dangerous, and that holiness requires suppressing or denying the body’s desires. It has appeared in various forms throughout Christian history, and colours many people’s instinctive sense of what a serious Christian should look like: austere, self-denying, suspicious of enjoyment.

But this is not the Bible’s picture. The God of Scripture made a world overflowing with good things and declared it very good. He is described as one who ’richly provides us with everything for our enjoyment’ (1 Tim 6:17). The problem is never that we desire pleasure. The problem is where we go to find it.

This chapter’s argument is not ’want less.’ It is ’want more truly’ – to find the real thing rather than the counterfeit.

elements.text: Christianity is not asceticism. God is not a God who grudgingly tolerates human pleasure. He is a God who made a world overflowing with good things, declared it very good, and gave it to human beings to enjoy. The desires for pleasure, rest, and reward after effort shouldn’t be suppressed. They are good and right. The problem is not that we want pleasure. The problem is where we go to find it, and what we do when the pleasure we find turns out not to be enough.

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>Command those who are rich in this world’s goods not to be haughty or to set their hope on riches, which are uncertain, but on God who richly provides us with all things for our enjoyment.

elements.question: What is God’s relationship to the good things of creation?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul describes God as the one who ’richly provides us with all things for our enjoyment.’ God is not a reluctant permitter of pleasure — he is its generous source. The word ’richly’ is important: this is not a God who provides a bare minimum and resents any excess. He is extravagant in his provision. The good things of creation are not things we enjoy despite God; they are things he provides specifically for enjoyment. The problem the verse identifies is not enjoyment itself, but misplaced hope — setting your heart on the gifts rather than the giver.
elements.questionHint: Paul doesn’t say God merely allows enjoyment — he says God ’richly provides’ for it. What does the word ’richly’ tell you about the attitude with which God gives, and how does that differ from the picture of God as reluctant or suspicious of human pleasure?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • Is the sense of ’I deserve this’ always wrong?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
’Entitlement’ is often used dismissively – as a character flaw belonging to spoiled or selfish people. It is easy to hear the word and think: that’s not me.

But the chapter describes something more subtle and more universal: the belief, often unexamined, that our relationship with God operates on a transactional basis. I have given something; I am owed something in return. When the return doesn’t come – when sacrifice goes unrewarded, when faithfulness produces suffering not blessing – the transaction feels broken, and we feel justified in taking what we are owed by other means.

This is not a flaw unique to difficult people. It is a theological error that runs through many sincere Christian lives – and it drives more addictive behaviour than most people recognise.

elements.question: God doesn’t just allow enjoyment, but ’richly provides’ for it. What does that tell us about him?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that God’s generosity is not reluctant or minimal — it is abundant. A God who merely permits enjoyment is grudging; a God who ’richly provides’ for it is genuinely delighted to give. This matters because it dismantles the image of God as a figure who tolerates human pleasure while really preferring austerity. The God of this verse is the kind of host who doesn’t give guests a glass of water and call it hospitality — he lavishes. His generosity is a reflection of his character, not a concession to human weakness.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between a host who tolerates your enjoyment and one who actively, generously provides for it — which of those pictures does Paul paint here, and what does it tell you about what kind of God you’re dealing with?

elements.text: The entitlement dynamic is one of the least examined false promises addiction makes. It operates across almost every addictive behaviour, and it sounds entirely reasonable in the moment: ‘I’ve worked hard. I’ve given up a lot. I deserve something for me.’

elements.passageText: …choosing rather 25to be ill-treated with the people of God than to enjoy sin’s fleeting pleasure.

elements.question: What does Paul say about the ’pleasures of sin’?
elements.sampleAnswer: The writer of Hebrews acknowledges frankly that sin has pleasures — he doesn’t deny it. Moses had access to them, and they were real. But they are described as ’fleeting’ — temporary, short-lived, passing away. The contrast being drawn is not between pleasure and no pleasure, but between pleasure that lasts and pleasure that doesn’t. Moses chose against sin’s pleasures not because he was indifferent to pleasure but because he was looking for something better and more durable.
elements.questionHint: Notice that the writer doesn’t say sin’s pleasures are false or non-existent — he acknowledges they’re real. So what is the actual problem with them, according to the word he uses to describe them?

elements.question: What’s significant about the word ’fleeting’ (‘for a short time’)? What does it tell us about the type of reward addiction offers?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘Fleeting’ captures the central problem with the reward addiction offers: it expires. The pleasure is real in the moment but doesn’t hold. This is not a moral judgment so much as a structural observation — the reward is built to run out, which is precisely what drives the cycle of return. If the reward were genuinely satisfying, one experience would be enough. The compulsive return is itself evidence that the pleasure didn’t deliver what it promised. Every person caught in an addictive cycle already knows this, even if they can’t yet articulate it.
elements.questionHint: If a reward genuinely satisfied the need it promised to meet, would you need to keep returning to it? What does the compulsive pattern of return tell you about whether the pleasure is actually delivering on its promise?

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>Jesus replied, "Everyone who drinks some of this water will be thirsty again. <sup>14</sup>But whoever drinks some of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again, but the water that I will give him will become in him a fountain of water springing up to eternal life."

elements.question: What does Jesus say about the water from the well?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says that anyone who drinks ordinary water will be thirsty again — the satisfaction it offers is real but temporary. It meets the need in the moment but doesn’t end the need. By contrast, the water he offers doesn’t merely satisfy thirst — it becomes a spring within the person, welling up to eternal life. The difference is not just duration but source: ordinary water satisfies from outside, temporarily; the water Jesus gives becomes an internal, self-renewing source.
elements.questionHint: Jesus points out that ordinary water satisfies — but only for a time. What is the structural difference between a water supply that runs out and needs replenishing, and a spring that wells up from within?

elements.question: Jesus speaks initially about physical water, but the conversation soon moves to her relational life (her five husbands, plus one). What is the connection between the two? What’s she been looking for, and why has it kept not working?
elements.sampleAnswer: The water metaphor and the woman’s relational history are connected by the same structure: she has been returning again and again to something that satisfies briefly and then runs out. Six relationships, each presumably entered with some hope of finally finding what she needs — and each one failing to provide it permanently. What she’s been looking for is the thing that will end the thirst, not just temporarily meet it. The tragedy of her story is not that she’s been looking for satisfaction in the wrong place — that’s understandable — but that she’s kept returning to the same kind of well expecting a different result. Jesus names the pattern without condemning her, and offers the alternative.
elements.questionHint: The woman has had five husbands and is with a sixth man — each presumably offered something real, or she wouldn’t have kept trying. What does the pattern of return tell you about whether any of them actually satisfied the underlying need?

elements.text: There is a specific version of the reward dynamic worth examining directly: the sense that we are owed something – by God, by life, by the people around us – that we are not receiving. This reflects a theological error: the belief that our relationship with God operates on a transactional basis. But God does not relate to us on a transactional basis. He relates to us on the basis of grace. What we deserve from God is judgment. What we receive, in Christ, is the full inheritance of beloved children.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>I am not saying this because I am in need, for I have learned to be content in any circumstance. <sup>12</sup>I have experienced times of need and times of abundance. In any and every circumstance I have learned the secret of contentment, whether I go satisfied or hungry, have plenty or nothing. <sup>13</sup>I am able to do all things through the one who strengthens me.

elements.question: What does Paul say about contentment, and how did he gain it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says he has learned to be content in any circumstance — in need and in abundance, satisfied and hungry. He’s been tested in both directions. The source of his contentment is not favourable circumstances but the one who strengthens him. Crucially, he says he ’learned’ it — it wasn’t natural or instant. Contentment, in Paul’s account, is a skill acquired through experience, not a temperamental trait or a spiritual gift given at conversion.
elements.questionHint: Paul says he has ’learned’ contentment — not that he was born with it, or that it arrived suddenly. What does describing it as something learned tell you about how contentment is acquired, and how long you might expect it to take?

elements.question: What is significant about contentment being ’learned’ rather than naturally possessed? What does that suggest about how it is acquired?
elements.sampleAnswer: If contentment were a natural possession, the absence of it would simply be a character flaw — and the solution would be to try harder to feel content. But if it’s learned, then its absence in a given moment is not a failure but a starting point, and its acquisition is a process that takes time and experience. ’Learned’ also implies a teacher and a curriculum — Paul hints at what both are: the teacher is Christ, and the curriculum is the experience of being sustained through both abundance and need. Contentment is not manufactured through effort; it is acquired through the repeated experience of being held.
elements.questionHint: Think about the things you know because you’ve genuinely lived them through, compared to things you know theoretically — what kind of knowledge is contentment, and what kind of experience is likely to produce it?

elements.text: There is another version of the reward dynamic that is less dramatic than entitlement but perhaps more common: the turn to addiction simply because it offers quick and easy pleasure, and the alternative seems like too much effort. Boredom is not a simple absence of stimulation. More often, it is the presence of stimulation that requires effort – prayer, Scripture, genuine conversation, creative work – and the unwillingness to invest that effort when something easier is available. This is not primarily a moral failure – it is a failure of faith.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>On this topic we have much to say and it is difficult to explain, since you have become sluggish in hearing. <sup>12</sup>For though you should in fact be teachers by this time, you need someone to teach you the beginning elements of God’s utterances. You have gone back to needing milk, not solid food. <sup>13</sup>For everyone who lives on milk is inexperienced in the message of righteousness, because he is an infant. <sup>14</sup>But solid food is for the mature, whose perceptions are trained by practice to discern both good and evil.

elements.question: What is said about those who have become ’lazy’ in their faith?
elements.sampleAnswer: The writer says they’ve become ’sluggish in hearing’ — the problem isn’t initially a moral collapse but a dulling of spiritual receptivity, a growing unwillingness to engage with anything demanding. The consequence is regression: people who should by now be mature enough to teach others have gone backwards, needing the simplest spiritual nourishment rather than the solid food their stage of life should have equipped them for. The failure of laziness in faith isn’t primarily that it’s wrong — it’s that it prevents growth and actually reverses it.
elements.questionHint: The writer says these people should be teachers by now — so what has laziness in their faith actually cost them, and is the loss just personal or does it affect others too?

elements.question: What is the relationship between effort and maturity here? And what happens to those who choose the easier path?
elements.sampleAnswer: Maturity, according to this passage, comes through trained perception — and training requires practice, which requires effort. Solid food requires more of the eater than milk does; deeper engagement with God’s word requires more than surface-level familiarity. Those who choose the easier path don’t simply stagnate — they regress. They end up less capable of discernment than they were before. The choice of ease over effort in faith isn’t neutral; it has a direction, and that direction is backward. The ability to ’discern both good and evil’ — which is precisely the capacity needed to resist addictive patterns — is described as something acquired through practice, not given automatically.
elements.questionHint: Discernment — the ability to tell good from evil — is described here as something ’trained by practice.’ What does that tell you about whether the capacity to resist temptation is something you either have or don’t have, or something that can be developed or atrophied?

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>You make known to me the path of life; you will fill me with joy in your presence, with eternal pleasures at your right hand.

elements.question: What is said about the experience of those who delight in God?
elements.sampleAnswer: Those in God’s presence experience fullness of joy — not merely some joy, but fullness. And at God’s right hand are pleasures that are described as ’eternal.’ The picture is not of a thin, joyless existence but of a joy that is complete and pleasures that don’t expire. This is the positive counterpart to the critique of sin’s fleeting pleasures — what God offers is not less pleasurable than what sin offers; it is more so, and permanently.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist doesn’t say God’s presence offers duty or obligation — he says it offers joy and pleasures. What does the fact that the pleasures at God’s right hand are described as ’eternal’ tell you about how they compare structurally with the pleasures addiction offers?

elements.question: What does it mean that the pleasures at God’s right hand are described as ’eternal’? How does that contrast with the structure of the pleasure that addiction offers?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘Eternal’ means they don’t run out, don’t expire, and don’t leave you emptier than before. The structure of addiction’s pleasure is the opposite: it depletes as it delivers. You need more next time to achieve the same effect, and the aftermath leaves you worse off than before you started. The pleasure at God’s right hand is not diminished by being received — it is, if anything, increased. This is not a marginal difference; it is the entire difference between a genuine reward and a counterfeit one.
elements.questionHint: If the pleasures of God’s presence are eternal and those of addiction are fleeting, what does that tell you about which is actually the greater pleasure — and what might be standing in the way of believing that?

elements.text: The Bible offers us a remarkable counter-example to the entitlement dynamic – a man who had legitimate access to enormous pleasure and chose to give it up, not because pleasure is bad, but because he had his eye on something better. Moses is not a man who has learned not to want things. He is a man who wants the right thing more than the wrong thing – and who is therefore free from the tyranny of the wrong thing.

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>By faith, when he grew up, Moses refused to be called the son of Pharaoh’s daughter, <sup>25</sup>choosing rather to be ill-treated with the people of God than to enjoy sin’s fleeting pleasure. <sup>26</sup>He regarded abuse suffered for Christ to be greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt, for his eyes were fixed on the reward.

elements.question: What choice does Moses make, and on what basis?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses refuses his place in Pharaoh’s household — a position of enormous privilege and pleasure — and chooses instead to identify with a suffering people. The basis for this choice is not that he has suppressed his desire for reward, but that he has evaluated the rewards on offer and judged one to be genuinely greater than the other. He regards suffering for Christ as greater wealth than Egypt’s treasures, and his eyes are fixed on a coming reward that makes the present sacrifice worth making. The word ’faith’ at the start of verse 24 is crucial — his ability to make this calculation depends on believing in a reward he cannot yet see.
elements.questionHint: Moses doesn’t renounce pleasure — he chooses a different pleasure on the basis of a comparison. What does the word ’faith’ at the start of verse 24 tell you about what makes it possible to value the unseen reward over the immediately available one?

elements.question: What is crucial about the phrase ’he was looking ahead to his reward’? Is Moses suppressing his desire for reward – or redirecting it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses is not suppressing his desire for reward — he is redirecting it toward something he believes is genuinely more rewarding. ’He was looking ahead’ suggests an active, attentive orientation toward a future reality — not the elimination of desire but its reorientation. This is the gospel’s fundamental answer to the reward dynamic: not ’want less’ but ’want the right thing more.’ Moses’ freedom from Egypt’s treasures is not achieved through asceticism or willpower — it is achieved through a clearer and more compelling vision of what real reward looks like.
elements.questionHint: The verse says Moses’ eyes were ’fixed’ on the reward — which implies active, sustained attention. What does that suggest about whether freedom from sin’s pleasures comes through suppressing desire, or through directing it somewhere more compellingly?

elements.text: The Gospel’s answer to the Reward dynamic is not less pleasure but more – truer, deeper, and without the morning after.

elements.passageText: <sup>35</sup>Jesus said to them, "I am the bread of life. The one who comes to me will never go hungry, and the one who believes in me will never be thirsty."

elements.question: What does Jesus promise to those who come to him?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus promises that those who come to him will never go hungry, and those who believe in him will never be thirsty. The promise is total and permanent — ‘never’ is an absolute. He is not promising occasional satisfaction or relief; he is claiming to be the bread and water that ends the recurring cycle of need and return. The promise is not the absence of desire but its permanent satisfaction.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’never hungry’ and ’never thirsty’ — these are permanent states, not temporary reliefs. What is the difference between a food that satisfies hunger for a while and something that ends hunger permanently — and which is Jesus claiming to be?

elements.question: What does it mean to ’never be hungry’ and ’never be thirsty’? Is Jesus promising the absence of desire – or its permanent satisfaction?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus is not promising the absence of desire — he is promising its permanent satisfaction. Hunger and thirst are good things; they are the signals that drive us toward what we need. To ’never be hungry’ in the sense Jesus means is not to have desire removed but to have it so completely met that the compulsive, anxious search for satisfaction ends. This is not numbness — it’s fullness. The person who has eaten a genuinely satisfying meal is not indifferent to food; they are simply at rest from the drive to find it. Jesus is claiming to be that kind of satisfaction for the soul’s deepest hunger.
elements.questionHint: A person who says they’re never hungry could mean two very different things — that they’ve lost their appetite, or that they’re always fully fed. Which of those does Jesus seem to be promising, and how does that change the picture of what life with him looks like?

elements.passageText: 4Rejoice in the Lord always. Again I say, rejoice! … 11I am not saying this because I am in need, for I have learned to be content in any circumstance. 12I have experienced times of need and times of abundance. In any and every circumstance I have learned the secret of contentment, whether I go satisfied or hungry, have plenty or nothing.

elements.question: What does Paul say is the source of his joy – and what is notable about the circumstances in which he says it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s source of joy is the Lord — ‘rejoice in the Lord always’ — not his circumstances, not the outcomes of his work, not the comfort of his situation. What is remarkable is the context: Paul is writing from prison, has experienced both poverty and abundance, and has known need as well as plenty. His contentment is explicitly not circumstantial — it remains stable across conditions that would destabilise anyone whose joy depended on things going well. This makes his claim credible in a way it wouldn’t be if he’d written it in comfort.
elements.questionHint: Paul’s joy is located ’in the Lord’ rather than in his circumstances — so what happens to that joy when circumstances change? And what does the fact that he writes from prison do to the credibility of his claim?

elements.question: Paul writes from prison. What does that context do to his claim that the believer can ’rejoice always’? Does it make it more or less credible – and why?
elements.sampleAnswer: Prison makes the claim considerably more credible, not less. If Paul had written ’rejoice always’ from a position of comfort, security, and success, it would be easy to dismiss as the enthusiasm of someone who hasn’t yet been tested. But Paul is writing from prison, having also known poverty and need. His contentment has been tested in the very conditions that expose whether it’s real. A joy that survives imprisonment, deprivation, and uncertainty is a different kind of joy from one that has never been under pressure. The context doesn’t weaken his claim — it validates it.
elements.questionHint: Contentment is easy to claim when conditions are comfortable — so what does the fact that Paul makes this claim from prison tell you about the nature of the contentment he’s describing and where it comes from?

elements.text:
The life of discipleship is not the diminished life, the life of suppressed pleasure and reluctant obedience. It is the good life – the life of someone whose deepest appetites are being met at their true source, who is therefore free from the compulsive need to take the quick version whenever it presents itself. This freedom is not achieved by effort. It grows, slowly and unevenly, as faith grows – as the vision of God becomes more real, and the counterfeits become less necessary.

James, standing in his kitchen with his bottle, is not a man who needs to try harder to resist reward. He is a man who needs a bigger vision of what reward actually looks like. So does Claire. So do most of us.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: James rewards himself with alcohol; Claire with online shopping. Neither sees it as addiction. What are the ’rewards’ in your life – things you reach for when you feel you’ve earned something? What do they offer you in the moment?
The entitlement dynamic can feel reasonable: ‘I’ve given enough; now it’s my turn.’ Have you experienced this? What triggered it, and what did it lead to?
Boredom is ’laziness of faith’ – the unwillingness to invest effort in pleasures that require it. Is there a version that you recognise in yourself?
Moses gave up Egypt’s pleasures not by suppressing desire but by redirecting it toward a greater reward. What would it look like to redirect desire in your own life?
Psalm 16:11 speaks of ’eternal pleasures’ at God’s right hand. Do you find that appealing – or does it feel distant compared to more immediate pleasures? What shapes your response?

elements.text:
Tom’s been married for 11 years. The marriage isn’t what he’d hoped for. There is distance, disappointment, and a low-level resentment. On the evenings when the distance feels sharp, Tom finds himself on his phone. He wouldn’t describe what he does as revenge. But beneath the surface, there is this thought: she’s not giving me what I need, so I’ll take it somewhere else. That’s what she gets.

Rachel has been a Christian since her teens. She knows the gospel, and has taught it to others. But she has never quite been able to apply it to herself. The sins of her past sit on her like a weight that forgiveness never quite lifts. And so she returns, again and again, to a behaviour she hates. Not because it brings her pleasure. Because, in a way she can’t fully articulate, it’s what she deserves. The shame is not a side-effect of the behaviour. It is, in some dark sense, the point.

Tom and Rachel are not obvious companions. One is angry; the other is drowning. One reaches out; the other turns inward. But they share a root – a distorted account of what they deserve, and a failure to receive the grace that is the Gospel’s final word on that question.

The desire to be treated fairly – to live in a world where effort is rewarded, faithfulness is recognised, wrong is put right – isn’t a character flaw. It is a reflection of God’s image in us.
