A sense of grievance isn’t always sinful. Sometimes it is accurate. The question isn’t whether the grievance is real. The question is what you do with it.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>He has told you, O man, what is good, and what the LORD really wants from you: He wants you to promote justice, to be faithful, and to live obediently before your God.

elements.question: What does Micah say that God requires of his people?
elements.sampleAnswer: Micah says God requires three things: to act justly, to love faithfulness, and to walk humbly with God. These are not three separate commands but three interlocking orientations — toward other people (justice), toward character and relationship (faithfulness/loyalty), and toward God himself (humility). Together they describe a life that is rightly ordered in all directions: outward to others, inward in character, and upward to God.
elements.questionHint: Micah gives three requirements, not one — what is the relationship between them? Could you genuinely have one without the others, or do they form a single whole?

elements.question: What is the connection between loving justice and walking humbly with God? Why do you think these are placed together?
elements.sampleAnswer: Loving justice without humility before God can slide into self-righteousness — a person who loves justice on their own terms, who appoints themselves judge, who uses the legitimate desire for fairness to justify their own grievances and anger. Walking humbly with God is what keeps the love of justice properly oriented — it means recognising that we are not the ultimate arbiter of what is right, and that our own record before God’s justice is not clean. The person who walks humbly with God can love justice without becoming enslaved to a sense of personal grievance, because they know they themselves depend on mercy.
elements.questionHint: What might happen to a person’s love of justice if it isn’t accompanied by humility before God — what kind of justice might they end up pursuing, and whose interests might it primarily serve?

elements.text: Addiction-as-revenge operates across many different behaviours. What they share is that they are a taking-back of what feels owed, a protest against whoever failed to provide it. Many Christians carry a version of this directed at God himself – silently, because it feels too dangerous to name.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Where do the conflicts and where do the quarrels among you come from? Is it not from this, from your passions that battle inside you? <sup>2</sup>You desire and you do not have; you murder and envy and you cannot obtain; you quarrel and fight. You do not have because you do not ask;

elements.question: What is the source of our quarrels and conflicts with others?
elements.sampleAnswer: James locates the source of external conflict firmly in internal desire — in ’your passions that battle inside you.’ The quarrels we have with others are not primarily caused by those others; they arise from unmet desires within us that battle for satisfaction. This is an uncomfortable diagnosis because it removes the simple narrative in which the other person is the problem. The conflict on the outside is a symptom of a war on the inside.
elements.questionHint: James says conflicts come from inside you, not from the people you’re fighting with — what does that do to the simple story that the other person is the problem?

elements.question: What is actually driving the conflict-behaviour? How does ’wanting’ and ’not having’ connect to Revenge?
elements.sampleAnswer: James describes the cycle precisely: desire, non-attainment, escalation into conflict. The revenge dynamic begins with the same structure — I want something (love, recognition, fairness, respect), I don’t receive it from the person or situation I expected to, and I respond with an action that is, at some level, a protest or a taking-back. The connection between wanting and not having, and revenge, is that revenge is what happens when we try to close the gap between what we want and what we’ve received by taking rather than receiving. It’s desire that has gone through frustration and come out as aggression.
elements.questionHint: James traces conflict from desire through frustration to fighting — at what point in that chain does the response cross from understandable to destructive, and what would the alternative at each stage look like?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>"Will the Lord reject me forever? Will he never again show me favor? <sup>8</sup>Has his loyal love disappeared forever? Has his promise failed permanently? <sup>9</sup>Has God forgotten to be merciful? Has his anger prevented him from showing mercy?"

elements.question: What does the Psalmist say to God in his acute distress?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalmist asks God a series of raw, accusing questions: Have you rejected me permanently? Will you never be gracious again? Has your loyal love vanished? Have your promises failed? Have you forgotten to be merciful? Is your anger blocking your compassion? These are not polished theological inquiries — they are the cries of someone who feels abandoned and is naming it directly to God. The psalmist is not managing his distress; he is pouring it out.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist directs these questions at God — not about God to someone else. What does the act of bringing even angry and accusing questions directly to God tell you about the kind of relationship being expressed here?

elements.question: God has included this kind of raw prayer in Scripture. What does that tell us about how he receives our anger?
elements.sampleAnswer: The inclusion of this psalm in Scripture is itself a kind of permission. God did not exclude it, smooth it over, or replace it with something more theologically tidy — he preserved it, in all its raw accusation, as part of his word. This tells us that anger directed honestly at God is not a relationship-ending offence. It is, in fact, a form of engagement — the person asking these questions has not walked away from God; they are fighting with him, which is still a form of facing toward him. God apparently prefers honest anger to polished silence that is actually concealing despair.
elements.questionHint: God could have left this psalm out of Scripture — instead he preserved it. What does the decision to include a prayer this raw and accusing tell you about whether God is threatened by our anger, or capable of receiving it?

elements.text: The father points to what the elder brother has failed to see: ‘You are always with me, and everything I have is yours.’ The inheritance was never withheld. The elder brother was standing outside a feast that had always been open to him, nursing a grievance about what he had not been given, blind to the vastness of what he already had. Do you recognise the elder brother in yourself? In what areas of life do you keep a tally of what you have given, given up, or endured?

elements.passageText: <sup>29</sup>but he answered his father, "Look, I have been serving you all these years, and I never disobeyed your commands. Yet you never gave me even a goat so that I could celebrate with my friends! <sup>30</sup>But when this son of yours came back, who has devoured your assets with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him!"

elements.question: What is the elder brother’s complaint?
elements.sampleAnswer: The elder brother’s complaint is threefold: I have served faithfully for years without reward; you never gave me so much as a goat for a party with my friends; and now this son of yours — who wasted your money on prostitutes — gets the fattened calf. It is a complaint built on a ledger: years of service on one side, perceived neglect on the other. What’s notable is that he doesn’t address his father as ’Father’ — he doesn’t say ’my father,‘ ‘my brother.’ The relationship language has disappeared, replaced by accounting language.
elements.questionHint: Notice that the elder brother opens with ’I have been serving you all these years’ — what does leading with service rather than with relationship tell you about how he understands his connection with his father?

elements.question: What does his complaint reveal about how he understands his relationship with his father?
elements.sampleAnswer: The complaint reveals that the elder brother understands his relationship with his father in essentially transactional terms: he has given labour and obedience, and he expects a commensurate return. His years at home have not been years of enjoying his father’s company and inheritance — they have been years of working to earn something he hasn’t yet received. He is, in this sense, as lost as the younger brother, but in the opposite direction: not in a distant country, but in the same house, and yet fundamentally estranged from the father’s heart because he has never understood that the relationship is gift, not contract.
elements.questionHint: The father says ’everything I have is yours’ — but the elder brother is furious that he hasn’t received what he earned. If everything was already his, what does his fury reveal about what he actually wanted from the relationship?

elements.passageText: <sup>31</sup>"My son," the father said to him, "you are always with me, and everything that belongs to me is yours. <sup>32</sup>But we had to celebrate and be glad, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again, and was lost and is found."

elements.question: What does the father say in response?
elements.sampleAnswer: The father’s response is tender — he begins with ’my son’ — and then makes a statement that should reframe everything: ‘you are always with me, and everything that belongs to me is yours.’ The elder brother has been standing outside a feast that was always open to him, fuming about what he hadn’t been given, blind to the fact that the entire inheritance was already his. The father then explains the celebration not as injustice but as necessity — this brother was dead and is alive, was lost and is found. The rejoicing is not a slight against the elder son; it is the only appropriate response to the return of someone from the dead.
elements.questionHint: The father tells his son ’everything I have is yours’ — so what had the elder brother actually been missing, and why had he failed to receive what was always available to him?

elements.text: God does not owe us a good marriage, a satisfying career, or relief from suffering. But he has given us his Son, and in him, everything. The cross is his answer to our grievance, and it is entirely sufficient.

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>For while we were still helpless, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly. <sup>7</sup>(For rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though for a good person perhaps someone might possibly dare to die.) <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

elements.question: What does Paul say we were like when Christ died for us?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says we were helpless, ungodly, and sinners. Not promising candidates, not people in the process of improvement, not people who had shown enough potential to be worth investing in. The three descriptions together paint a picture of complete moral inability — we had nothing to offer and no capacity to change our situation. Christ died for us in precisely that condition, not after it had improved.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses three words to describe what we were when Christ died for us — helpless, ungodly, sinners. What do those three words together tell you about whether Christ’s love for us was a response to anything deserving in us?

elements.question: What does it tell us about the nature of grace that God acted ’at just the right time’ – before we had earned anything?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘At the right time’ — before we had done anything, while we were still sinners — tells us that grace is entirely God’s initiative and entirely unearned. The timing is the point: if God had acted after we cleaned up, it might look like a reward. Acting before we had done anything at all makes it unmistakably grace. This demolishes any transactional reading of our relationship with God — we cannot be owed anything by a God who gave everything before we had earned any of it.
elements.questionHint: The timing of grace — ‘while we were still sinners,‘ before any improvement — matters as much as the act itself. What does acting before we had earned anything tell you about whether our relationship with God can be understood as a transaction?

elements.passageText: <sup>32</sup>Indeed, he who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all — how will he not also, along with him, freely give us all things?

elements.question: What did God not spare, and what do we learn from that?
elements.sampleAnswer: God did not spare his own Son — he gave him up for us all. What we learn from that is the logic Paul immediately draws: if God was willing to give the most precious and costly thing possible, the very thing most deeply his own, then he cannot be a God who withholds lesser goods out of indifference or stinginess. The gift of the Son is the proof that God is not holding anything back from us — it is the ultimate demonstration of the direction of his generosity.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses a ’from greater to lesser’ argument — if God gave the most costly thing, what can we conclude about his willingness to give lesser things? And what does that do to the idea that God might be deliberately withholding good things from us?

elements.question: If God has already given us his Son, what does that tell us about his willingness to withhold good things from us arbitrarily? How does it reframe the idea of God not giving what we deserve?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that withholding arbitrarily is not in God’s character — the cross is the definitive proof of his generosity. If God gave his Son, the grievance ’God isn’t giving me what I deserve’ becomes almost impossible to sustain with integrity. What we actually deserve from God is judgment — what we’ve received instead is his Son, and in him everything. When something good we wanted doesn’t come, the issue cannot be God’s unwillingness to give; there must be a better explanation, grounded in trust rather than grievance.
elements.questionHint: If the cross is the ultimate demonstration of God’s generosity, what does it do to the complaint ’God is withholding what I deserve’? What does it reveal about what we would actually receive if God gave us what we deserved?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it means for Christ to have ’atoned’ for sin?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Atonement is sometimes understood as a vague word for forgiveness, or as Christ demonstrating solidarity with human suffering. More commonly, people understand it in everyday terms: to atone for something is to make up for it – to apologise, make amends, or do enough good to balance out the bad.

But the theological meaning is more specific. Atonement describes the satisfaction of a debt before God – the penalty for sin being paid in full, by a substitute, so that it no longer stands against us. Hebrews 10 captures it in a single image: Christ, having offered one sacrifice for sins for all time, sat down. The posture of completed work. Nothing left to add.

This matters directly for this chapter. The person using their addiction as self-punishment is, in effect, attempting their own atonement – paying a debt they cannot believe has already been paid. The Gospel’s answer is not ’try harder.’ It is: the priest has sat down.

elements.text: The desire to be clean – to be forgiven, to start again unburdened by the past – is entirely right. We are, in fact, guilty. We do, in fact, need to be redeemed. The conscience that will not be silenced is not an enemy. It is a signpost toward grace.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>When I refused to confess my sin, my whole body wasted away, while I groaned in pain all day long. <sup>4</sup>For day and night you tormented me; you tried to destroy me in the heat of summer.

elements.question: What does David say about the experience of unconfessed sin?
elements.sampleAnswer: David describes unconfessed sin as a physical and psychological weight that wasted his body, produced groaning, and felt like God’s hand pressing down on him all day and all night. The experience is not abstract — it has bodily symptoms and is relentless, even seasonal in its intensity (‘the heat of summer’). The point is that unconfessed sin doesn’t just sit quietly — it costs you. Concealment has a price, and David describes that price in visceral terms.
elements.questionHint: David describes unconfessed sin as having physical effects on his body — why might sin that is kept hidden produce more suffering than sin that is brought into the open and dealt with?

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Then I confessed my sin; I no longer covered up my wrongdoing. I said, "I will confess my rebellious acts to the LORD." And then you forgave my sins.

elements.question: And what happens when he brings it into the open?
elements.sampleAnswer: When David confesses — stops covering up, names his rebellion to God directly — God forgives. The movement is simple and immediate: confession, then forgiveness. The resolution of everything described in verses 3-4 is a single act of bringing it to God openly. The weight that concealment created is lifted by exposure — not by accumulating more good deeds to balance the ledger, not by enduring the suffering long enough to have paid the debt, but by the straightforward act of confession.
elements.questionHint: The relief in verse 5 follows directly from the act of confession — and only from that. What does that tell you about whether there is any other route to the relief David describes?

elements.question: What changes the weight of v3-4 into the relief of v5?
elements.sampleAnswer: The single act of confession — deciding not to cover it up any longer, bringing it to God in his own words — is what transforms the experience. Nothing else changes between verses 4 and 5: the sin is the same, God is the same, David is the same. What changes is David’s posture — from concealment to openness, from covering to confession. The weight of verses 3-4 is not relieved by time, by improvement, or by punishment. It is relieved by honesty directed toward God. The structure of the psalm makes the point inescapable.
elements.questionHint: Between verses 4 and 5, nothing changes about David’s sin or about God — so what is the only thing that changes, and what does that tell you about what actually produces forgiveness and relief?

elements.text:
Rachel’s story is less commonly discussed, but it is far from rare. Shame accumulates to a point where ordinary forgiveness feels insufficient. And so the person enacts their own judgment. The behaviour is the punishment. They hate what they are doing, but they need to do it: it is the only form of atonement available to them.

Self-atonement – however soaked in shame and self-loathing – contains a hidden pride. It refuses the sufficiency of what Christ has done. It says, in effect: the cross was enough for other people, but my sin is too great. That’s not humility. Humility accepts the gift.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>And every priest stands day after day serving and offering the same sacrifices again and again — sacrifices that can never take away sins.

elements.question: What is said about the repeated sacrifices of the priests?
elements.sampleAnswer: The writer says the priests stand day after day offering the same sacrifices again and again — and that these sacrifices can never take away sins. The daily repetition is not a mark of effectiveness; it is evidence of insufficiency. If any single offering had actually solved the problem, the repetition would be unnecessary. The endless cycle of sacrifice is its own testimony to its inability to achieve what it was attempting.
elements.questionHint: What does the fact that the priests had to keep offering the same sacrifices every day tell you about whether those sacrifices were actually dealing with sin permanently?

elements.question: What does that repetition reveal about their effectiveness?
elements.sampleAnswer: The repetition reveals inadequacy. A solution that has actually solved the problem doesn’t need to be repeated. The structure of the old sacrificial system — the same offering, again and again, day after day — is an implicit admission built into the system itself that it was never providing a permanent answer. The writer of Hebrews is using the very rhythm of the Temple to make the argument that it was pointing beyond itself to something better.
elements.questionHint: If you had to solve the same problem every day, what would that tell you about whether your solution was actually working? How does that logic apply to the daily sacrifices of the priests?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>But when this priest had offered one sacrifice for sins for all time, he sat down at the right hand of God, <sup>13</sup>where he is now waiting until his enemies are made a footstool for his feet. <sup>14</sup>For by one offering he has perfected for all time those who are made holy.

elements.question:
• Do you think shame and guilt are the same thing?
  • What is the difference, if any?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Shame and guilt are often used as synonyms, but they describe quite different experiences – and the Gospel speaks to them differently.

Guilt says: I have done something wrong. It is a verdict on an action. It is specific, and it is resolved by confession and forgiveness. When guilt is dealt with, the weight lifts.

Shame says: I am something wrong. It is a verdict on the self – on identity, not just behaviour. It is resistant to simple forgiveness, because forgiveness addresses what you did, while shame is about who you are.

The Gospel speaks to both – but differently. To guilt it says: your sins are forgiven. To shame it says something deeper: you are a beloved child. Not merely acquitted – adopted. Understanding the difference is essential for understanding Rachel’s story in this chapter.

elements.question: What does Hebrews then say about Christ’s sacrifice?
elements.sampleAnswer: Hebrews says that Christ offered one sacrifice for sins for all time — a single, unrepeated offering that accomplished permanently what the daily sacrifices of the priests could never achieve. The scope is total: one offering, for all sins, for all time, perfecting for all time those who are being made holy. The contrast with the repetition of verse 11 is intentional and stark: where the old system required endless repetition, Christ’s sacrifice required only one.
elements.questionHint: Hebrews describes Christ’s sacrifice as ’one’ and ’for all time’ — how does each of those words contrast specifically with what was said about the priests’ sacrifices in verse 11?

elements.question: Christ ’sat down.’ What does that say about his work?
elements.sampleAnswer: Sitting down is the posture of completed work. The priests in verse 11 stand — because their work is never done, the same sacrifice must be offered again tomorrow. Christ sits — because there is nothing left to do. The work is finished. Sitting at the right hand of God is not passive waiting; it is the position of accomplished authority. For the person trapped in self-punishment — trying to add their own suffering to what Christ has done — the image of the sitting priest is the answer: there is nothing left to add, because there is nothing left to do.
elements.questionHint: The priests stand because their work is never finished — they must come back tomorrow. Christ sits. What is the difference between the posture of someone whose work is ongoing and someone whose work is complete, and what does Christ’s sitting tell you about the state of the debt for sin?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.

elements.question: What is now true of those who are in Christ Jesus?
elements.sampleAnswer: There is now no condemnation. The verdict is not ’partially condemned,‘ or ’condemned pending improvement,‘ or ’condemned for past sins but acquitted going forward.’ It is total: no condemnation. The word ’therefore’ connects this to everything Paul has argued in Romans about what Christ has accomplished — the ’no condemnation’ rests on completed work, not on the believer’s performance. The word ’now’ makes it present tense — not a future hope contingent on further progress, but a current reality.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’no condemnation’ — not ’less condemnation’ or ’condemnation for future sins only.’ What does the absoluteness of ’no’ tell you about whether this verdict is based on how well you’re doing?

elements.question: What stands between us and that verdict of ’no condemnation’? What is the condition – and what is it not?
elements.sampleAnswer: The condition is being ’in Christ Jesus’ — union with Christ through faith. That is the only condition named. The verdict of ’no condemnation’ is not conditional on moral performance, level of progress in sanctification, absence of struggle, or degree of felt confidence. It rests entirely on the status of being in Christ. What it is not conditional on is anything we do or feel. This is the word that Rachel needs: the verdict doesn’t depend on her improvement. It depends on Christ’s completed work and her union with him.
elements.questionHint: Paul names one condition for ’no condemnation’ — being in Christ Jesus. What is notably absent from that condition, and what does that absence tell Rachel — or anyone living under self-condemnation?

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>And by this we will know that we are of the truth and will convince our conscience in his presence, <sup>20</sup>that if our conscience condemns us, that God is greater than our conscience and knows all things.

elements.question: What assurance is available even when our hearts condemn us?
elements.sampleAnswer: The assurance is that God is greater than our conscience — and that he knows all things. This is significant because John is not saying our conscience is wrong to feel condemnation (sometimes it accurately identifies real failure). He is saying that even when our conscience condemns us, the verdict of God — who knows everything, including what our conscience doesn’t — is greater and more authoritative. The self-condemning heart is not the final court. God is. And his verdict, in Christ, is ’no condemnation.’
elements.questionHint: John doesn’t say our conscience is wrong when it condemns us — but he does say something is greater than our conscience. What is it, and why does it matter that God ’knows all things’ — including things our conscience may not be accurately weighing?

elements.question: What is more reliable: our own heart’s verdict, or God’s? Why does that matter for the person trapped in self-condemnation?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s verdict is more reliable — because God knows all things, including the things our hearts don’t accurately weigh. Our conscience is a useful faculty but not an infallible one: it can accuse accurately, but it can also over-accuse, distort, accumulate shame beyond what is proportionate, and fail to register the full reality of what Christ has done. For the person trapped in self-condemnation, this matters enormously: the verdict they keep hearing from within is not the final word. The final word comes from one who knows everything — and that word is ’no condemnation’ for those who are in Christ.
elements.questionHint: John says God is ’greater than our conscience’ and ’knows all things’ — what do those two claims together tell you about whether the accusing voice inside you is necessarily giving you the most accurate account of your standing before God?

elements.text:
Guilt says: I’ve done something wrong. It is specific and is resolved by confession and forgiveness. Shame says: I am something wrong. It is a verdict not on an action but on the self – and it is resistant to simple forgiveness, because it is about identity not acts.

The Gospel speaks to shame differently than it speaks to guilt. To guilt it says: your sins are forgiven. To shame it says something deeper: you are a beloved child. Not merely acquitted – adopted.

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>But the father said to his slaves, "Hurry! Bring the best robe, and put it on him! Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet! <sup>23</sup>Bring the fattened calf and kill it! Let us eat and celebrate, <sup>24</sup>because this son of mine was dead, and is alive again — he was lost and is found!" So they began to celebrate.

elements.question: What does the father give his returning son – before the son has said a word of his prepared speech? What do the robe, the ring, and the sandals tell us about the father’s intention toward his son’s identity, not just his legal status?
elements.sampleAnswer: Before the son has said a word, the father is already issuing commands — bring the best robe, put a ring on his finger, put sandals on his feet, kill the fattened calf. The gifts come before the speech, which means they are not a response to the son’s repentance — they are the father’s pre-emptive declaration of restoration. Each gift speaks to identity: the best robe covers the shame and marks him as honoured; the ring is a signet ring, a mark of status and authority; sandals on the feet mark him as a free man, not a servant (slaves went barefoot). The father’s intention is not merely legal acquittal but full restoration of identity — the son returns not as a hired hand but as a son.
elements.questionHint: The gifts come before the son’s prepared speech — so they can’t be a reward for good words. What does giving them before hearing any explanation tell you about what the father is responding to, and what does each specific gift say about what kind of restoration the father intends?

elements.text:
To Tom – and to everyone whose addiction is, at some level, a protest – the Gospel says: God has already given you more than you could ever be owed. The cross is his answer to your grievance. Bring the anger there, and find it met with something larger than justice.

To Rachel – and to everyone whose addiction is a punishment and a penance – the Gospel says: the priest has sat down. The debt is paid. Your self-administered suffering adds nothing to what Christ has already accomplished. Receive the gift.

The Gospel does not merely adjust the ledger. It tears it up – and replaces it with a gift so large that the question of what we deserve becomes the wrong question.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>I relied completely on the LORD, and he turned toward me and heard my cry for help. <sup>2</sup>He lifted me out of the watery pit, out of the muddy slime. He placed my feet on a rock and made my steps secure. <sup>3</sup>He gave me reason to sing a new song, praising our God. Many will see this and worship him; they will trust in the LORD.

elements.question: What does God do for those who cry out to him from the pit?
elements.sampleAnswer: God hears the cry, turns toward the person, lifts them out of the pit and the mud, sets their feet on a rock, makes their steps secure, and gives them a new song. The movement is entirely God’s: he is the one who turns, lifts, places, and gives. The person in the pit doesn’t climb out — they cry, and God acts. The destination is also remarkable: not just freedom from the pit, but feet on a rock and a new song. The rescue is not just evacuation; it is relocation to something stable and a new capacity for praise.
elements.questionHint: David doesn’t describe climbing out of the pit himself — he describes crying out and being lifted. What does the direction of the rescue tell you about who is doing the work, and what does arriving on a rock with a new song tell you about what the rescue produces?

elements.question: What is the source of the new song?
elements.sampleAnswer: The new song comes from God — he ’put a new song in my mouth.’ It is not something David has worked up or performed. It is given. The song is praise to God, and it has an outward effect: many will see it and be moved to worship and trust. The source of the new song is the experience of rescue — the contrast between the pit and the rock, between the mud and the secure place. It is the song of someone who knows where they came from and who brought them out.
elements.questionHint: David says God ’put a new song in my mouth’ — he doesn’t say he worked up the praise himself. What does the source of the song tell you about the relationship between being rescued and being able to worship?

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Tom uses sexual behaviour as a form of protest – taking what feels owed. Rachel uses destructive behaviour as a form of self-punishment. Which dynamic(s) do you identify with?
The Psalms give permission for honest lament and even anger at God. Have you ever been angry at God? Did you bring that anger to him, or did it go underground?
The elder brother has been faithful – and his faithfulness has become a list of grievances. Where in your life do you keep a tally like that? What you feel is owed to you?
Self-atonement is ’a pride so thorough it has inverted itself into self-loathing.’ Does that feel accurate to you, or unfair?
Guilt says ’I have done something wrong,‘ shame says ’I am something wrong.’ Which do you more often carry? What would it mean for the Gospel to speak to your identity?

chapters.chapterTitle: The Gospel Offers More: Foundations for Change

elements.text:
There is a person who has sat through the previous seven sessions of this series. They have recognised themselves – perhaps painfully, perhaps with relief – in one or more of the dynamics we have explored. They have understood the framework. They have agreed, at least intellectually, that God offers more than the thing they keep reaching for. They have prayed, with complete sincerity, that this time will be different.

And then they went home and did the thing anyway.

This chapter is for that person. It is also, in one way or another, for everyone in the room – because the gap between understanding the Gospel and being changed by it is one of the most common and most distressing experiences in the Christian life. If you have sat through seven sessions and still feel stuck, you have not misunderstood something. You have discovered something true: that information, on its own, is not enough.

The will follows the heart. The heart follows what it worships. And what the heart worships is not changed by argument alone – however good the argument, however clearly it is understood, however sincerely it is agreed with. The problem is not ignorance. It is not even weakness of will. It is that desire is not changed by resolution. It is changed by encounter – by the slow, Spirit-worked process of finding God more real, more beautiful, and more satisfying than the thing we have been reaching for.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>For I know that nothing good lives in me, that is, in my flesh. For I want to do the good, but I cannot do it. <sup>19</sup>For I do not do the good I want, but I do the very evil I do not want!

elements.question: What is said about wanting to do good yet doing the opposite?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul describes an experience of complete internal contradiction: he wants to do good, he knows what good is, and yet he does evil. He does the very thing he doesn’t want to do, and fails to do the thing he wants. This is not a person who is indifferent to good; it is a person whose wanting and doing have become decoupled. The will is not free — it is in some sense captive to something other than conscious intention. Paul’s frankness here is striking: he is not describing a minor struggle or an occasional slip, but a structural reality of the fallen human condition.
elements.questionHint: Paul doesn’t describe someone who doesn’t know what’s right — he describes someone who knows perfectly well and does the opposite anyway. What does the gap between his wanting and his doing tell you about whether the problem of sin is primarily an information problem?

elements.question: What does Paul’s honesty here tell us – about the complexity of human motivation, and about the limits of moral effort as a route to change?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s honesty tells us that human motivation is not simple or unified — there are competing forces within us, and willing doesn’t automatically translate into doing. It also tells us that the limits of moral effort are severe: Paul is not a morally lazy person making excuses. He is the apostle, deeply committed to doing good, and he still finds that wanting and trying are not sufficient on their own. If moral effort alone could solve this problem, Paul would have solved it. The fact that he describes this experience at all tells us that the solution has to come from outside the effort-and-willpower framework.
elements.questionHint: If anyone in the New Testament had sufficient motivation and moral seriousness to change through effort and discipline, it would be Paul — so what does his description of this struggle tell you about the limits of willpower as a route to transformation?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Therefore I exhort you, brothers and sisters, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a sacrifice — alive, holy, and pleasing to God — which is your reasonable service. <sup>2</sup>Do not be conformed to this present world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind, so that you may test and approve what is the will of God — what is good and well-pleasing and perfect.

elements.question: What is the mechanism by which we are transformed?
elements.sampleAnswer: Transformation comes through the renewing of the mind. Paul doesn’t say ’try harder’ or ’obey more strictly’ — he says be transformed, which is a passive construction: it happens to you, not by you. The renewing of the mind is what produces the transformation, and the renewed mind is what enables us to discern and approve God’s will. The mechanism is not primarily behavioural but cognitive and, ultimately, spiritual — the reshaping of how we think and what we perceive as good, which then reshapes what we desire and choose.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses a passive verb — ‘be transformed’ — rather than ’transform yourself.’ What does that grammatical choice tell you about who is doing the transforming, and what is your role in the process?

elements.question: What is the difference between being ’conformed’ to the pattern of this world and being ’transformed by the renewing of your mind’? And what is the agent of that transformation?
elements.sampleAnswer: Conformity is passive in one direction — shaped by external pressure without awareness or resistance, like water taking the shape of its container. Transformation is also passive, but driven by a different agent and in a different direction: the renewing of the mind by the Spirit, which progressively reshapes what we see as real, valuable, and desirable. The world conforms us by saturating us with its values and assumptions until they feel natural. Transformation works by changing the mind at a deeper level — not through external compliance but through a genuine shift in perception and desire. The agent of transformation is the Spirit, working through the means he has ordained.
elements.questionHint: Both conformity and transformation are described as things that happen to you — but they have different agents and different directions. What is doing the conforming in one case, and what is doing the transforming in the other?

elements.text:
Before we can say how the Gospel produces change, we need to be precise about what the Gospel is – because many people carry a version of it that is too thin to bear the weight of recovery. The Gospel is not ’try harder with God’s help.’ It is not ’God accepts you if you are genuinely sorry.’ It is not even, primarily, ‘your sins are forgiven’ – though that is gloriously true.

The word translated ’perish’ implies not just death but destruction – the loss of everything we were made to be. And ’eternal life’ is not merely life that goes on forever, but life of a specific quality – the life of God himself, shared with us.

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>For while we were still helpless, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly. <sup>7</sup>(For rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though for a good person perhaps someone might possibly dare to die.) <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. <sup>9</sup>Much more then, because we have now been declared righteous by his blood, we will be saved through him from God’s wrath. <sup>10</sup>For if while we were enemies we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son, how much more, since we have been reconciled, will we be saved by his life? <sup>11</sup>Not only this, but we also rejoice in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received this reconciliation.

elements.question: What is the Gospel: what has God done, and for whom?
elements.sampleAnswer: God acted for the helpless, the ungodly, sinners, and enemies — people who had done nothing to deserve it and everything to disqualify themselves from it. What he did: Christ died for us, bringing justification through his blood, reconciliation through his death, ongoing salvation through his life. The result is not just forgiveness but active reconciliation — a restored relationship, not just a cleared record — and a present joy in God through Christ. The gospel Paul describes here is extraordinarily rich: we are not merely pardoned but reconciled, not merely saved from wrath but brought into rejoicing.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses four different descriptions for what we were when God acted for us — helpless, ungodly, sinners, enemies. What do those four words together tell you about the kind of people the gospel is actually for?

elements.question: Look carefully at Paul’s verbs: ‘died for,‘ ‘justified,‘ ‘reconciled,‘ ‘saved.’ What is the cumulative picture they build? What tenses does Paul use – past, present, or future?
elements.sampleAnswer: The cumulative picture is of a completed and ongoing work: Christ died (past, completed); we have been justified (past, with present effect); we have been reconciled (past, with present effect); we will be saved (future completion of what has already begun). Paul moves across time — the foundation is laid in the past, its effects are current, and its completion is future. The mix of tenses tells us the gospel is not merely a past event to be remembered, or a future hope to be waited for — it is a present reality operating in and around us now. We are ’now’ justified, ‘now’ reconciled, ‘now’ receiving these things.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses a mixture of past and present tenses in this passage — some things have already happened, some are ongoing. What does that temporal spread tell you about whether the gospel is primarily about the past, the present, or the future?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life. <sup>17</sup>For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world should be saved through him.

elements.question: What was God’s purpose in sending his Son?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s purpose was salvation, not condemnation. John states this explicitly in verse 17: the Son was not sent to condemn the world but to save it. The positive purpose is that those who believe will not perish but have eternal life. The motivation behind the sending is love — ‘God so loved the world.’ God’s initiative in sending his Son was not reluctant or judicial; it was an act of love aimed at rescue.
elements.questionHint: Verse 17 specifically says what God’s purpose was not — condemnation — before stating what it was. Why might John make that contrast explicit, and what does it tell someone who approaches God expecting judgment?

elements.question: How does a fuller understanding of what we are saved from and saved into change the weight of the Gospel for you?
elements.sampleAnswer: Being saved ’from’ is only half the picture. The word ’perish’ points to something worse than death — destruction, the loss of everything we were made to be. And ’eternal life’ is not merely survival; it is life of a specific quality — participation in the life of God himself, the life we were originally designed for and lost. When the gospel is understood as rescue from destruction into genuine life, rather than simply as the clearing of a debt, it becomes bigger and more compelling. For the person whose addiction represents a slow kind of destruction, the gospel’s offer of the life they were made for has a different weight than a message about rule-keeping or forgiveness.
elements.questionHint: If ’perish’ means more than just dying, and ’eternal life’ means more than just living forever — what is the actual scale of what the gospel is offering and what it is rescuing us from, and how does understanding that change the stakes?

elements.text:
If the Gospel is the raw material, faith is the mechanism by which it produces change. But faith is frequently misunderstood – reduced to intellectual assent, or to a feeling of confidence, or to the effort of believing harder.

Faith, in the biblical sense, is not primarily a feeling. It is an orientation of the whole person – mind, heart, and will – toward the reality of who God is and what he has done in Christ. The specific way that faith produces freedom from addiction is this: it sees through the false promise.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>because everyone who has been fathered by God conquers the world. This is the conquering power that has conquered the world: our faith. <sup>5</sup>Now who is the one who conquers the world except the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God?

elements.question: What is the victory that overcomes the patterns of this world?
elements.sampleAnswer: John says the victory that overcomes the world is faith — specifically, faith in Jesus as the Son of God. Not moral effort, not discipline, not willpower, not therapeutic technique. The victory is located in a particular belief about a particular person. John is not being simplistic — he is saying that the capacity to overcome the world’s patterns is generated by a right understanding of who Jesus is and what that means, because faith in Christ is what connects us to the power of God and to the new identity that makes genuine change possible.
elements.questionHint: John doesn’t say the victory over the world’s patterns comes from trying harder or from a programme of behaviour change — he says it’s faith. What does that tell you about where the power for change is located, and what your responsibility is in accessing it?

elements.question: What is notable about John’s claim here – that victory is not achieved through effort or discipline, but through faith? What kind of faith does he specify?
elements.sampleAnswer: What’s notable is that John doesn’t describe victory as the result of a programme or a sustained exercise of willpower — he describes it as the result of faith, which is a different kind of activity altogether. The faith he specifies is precise: believing that Jesus is the Son of God. This isn’t a vague spiritual orientation or a general trust in goodness. It is a specific conviction about a specific person — about who Jesus is, what he has done, and what that means. This matters because the faith that overcomes the world is not feeling confident or trying hard to believe; it is being rightly oriented toward the reality of Jesus.
elements.questionHint: John specifies what the faith is faith in — believing that Jesus is the Son of God. Why might the content of faith matter, rather than just the intensity or sincerity of believing?

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>I am not saying this because I am in need, for I have learned to be content in any circumstance. <sup>12</sup>I have experienced times of need and times of abundance. In any and every circumstance I have learned the secret of contentment, whether I go satisfied or hungry, have plenty or nothing. <sup>13</sup>I am able to do all things through the one who strengthens me.

elements.question: What has Paul learned to do – and what is the secret of it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul has learned to be content in any and every circumstance — a stability of inner orientation that is not dependent on the external situation being favourable. The secret, which he names in verse 13, is not technique or mental discipline — it is ’the one who strengthens me.’ His contentment is not self-generated; it is received from Christ. He is able to do ’all things’ — including enduring hardship and resisting what he doesn’t need — through the strength that Christ supplies. The secret of contentment is not having enough of the right things; it is being connected to the one who is enough in all things.
elements.questionHint: The ’secret’ Paul names in verse 13 is not a technique or a mindset — it is a person. What does locating the source of contentment in ’the one who strengthens me’ tell you about where you need to go to find it?

elements.question: Paul speaks of having ’learned’ contentment – a process, not an event. What does that suggest about the realistic timeframe for the kind of change this series is describing?
elements.sampleAnswer: Learning is a process that takes time, requires repeated exposure, and typically involves failure as part of the curriculum. Paul didn’t acquire contentment at conversion and then simply maintain it — he learned it through the experience of both need and abundance, over time, in a range of conditions. The realistic timeframe for the kind of change this series describes is therefore measured in years and seasons, not sessions or decisions. This is not discouraging — it is honest. The person who expects transformation to be complete after eight sessions is setting themselves up for disappointment. The person who understands it as a lifelong learning process, sustained by Christ, has a framework that can absorb both progress and setback.
elements.questionHint: Learning requires time, repetition, and usually some failure along the way — none of which can be rushed. What does framing contentment as ’learned’ rather than ’received instantly’ tell you about the realistic shape of the journey this series is pointing toward?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The word ’sanctification’ rarely appears in conversation, but the idea is straightforward: becoming more holy over time. The misunderstanding is treating it as a self-improvement project – trying harder, behaving better, managing sin more effectively.

But sanctification is more like a plant growing than a machine being upgraded. The life is already there. The task is staying connected to the source – not generating growth through willpower, but remaining in the One from whom growth comes.

This chapter is the destination the series has been building toward: not just freedom from addiction, but conformity to the image of Christ. That is a larger and more beautiful goal – and it is entirely God’s work, done in us as we cooperate with the means he has provided.

elements.text: The reordering of our desires – the slow shift from finding our deepest satisfaction in counterfeits to finding it in God – is not something we can manufacture through effort. It is the work of the Holy Spirit, given to every believer. The Spirit does not simply help us to obey. He progressively reshapes what we want – slowly conforming our desires to God’s own desires. This is sanctification. We cooperate with it, but we do not perform it. The pressure is off. The work is his.

elements.question:
• Have you heard this phrase before?
  • What do you think it refers to?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
’The means of grace’ is traditional theological language for the ordinary practices through which God typically works to transform his people: Scripture, prayer, worship, the Lord’s Supper, Christian community, sabbath rest, and service of others.

The phrase can sound mechanical – as if these are levers you pull to produce spiritual results. But that is precisely the misunderstanding. They are not magic. They do not work automatically. They are the normal channels through which the Spirit does his work – and neglecting them while expecting change is like complaining about being hungry while refusing to eat.

They are also not a substitute for the Gospel. The means of grace do not save us or sanctify us by their own power. They are the soil in which the Spirit works. The person engaging seriously with them over time is giving the Spirit the conditions he typically uses. The person neglecting them is, in effect, asking God to work without the tools he has chosen.

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness, for we do not know how we should pray, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with inexpressible groanings. <sup>27</sup>And he who searches our hearts knows the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes on behalf of the saints according to God’s will.

elements.question: What does the Spirit do within us?
elements.sampleAnswer: The Spirit helps us in our weakness — specifically in the weakness of not knowing how to pray. He intercedes for us from within, with groanings that go beyond words, and he does so in accordance with God’s will. The Spirit’s work within us is therefore not merely supportive; it is intercessory — he is actively mediating between us and God at a level deeper than our conscious awareness. He knows what we need better than we do, and he brings that before God on our behalf.
elements.questionHint: Paul says the Spirit intercedes ’for us’ from within us — he’s praying on our behalf, inside us. What does that tell you about whether the Spirit’s work depends on us having our spiritual life perfectly together before he can act?

elements.question: The Spirit intercedes for us ’in accordance with the will of God’ – even when we do not know what to pray for. What does it tell us about the nature of the Spirit’s work that it operates at a level deeper than our own conscious understanding?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that the Spirit’s work is not dependent on our insight, clarity, or articulacy. He operates at a level below and deeper than our conscious understanding — he knows both the mind of God and the true state of our hearts, and brings them together in intercession that we cannot manage ourselves. This is profoundly reassuring for the person in the midst of struggle who doesn’t know what to ask for, or who can’t find words for what they need. The Spirit’s work doesn’t wait for us to get our theology straight or our prayers coherent. He is already working, at the level where the real issues are, in ways we may not be able to see.
elements.questionHint: The Spirit intercedes even when we don’t know what to pray — which means his work doesn’t depend on our understanding of our own situation. What does it tell you about the sufficiency of the Spirit’s work that it operates at a level deeper than our own awareness?

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, <sup>23</sup>gentleness, and self-control. Against such things there is no law.

elements.question: What is the Spirit progressively producing in believers’ lives?
elements.sampleAnswer: The Spirit is progressively producing love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control. These are not the outcomes of a self-improvement programme — they are fruit, which grows as a natural consequence of connection to the right source. Notably, self-control is the last item on the list — not the starting point of the Christian life but a fruit that grows as the others do. The person who is trying to produce self-control by willpower, without the rest of this fruit, is attempting to harvest without the vine.
elements.questionHint: Self-control is last on the list, not first — it’s a fruit that grows, not a prerequisite for growth. What does that order tell you about whether self-control is something you can manufacture at the start, or something that develops as you remain connected to the Spirit?

elements.question: These are not achievements – they are fruit. What is the difference between a fruit and an achievement, and why does that distinction matter for how we approach change?
elements.sampleAnswer: An achievement is produced by effort and belongs to the person who worked for it. Fruit is produced by connection to a living source and belongs, in some sense, to the vine rather than the branch. A branch can’t produce fruit by trying harder — it can only remain connected. The distinction matters enormously for how we approach change: if love, joy, peace, and self-control are achievements, then their absence is a performance failure, and the response is to try harder. If they are fruit, then their absence is a connection problem, and the response is to return to the source. This reframes the whole project of change from a performance to a relationship.
elements.questionHint: A branch doesn’t produce fruit by effort — it produces fruit by remaining in the vine. What does that picture tell you about your role in producing the fruit of the Spirit, and what you should do when you notice its absence?

elements.text: If the Spirit is the agent of change, he typically works through specific, ordinary, repeated means – what the Christian tradition has called the means of grace. These are not magic. They do not work automatically. But they are the normal channels through which God does his transforming work, and neglecting them is like complaining about being hungry while refusing to eat.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, <sup>17</sup>that the person dedicated to God may be capable and equipped for every good work.

elements.question: What is the effect of sustained engagement with Scripture?
elements.sampleAnswer: Sustained engagement with Scripture makes the person dedicated to God capable and fully equipped for every good work. The path to that equipping runs through four specific functions: teaching (correct understanding), reproof (identifying what is wrong), correction (setting back on the right path), and training in righteousness (developing the habits and patterns of a godly life). Together these four functions address the whole person — mind, conscience, will, and character. Scripture is not just information; it is a tool of formation.
elements.questionHint: Paul says Scripture is useful for four specific things — teaching, reproof, correction, and training. Which of those four functions addresses knowledge, which addresses behaviour, and which addresses character? And which do you tend to receive from Scripture most readily?

elements.question: The word translated ’useful’ or ’profitable’ implies practical effectiveness – Scripture actually does something. What four things does Paul say it does, and how might each one specifically address the dynamics explored in this series?
elements.sampleAnswer: Teaching addresses the lies embedded in addictive thinking — that we deserve the reward, that the refuge is safe, that our sin is too great for forgiveness — by replacing them with truth. Reproof names the patterns for what they are, without minimising or excusing. Correction doesn’t just identify the problem but points toward a better path — the genuine refuge, the real reward, the sufficient atonement. Training in righteousness builds the habits and dispositions that make godly choices more natural over time, through repeated practice rather than single decisive acts. Each function targets a different layer of the dynamics this series has explored.
elements.questionHint: Each of the four functions Paul names addresses a different dimension of the human person — what you think, what you do wrong, how you get back on track, and how you grow in character. Which of the dynamics from this series do you think most needs each of those four?

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>And let us take thought of how to spur one another on to love and good works, <sup>25</sup>not abandoning our own meetings, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging each other, and even more so because you see the day drawing near.

elements.question: Why is it important not to neglect to meet together?
elements.sampleAnswer: Gathering together is the context for the mutual encouragement and spurring on that neither individual can provide for themselves. Without meeting together, we lose access to the encouragement of others, to the accountability that comes from being known, and to the sharpening effect of people who will provoke us toward love and good works. The writer adds an urgency — ‘all the more as you see the Day drawing near’ — which suggests that the stakes of neglecting this increase as time goes on, not decrease.
elements.questionHint: The instruction to meet together is tied to a purpose — ‘spurring one another on.’ What does it tell you about why gathering matters that it’s linked to something that can only happen in the presence of others?

elements.question: The language is active and mutual: we are to ’spur one another on.’ What does that suggest about what Christian community is meant to produce in you personally, and how does that differ from simply attending a service?
elements.sampleAnswer: The language of spurring on is active, deliberate, and directed at another person — it implies knowing someone well enough to know what they need to be encouraged toward. Attending a service is passive: you receive what is given. The community described here is one where people are actively invested in each other’s growth, where relationships are close enough for honest encouragement and provocation, and where the movement of care goes in both directions. For the person dealing with the dynamics this series has explored, being known and encouraged — and being challenged to grow — can only happen in the context of genuine community, not mere attendance.
elements.questionHint: You can attend a service without anyone knowing you are there, or what you are struggling with. Is that the kind of community Hebrews is describing? What would you need in order to be genuinely ’spurred on’ by another person?

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>For every time you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes.

elements.question: What is the significance of the bread and cup?
elements.sampleAnswer: The bread and cup are a proclamation — a public, embodied declaration of the Lord’s death. Each time the Lord’s Supper is received, the central fact of the gospel is announced again, not in words but in action. It is the gospel made physical, made regular, made communal. Paul’s framing — ‘every time you eat’ — emphasises its repetition: this is not a once-in-a-lifetime event but a recurring re-proclamation of the same foundational reality.
elements.questionHint: Paul describes the Lord’s Supper as a ’proclamation’ — an announcement, not just a ritual. Who is the proclamation addressed to, and what is being declared? And what might it mean to receive it as a word spoken to you personally?

elements.question: The Lord’s Supper is a proclamation of the Gospel, made in action rather than words, repeated regularly. What might it mean to receive it specifically as a declaration addressed to your own struggle – as a repeated, embodied reminder that the debt is paid and the life is given?
elements.sampleAnswer: To receive it as a declaration addressed to your own struggle would mean coming to the table not as a ritual observer but as someone who needs to hear the gospel again — specifically, in your body, in your hands, on your tongue. For the person trapped in shame, the bread is the body given for exactly the kind of person you are, before you improved. For the person trapped in self-punishment, the cup is the blood that paid the debt you’re still trying to pay yourself. Received this way, the Lord’s Supper becomes a regular, embodied interruption of the false narratives that addiction tells — a repeated physical proclamation that cuts through the shame cycle with the same truth every time.
elements.questionHint: The Lord’s Supper is received regularly — not once. Why might the gospel need to be proclaimed to you again and again in this embodied way, rather than being something you heard once and stored as information?

elements.text: Change is not a straight line. Progress is real but uneven. Relapse is common – not universal, but common enough that it should be named rather than whispered about. The response to relapse is not despair, and it is not self-atonement – we dealt with that in Chapter 7. It is repentance: the simple, repeated turning back. As many times as necessary. The father in Luke 15 does not keep a count of how many times the son has returned and left again. He simply runs, every time he sees him coming.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>Not that I have already attained this — that is, I have not already been perfected — but I strive to lay hold of that for which Christ Jesus also laid hold of me. <sup>13</sup>Brothers and sisters, I do not consider myself to have attained this. Instead I am single-minded: Forgetting the things that are behind and reaching out for the things that are ahead, <sup>14</sup>with this goal in mind, I strive toward the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus.

elements.question: What does Paul say about his ongoing experience of struggle?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says twice, with emphasis, that he has not attained, that he has not yet arrived. He explicitly refuses to present himself as having completed the journey. What he has instead is a direction and a motivation: he is pressing on, straining toward what lies ahead, forgetting what is behind, keeping his eyes on the goal. His experience of ongoing struggle does not disqualify him from pressing on — it is simply the normal condition of someone who is genuinely moving toward the prize rather than pretending they’ve already collected it.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’I have not yet attained this’ — twice. Why might an apostle need to say that twice, and what does his ongoing struggle tell you about whether arrival in this life is the right expectation?

elements.question: Paul – the apostle, the theologian of grace, the man who has written most of what we have been studying – has ’not yet arrived.’ He is pressing on. What does that honestly do to your expectations of what change looks like, and how quickly it comes?
elements.sampleAnswer: It should recalibrate expectations significantly. If Paul — who has experienced the reality of the risen Christ, written the most profound theological accounts of grace in human history, and planted churches across the Roman world — has not yet arrived, then the expectation that we should be done with this struggle after eight sessions or a year of serious effort is not a humble one. Paul’s ’not yet’ is not a note of despair — he is pressing on with energy and purpose. But it normalises the ongoing nature of the struggle and removes the false expectation that genuine faith should have produced completion by now.
elements.questionHint: If Paul’s ’not yet’ is the honest experience of the apostle, what does that tell you about the expectation that you should have arrived by now — and does knowing that Paul was still pressing on make your own ongoing struggle feel different?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>If we say we do not bear the guilt of sin, we are deceiving ourselves and the truth is not in us. <sup>9</sup>But if we confess our sins, he is faithful and righteous, forgiving us our sins and cleansing us from all unrighteousness.

elements.question: What happens when we confess our sins?
elements.sampleAnswer: When we confess, God forgives our sins and cleanses us from all unrighteousness. The basis for this is his faithfulness and righteousness — not our quality of confession or the depth of our remorse, but his character. The promise is total: all unrighteousness, not a selected portion of it. And the action is dual — forgiveness addresses the guilt of specific acts, and cleansing addresses the deeper pollution of unrighteousness. Both are given on confession.
elements.questionHint: John grounds the promise of forgiveness not in our sincerity or our quality of repentance, but in God’s faithfulness and righteousness. What does that grounding tell you about how secure the promise is, and what it depends on?

elements.question: What is notable about the tenses and the absence of any limit in John’s promise here? Is there a point at which this promise expires?
elements.sampleAnswer: John writes in the present tense and provides no limit — no maximum number of confessions, no point at which the faithfulness of God wears out, no clause that withdraws the promise if you’ve relapsed too many times. The condition is simply confession. The promise is simply forgiveness and cleansing. There is no footnote saying ’this offer expires after three returns’ or ’does not apply to repeat offenders.’ For the person in a cycle of relapse and shame, this is the word they most need to hear: the promise is still in force, as many times as necessary.
elements.questionHint: John says ’he is faithful’ — present tense, no qualifier. Is there any condition in the verse that would cause the promise to expire or become unavailable? What does the absence of any such condition tell you about what happens the fifth or the fifteenth time you need to confess the same thing?

elements.text: The goal is not sobriety. It is not freedom from addiction, as important as that is. It is not even happiness, though happiness is part of it. The goal is something far larger and more beautiful. Addiction is not just a problem to be solved. It is an obstacle to the life we were made for. The Gospel does not merely offer us freedom from something. It offers us freedom for something.

elements.passageText: <sup>29</sup>Because those whom he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, that his Son would be the firstborn among many brothers and sisters.

elements.question: What has God determined in advance for those he called?
elements.sampleAnswer: God has determined in advance that those he called would be conformed to the image of his Son — that they would come to resemble Jesus. This is the ultimate destination of the Christian life, and it was God’s intention from the beginning. It is also, crucially, something God himself has determined and is bringing about — not something we achieve or earn, but something we are being shaped toward by the one who foreknew us.
elements.questionHint: God’s predetermined destination for his people is not just that they avoid certain sins — it’s that they be ’conformed to the image of his Son.’ What is the difference between a goal of avoiding bad things and a goal of becoming like Jesus, and which is bigger?

elements.question: What does it mean to be ’conformed to the image of his Son’? What kind of person does that describe – and how does that vision compare with simply being free from the thing that has been holding you back?
elements.sampleAnswer: To be conformed to the image of Christ means to become, over time, the kind of person Jesus is — in character, in love, in relationship with the Father, in orientation toward others. It is not just moral improvement; it is ontological change — becoming a different kind of person. The person described is someone in whom love, joy, peace, patience, and faithfulness have become genuinely characteristic, rather than aspirational. Compared to simply being free from addiction, this is an incomparably larger and more beautiful goal. Freedom from addiction clears the obstacle; conformity to Christ is what the cleared path leads toward. The one is absence of a bad thing; the other is the fullness of the good thing.
elements.questionHint: Freedom from addiction is ’freedom from’ — the removal of an obstacle. Conformity to Christ is ’freedom for’ — a positive destination. How does having a destination rather than just an escape change the meaning and the motivation of the journey?

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>For I consider that our present sufferings cannot even be compared to the coming glory that will be revealed to us. <sup>19</sup>For the creation eagerly waits for the revelation of the sons of God. <sup>20</sup>For the creation was subjected to futility — not willingly but because of God who subjected it — in hope <sup>21</sup>that the creation itself will also be set free from the bondage of decay into the glorious freedom of God’s children.

elements.question: What awaits the creation – and us – at the end of the story?
elements.sampleAnswer: At the end of the story, creation itself is set free from the bondage of decay into the glorious freedom of God’s children, and the glory to be revealed to us is beyond comparison with present suffering. The scope is cosmic — not just individual redemption but the liberation of the entire created order. The direction of travel is from bondage and futility toward glory and freedom. Paul’s vision of the ending is not a thin survival but a radiant restoration — the ’glorious freedom’ that awaits both creation and the children of God.
elements.questionHint: Paul says present sufferings ’cannot even be compared’ to coming glory — not that they’re slightly outweighed, but that they’re in a different category altogether. What does that scale of disproportion tell you about the size of what is coming, and how does it change the way you hold present difficulty?

elements.question: The present sufferings – including the suffering of the struggle with addiction – are not ’worth comparing’ with what is coming. That is an extraordinary claim. Does it feel true to you? And if not – what would need to shift for it to feel true?
elements.sampleAnswer: For many people in the grip of present suffering, Paul’s claim feels remote rather than real — the glory is distant, the suffering is immediate. For it to feel true rather than merely known, something would need to shift in our vision: we would need the coming glory to become more vivid and more certain in our imagination than the present pain. This is not primarily an intellectual problem — it is a vision problem, a faith problem. The means of grace exist, in part, precisely to make this happen: to keep the reality of what is coming alive and luminous enough to relativise the weight of what is present. It takes time, and it takes the Spirit, and it is part of the same slow process of sanctification this chapter has been describing.
elements.questionHint: Paul’s claim feels more or less true depending on how vividly you can see what is coming, compared to how vividly you feel what is present. What might make the coming glory more real — more vivid, more certain — than it currently is for you?

elements.text: Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: The chapter opens with a person who has understood everything and still feels stuck. Do you recognise that experience? What does it feel like – and what has it led you to conclude about yourself, or about God?

Romans 12:1-2 describes transformation coming through the ’renewing of the mind’ – a process, not an event. What does that suggest about the realistic timeframe for change? How does that sit with you?

The chapter describes faith not as opposed to desire but as working through desire – satisfying our thirst for joy more deeply than sin can. Does that reframing of faith feel unfamiliar? How does it change the way you think about fighting temptation?
Which of the means of grace – Scripture, prayer, worship, the Lord’s Supper, Christian community, sabbath – do you most consistently neglect? What would it look like to invest more seriously in it?

The final goal described in Romans 8:29 is conformity to the image of Christ – not merely freedom from addiction. How does that larger vision change the way you think about your struggle? Does it make it feel more significant, or less?

chapters.chapterTitle: Walk in the Light: Community, Accountability & the Long Haul

elements.text:
Michael has been sober for 14 months. He knows, with the clarity that only those in recovery possess, that he didn’t get there alone.

There was the Tuesday night group – five men from his church who knew the real shape of his struggle, not the edited version. There was the friend who answered the phone at 11pm on a Thursday when the pull was almost irresistible. There was the pastor who didn’t flinch when Michael finally told him everything, and who said, simply: let’s bring this to Christ together. There were the ordinary practices – the daily reading, the weekly gathering, the deliberate restructuring of the evenings that used to be his most dangerous hours.

Michael would be the first to say that the theology mattered – that understanding why he was reaching for the bottle, and what God was offering instead, was not incidental to his recovery but central to it. But he would also say this: if he had tried to walk it out alone, he would not have walked it out.

Western individualism has thoroughly infected the Church. We believe in personal salvation, personal quiet times, personal sin management. We treat our struggles as private matters – to be dealt with between ourselves and God, and revealed to others only when we have achieved sufficient victory to make the telling safe.

The Bible expects a communal framework for the life of faith. The Christian life is not a solo project with occasional group check-ins. It’s a fundamentally shared life – a body, a family, a community of people who are being transformed together.

The person who tries to walk the road of recovery alone isn’t being strong. They’re being disobedient – refusing the form of life that God has designed for his people, precisely because he knows we can’t do this alone.

elements.passageText: <sup>25</sup>so that there may be no division in the body, but the members may have mutual concern for one another. <sup>26</sup>If one member suffers, everyone suffers together with it. If a member is honored, all rejoice together with it.

elements.question: What is the relationship between the parts of Christ’s body?
elements.sampleAnswer: The parts of Christ’s body are to have mutual concern for one another — no division, but a shared life in which suffering and honour are experienced together.
