When one member suffers, all suffer; when one is honoured, all rejoice. The relationship is one of organic interdependence: what happens to any part affects the whole. This is not a voluntary arrangement but the nature of being a body.
elements.questionHint: Paul describes two scenarios — one member suffering and one being honoured. In both cases, what does he say happens to the rest of the body, and what does that imply about whether any member can be isolated from the others?

elements.question: What does it mean, practically, that the members ’have equal concern for each other’ – that when one suffers, all suffer? How does that describe the community you currently belong to?
elements.sampleAnswer: Practically, it means that another person’s struggle is not their private business — it is something the whole body is involved in. True mutual concern means noticing when someone is absent, asking questions that go beyond ’fine’, and feeling something when a fellow member is hurting. The question of how this describes your current community is personal, but the honest answer will reveal whether what you belong to is a genuine body or a collection of individuals who happen to gather in the same room.
elements.questionHint: Think about the last time someone in your community was genuinely suffering — did others in the group feel it and respond to it, or did that person carry it alone? What does your answer reveal about whether the community is functioning as a body?

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>And let us take thought of how to spur one another on to love and good works, <sup>25</sup>not abandoning our own meetings, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging each other, and even more so because you see the day drawing near.

elements.question: What picture of Christian community are we given?
elements.sampleAnswer: The picture is of an active, intentional, mutually responsible community. Believers are to think carefully about how to spur one another on to love and good works — this requires presence and relationship. They are to keep meeting together, to encourage each other, and to do this with increasing urgency as the day of Christ’s return approaches. The community is not passive or incidental; it is purposeful and irreplaceable.
elements.questionHint: The writer says we should ’take thought’ of how to spur one another on — that phrase implies deliberate planning. What does that suggest about whether Christian community happens naturally or requires intentional effort?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • Is it something Christians do – and to whom?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
For many Protestants, confession means confessing directly to God in private prayer – and nothing more. The idea of confessing to another person may feel either Catholic (sacramental, requiring a priest) or simply unnecessary: God has forgiven me; why does anyone else need to know?

But James 5:16 is clear: ‘Confess your sins to one another and pray for one another so that you may be healed.’ The command is mutual and horizontal, not just vertical. It assumes that something happens in the presence of another human being that does not happen in private prayer alone.

This chapter argues that bringing our struggle into the light before another person is not just therapeutically useful – it is spiritually significant. It is an act of humility that breaks shame’s power, and it enacts the Gospel: we are fully known, and not rejected.

elements.question: Gathering is to be active and mutual: to ’spur one another on.’ What’s the difference between that and just attending a service? What would that difference look like in practice?
elements.sampleAnswer: Attending a service is largely passive and one-directional — you receive, but you don’t necessarily give anything to anyone. Spurring one another on requires knowing someone well enough to notice when they need encouragement, and caring enough to offer it. In practice, the difference looks like: asking specific questions rather than accepting vague answers; following up on something someone shared the previous week; initiating contact between gatherings; being willing to be known as well as to know others.
elements.questionHint: The text uses the word ’spur’ — which is an active, directional word. Can you spur someone on without knowing where they’re going or what’s making the journey hard for them? What does that imply about the depth of relationship the passage assumes?

elements.text:
Addiction thrives in the dark. The more the thing is hidden, the more it grows. Shame that drives us underground makes the addiction more necessary. And as long as the secret is kept, the cycle has no outside point of intervention.

Bringing our struggle into the light – telling another person, in specific terms, what we are actually dealing with – isn’t just therapeutically useful. It’s spiritually significant. It enacts the Gospel itself: we’re known, fully, and we’re not rejected.

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>But if we walk in the light as he himself is in the light, we have fellowship with one another and the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin.

elements.question: What is the effect of bringing things into the light?
elements.sampleAnswer: Two things happen when we walk in the light: we have genuine fellowship with one another, and the blood of Jesus cleanses us from all sin. Walking in the light is the condition for both real community and ongoing cleansing. The alternative — walking in darkness while claiming fellowship — is not just spiritually dishonest; it cuts off both the relationship and the cleansing that light makes possible.
elements.questionHint: John connects walking in the light with two distinct outcomes — one relational and one to do with cleansing. What are those two outcomes, and what does their being joined together suggest about the link between honesty before God and genuine community with others?

elements.question: This verse is primarily about our relationship with God, but the principle goes further. In your experience, what happens when something that’s been hidden is brought into the open?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is a personal reflection question — but the pattern John describes tends to hold broadly: things hidden grow larger in the dark. The secret becomes more powerful than the thing itself. When something is finally brought into the open before a trusted person, the most common experience is relief — not the judgment that was feared, but a kind of lightening. The thing that felt enormous in private often becomes more manageable when named and shared. Shame is broken not by resolution but by being known.
elements.questionHint: John says walking in the light produces fellowship — which suggests that hidden things damage community, not just personal integrity. What does your own experience tell you about what secrets do to relationships, and what happens when they’re brought into the open?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word in a church context?
  • Have you experienced it – and what was it like?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
In church culture, ‘accountability’ often means having someone who occasionally asks ’how are you doing?’ – and accepting ’fine’ as an answer. At its worst, it means surveillance: someone checking up on you to make sure you haven’t failed, with disappointment as the consequence if you have.

Neither of these is what this chapter means. True accountability involves a specific person who knows the real shape of the struggle (not a sanitised version), asks hard questions, hears honest answers, and responds to failure with ’let’s bring this to Christ’ rather than judgment.

The goal is not behavioural compliance. It is relationship – the experience of being fully known and not rejected. That is both a foretaste of the Gospel and a direct antidote to the false intimacy that addiction offers.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>So confess your sins to one another and pray for one another so that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person has great effectiveness.

elements.question: What practice belongs to genuine Christian community – and what does it produce?
elements.sampleAnswer: The practice commanded is mutual confession of sins to one another, accompanied by mutual prayer. What it produces is healing. The structure is reciprocal — ‘confess to one another and pray for one another’ — not a one-way transaction with a priest or leader. And the result is described in terms that go beyond forgiveness: healing, suggesting a restoration that is deeper than simply having guilt removed.
elements.questionHint: James uses the word ’healed’ for what mutual confession and prayer produce — not just ’forgiven’ or ’restored’. What does that word choice suggest about the depth of what happens when sin is confessed in the presence of another person?

elements.question: Why do you think confession to another person – not just to God – is part of what James prescribes? What does the presence of another human being add?
elements.sampleAnswer: Confession to God alone is essential — but James prescribes something additional, which suggests it does something different. The presence of another human being makes the confession concrete and embodied in a way that private prayer doesn’t: you can’t use vague language and get away with it; you can’t avoid the shame entirely; and you have to trust someone with what you’ve done. That vulnerability is itself significant — it breaks isolation, defeats shame, and enacts the Gospel in a tangible way: you are fully known by another person, and not rejected.
elements.questionHint: If confession to God were sufficient on its own, why would James add the instruction to confess to one another? What does the horizontal, human dimension add that the vertical, private dimension alone cannot provide?

elements.text:
True accountability isn’t just having someone who asks ’how are you doing?’ once a month and accepts ’fine’ as an answer. It involves: a specific, named person who knows the real shape of the struggle; regular honest reporting of failures and the heart-dynamics beneath them; permission to ask hard questions and the commitment to answer them honestly; prayer specific to the actual situation; and practical support around known triggers.

The goal of accountability is not surveillance. It is relationship – the experience of being fully known and not rejected. The right accountability partner is someone rooted in grace rather than moralism.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Brothers and sisters, if a person is discovered in some sin, you who are spiritual restore such a person in a spirit of gentleness. Pay close attention to yourselves, so that you are not tempted too. <sup>2</sup>Carry one another’s burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ.

elements.question: What is the value of another person in the work of restoration?
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage makes restoration an explicitly communal task — it’s not something the person in sin does alone, but something that requires others who are ’spiritual’ (that is, walking in the Spirit) to come alongside. The value of another person is: they can see what the person caught in sin cannot; they can carry the burden alongside them (verse 2); and they provide the embodied, relational experience of being helped rather than condemned. Paul’s language (‘restore’) implies bringing someone back to a right condition — a task that requires more than private resolve.
elements.questionHint: Paul says to ’restore’ someone caught in sin — and then immediately adds ’carry one another’s burdens.’ What does the burden-carrying image add to our understanding of what restoration actually involves?

elements.question: What does the word ’gently’ say about the spirit in which accountability should operate?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘Gently’ rules out harshness, moral superiority, and the kind of heavy-handed confrontation that compounds shame rather than relieving it. The goal of restoration cannot be achieved by crushing the person you’re trying to restore. Gentleness implies that the person offering accountability comes alongside rather than standing over — that they are primarily concerned with the other person’s healing, not with registering their own disapproval. The spirit of accountability should feel more like help than judgment.
elements.questionHint: Paul says restore ’in a spirit of gentleness’ — not ’with firmness’ or ’without compromise’. Why do you think he specifically chooses gentleness as the controlling spirit, rather than another quality like honesty or seriousness?

elements.question: And what does ’watch yourself’ add – what does it say about the posture of the person offering accountability?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘Watch yourself, or you also may be tempted’ demolishes any sense of moral superiority in the person offering accountability. It places both parties on the same level: neither is beyond the pull of temptation. This posture is essential to genuine accountability — it’s the difference between someone who comes alongside as a fellow traveller who knows the terrain and someone who looks down from a position of assumed strength. The warning produces humility, which is the right soil for genuine restoration.
elements.questionHint: Paul directs the warning ’watch yourself’ at the person doing the restoring, not the person being restored. What does that suggest about who is actually at risk in this encounter — and why that matters for the posture of the one offering help?

elements.text: Beyond one-to-one accountability, the wider life of the church community is itself a form of recovery. Regular worship redirects our affections toward what is real and lasting. The Lord’s Supper is an embodied declaration that the debt is paid. Serving others moves us out of the self-focused spiral that addiction creates. And genuine friendship – not just church attendance, but the sharing of ordinary life – fills some of the relational gap that addiction has occupied.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Two people are better than one, because they can reap more benefit from their labor. <sup>10</sup>For if they fall, one will help his companion up — but pity the person who falls down and has no one to help him up! <sup>11</sup>Furthermore, if two lie down together, they can keep each other warm, but how can one person keep warm by himself? <sup>12</sup>Although an assailant may overpower one person alone, two can withstand him. Moreover, a three-stranded cord is not quickly broken.

elements.question: What effect does genuine community have on our ability to bear difficulty?
elements.sampleAnswer: Genuine community multiplies the capacity to bear difficulty in several distinct ways: when one falls, the other helps them up; when one is cold, the other provides warmth; when one is attacked, the other provides strength to withstand. The writer doesn’t romanticise — he points to specific, practical scenarios of vulnerability, and shows that each of them is better managed together than alone. The person without community is explicitly described with pity: ‘pity the person who falls down and has no one to help him up.’
elements.questionHint: The writer gives three different scenarios — falling, being cold, being attacked — and in each case, the person alone is more vulnerable. What do these three scenarios have in common, and what does their variety suggest about how broadly the principle applies?

elements.question: The image of ’a cord of three strands’ suggests something stronger than the sum of its parts. What does community provide that individual faith, however strong, cannot?
elements.sampleAnswer: A cord of three strands is stronger than three separate strands added together — the strength comes from their being woven together, not simply from their individual qualities. Community provides: the perspective of others who can see what we can’t see about ourselves; the support that holds us up when our own faith is weakest; the accountability that gives sin a harder time when it thrives on secrecy; and the shared witness to truth that resists the distortions of isolation. Strong individual faith can be worn down alone in a way that the same faith, woven into community, cannot.
elements.questionHint: Notice that the image isn’t ’one very strong strand’ — it’s three strands woven together. What does the weaving suggest about how community’s strength works, compared to simply having one very determined individual?

elements.text: There is a further dimension of walking in the light that is sometimes resisted because it feels unspiritual – but it is, in fact, wisdom. Practical structures (internet filtering, not keeping alcohol in the house, changing dangerous routines) do not change the heart – only the Gospel does that. But they reduce the occasions on which the heart is tested before it has grown strong enough to withstand them. There is no virtue in unnecessary exposure.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Instead, put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh to arouse its desires.

elements.question: How should we handle situations that inflame sinful desires?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s instruction is to make no provision for the flesh — to give it no opportunity to arouse its desires. This is not passive avoidance but active, deliberate management: arranging life so that known triggers are removed or minimised. The positive side is ’putting on Christ’ — filling the space with something better. The practical implication is that wisdom in recovery involves environmental change: not simply resolving to be stronger in the same circumstances, but changing the circumstances.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’make no provision for the flesh’ — the word ’provision’ implies deliberate planning and preparation. What does that suggest about the level of intentionality required in managing situations that inflame sinful desires?

elements.question: The instruction is practical and specific: ‘do not think about how to gratify the desires of the flesh.’ What does that say about the role of deliberate environmental management – arranging our lives so that opportunity for the behaviour is reduced?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s instruction legitimises environmental management as a spiritual strategy — not a substitute for inward change, but a companion to it. The person who removes alcohol from the house, or installs an internet filter, or avoids certain places isn’t being weak; they’re being biblical. Making no provision for the flesh means removing the planning and preparation that the flesh relies on. This kind of deliberate restructuring of opportunity is explicitly what Paul has in mind.
elements.questionHint: If Paul is telling believers not to make provision for the flesh — that is, not to prepare or set up opportunities for it — what does that suggest about whether arranging our environment to reduce temptation is a sign of weakness or a form of obedience?

elements.text:
We need to speak directly about relapse – because silence on this point has caused enormous damage in Christian communities, where the expectation of clean, linear recovery has driven countless people into isolation and despair when they fail. Relapse is not the end of the story. It is evidence that you are in the middle of a battle – a battle whose outcome is certain but whose path is hard.

The right response to relapse isn’t despair or self-atonement. It’s repentance: the simple, repeated turning back. The person who confesses a relapse to their accountability partner and returns to the path isn’t a failure. They’re doing what the Christian life requires. The person to worry about is the one who stops telling anyone.

elements.passageText: <sup>20</sup>So he got up and went to his father. But while he was still a long way from home his father saw him, and his heart went out to him; he ran and hugged his son and kissed him.

elements.question: What does the father do when he sees his son returning – even after everything that has happened?
elements.sampleAnswer: The father sees his son while he is still a long way off — suggesting he’s been watching, waiting, hoping. He doesn’t wait for his son to arrive and make his case. His heart goes out to him immediately; he runs toward him; he hugs him and kisses him. The response is extravagant, urgent, and undeserved. Before a word of the rehearsed speech has been spoken, the welcome has already happened.
elements.questionHint: The father runs — which is undignified for a man of his status in the culture of the time. What does his running, before the son has even arrived or spoken, suggest about the posture with which the father has been waiting?

elements.question: What does the father’s running tell us about what repentance meets? Is there any indication that he is keeping a count?
elements.sampleAnswer: The father’s running tells us that repentance meets not reluctant acceptance, but eager, joyful welcome. There is no calculation here, no condition, no record of the son’s failures — only the sight of him returning and the father’s immediate, wholehearted response. Nothing in the text suggests the father is tallying how many times this has happened or setting a limit on how many times he’ll respond. The welcome is unreserved, and there is no indication that it would be different on a second or third return.
elements.questionHint: The text tells us what the father sees (his son returning) and what he immediately does (runs, hugs, kisses) — but it says nothing about what he’s thinking or calculating. What does that silence tell you about whether the father is keeping a count?

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>But if we confess our sins, he is faithful and righteous, forgiving us our sins and cleansing us from all unrighteousness.

elements.question: What happens when we confess?
elements.sampleAnswer: When we confess our sins, God forgives us our sins and cleanses us from all unrighteousness. Both things happen together: forgiveness (the removal of guilt) and cleansing (the removal of defilement). And John grounds this not in God’s mood or patience on any given occasion but in his character: he is ’faithful and righteous’ — the forgiveness is reliable because it flows from who God is, not from how we feel or how well we’ve confessed.
elements.questionHint: John says God forgives because he is ’faithful and righteous’ — not because he is kind or patient (though he is). What does grounding forgiveness in God’s faithfulness and righteousness rather than his mood tell you about the reliability of this promise?

elements.question: Is there a limit on this promise – a point at which it expires, or a condition beyond which it does not apply?
elements.sampleAnswer: The text sets no limit. The condition is confession — and the scope of the promise is ’all unrighteousness.’ There is no qualifying clause about how many times, how serious the sin, or how long the pattern has lasted. The promise holds because it is grounded in God’s faithfulness and righteousness — both of which are unlimited. The only condition is that we confess; the only limit on the promise is if we stop doing that.
elements.questionHint: Read the verse again carefully — does John add any qualification about the number of times this applies, or the severity of the sin it covers? What does the absence of any such qualification tell you?

elements.text: Deep patterns of addiction don’t usually dissolve quickly, even in the context of genuine faith and good community. This isn’t a reason for despair. It is a reason for patience – with ourselves, with others, and with the pace at which the Spirit works. Celebrate small victories. Measure progress in years, not weeks. Don’t mistake slow sanctification for no sanctification.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>For I consider that our present sufferings cannot even be compared to the coming glory that will be revealed to us.

elements.question: What is the relationship between present suffering and future glory?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says present sufferings cannot even be compared to the coming glory — not that they’re slightly outweighed by it, but that the comparison itself doesn’t hold. The glory is of a different and incomparably greater order than the suffering. This doesn’t make the suffering unreal or trivial, but it does mean that our current experience is not the final measure of what is true about our situation. The direction of travel — toward glory — is fixed.
elements.questionHint: Paul doesn’t say the coming glory outweighs the suffering — he says the two ’cannot even be compared.’ What does that language tell you about the relative scale of what’s coming versus what we’re currently experiencing?

elements.passageText: <sup>28</sup>And we know that all things work together for good for those who love God, who are called according to his purpose, <sup>29</sup>because those whom he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, that his Son would be the firstborn among many brothers and sisters.

elements.question: What is God working toward through the whole of our experience – including the hard parts?
elements.sampleAnswer: God is working toward conformity to the image of his Son — that is the destination named in verse 29 as the purpose behind verse 28’s ’all things working together for good.’ ’Good’ here is not comfort or the absence of difficulty but being made like Christ. That includes the hard parts: the struggles, the relapses, the slow progress. Nothing is outside God’s purposes; everything is being worked toward the specific and glorious end of Christlikeness.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’all things’ work together for good — and then verse 29 defines what that ’good’ is. What is the specific good God is working toward, and how does naming it change what we expect the hard parts of life to be doing?

elements.question: What difference does it make in the slow work of recovery to know that God is working toward a specific and glorious end – and that nothing, including our failures, is outside his purposes?
elements.sampleAnswer: It means that slow progress and even failure are not evidence that God’s purposes have been derailed. Recovery can feel like it’s going nowhere, or like every step forward is followed by a step back — and in that context, the temptation is to conclude that nothing is working. But if God is working all things toward a fixed end, then even the hard and slow seasons are inside his purposes, not outside them. That doesn’t make the difficulty disappear, but it gives the person in the middle of it a horizon that is secure beyond what they can currently see.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’all things’ work together for good — not ’most things’ or ’the good days.’ What does the inclusion of ’all things’ specifically mean for those days when recovery feels like it’s failing rather than progressing?

elements.text: The person who is still fighting – still showing up, still honest with their accountability partner, still bringing their failures to God rather than hiding them – isn’t losing. They are winning slowly.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Michael’s recovery depended on a community of specific people who knew the real shape of his struggle. Do you have someone who knows the truth? If not, what has prevented it?
Accountability is someone who knows the real shape of your struggle and asks hard questions. How does that compare with the accountability you currently have?
1 John 1:7 and James 5:16 both connect bringing things into the light with something being released or healed. Have you felt the relief of telling someone the truth? What happened?
Which accountability – ‘I’ll be disappointed’ or ’Let’s bring this to Christ together’ – have you experienced? What was it like?
The person who is still fighting – still showing up, still honest – is not losing. Does that feel true to you? Have you been measuring your progress by the wrong standard?

elements.text:
Emma came to the first session of this series because her small group leader suggested it, and she couldn’t think of a good reason to say no. She was not, she told herself, the kind of person this series was for. She didn’t have an addiction. She had a habit – a fairly manageable one, most of the time – and a vague, persistent sense that she was not quite living the life she was supposed to be living.

Ten sessions later, Emma would tell you that she was wrong about almost all of that. She did have an addiction – or something close enough to one that the distinction no longer felt important. She recognised herself, with uncomfortable precision, in the Rhythm chapter and the Rescue chapter and, to her considerable surprise, in the Revenge chapter as well. She understood, for the first time, what her habit had been offering her and why willpower alone had never been enough to resist it.

But the thing she would most want to tell you – the thing that surprised her most – is this: the series did not primarily make her feel worse about the thing she was doing. It made her feel better about God. It gave her a vision of him – as the true source of everything she had been looking for in the wrong places – that was bigger and more beautiful than the vision she had arrived with. And that vision, more than anything else, is what has begun to change her.

One of the subtle dangers in recovery is stopping too soon – treating freedom from addiction as the goal, and, once some freedom has been achieved, settling there. Freedom from addiction is good. It is worth fighting for, worth celebrating. But it is not the final destination. It is the clearing away of an obstacle so that the real journey can begin. Sobriety without transformation is an empty achievement in the end. The goal is not the absence of addiction. It is the presence of something better.

elements.passageText: <sup>29</sup>Because those whom he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, that his Son would be the firstborn among many brothers and sisters.

elements.question: What is the ultimate destination of everyone God has called?
elements.sampleAnswer: The ultimate destination of everyone God has called is conformity to the image of his Son — to be made like Christ. This is not a secondary or optional destination but the predetermined purpose God is working toward in all those he has foreknown. The goal is not mere freedom from sin, but positive transformation into Christlikeness.
elements.questionHint: Paul says God ’predestined’ believers to a specific destination — what is it, and how does naming Christ’s image as the goal change what we understand the whole Christian life to be moving toward?

elements.question: What does it mean to be ’conformed to the image of his Son’? What kind of person does that describe – and how does it compare with simply being free from the thing that has been holding you back?
elements.sampleAnswer: To be conformed to the image of Christ is to be shaped into the kind of person he is — characterized by love, humility, compassion, obedience, self-giving, and joy in the Father. It is a thoroughly positive vision, not merely the removal of what was wrong. Freedom from addiction is a clearing away — removing an obstacle. Conformity to Christ’s image is the destination: a fully flourishing life shaped by who he is. The difference is between an empty house and a furnished home.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between a description that says what you’ve been freed from and one that says what you’re being made into. Which is the more compelling vision — and which does Paul set as the goal here?

elements.text: What does that better thing look like? The New Testament is not vague about this. It is a life of growing beauty – the life of a person being remade, slowly, in the image of the Son of God.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>Therefore, as the elect of God, holy and dearly loved, clothe yourselves with a heart of mercy, kindness, humility, gentleness, and patience, <sup>13</sup>bearing with one another and forgiving one another, if someone happens to have a complaint against anyone else. Just as the Lord has forgiven you, so you also forgive others. <sup>14</sup>And to all these virtues add love, which is the perfect bond.

elements.question: What does this life look like in practice?
elements.sampleAnswer: In practice, it looks like mercy, kindness, humility, gentleness, and patience — not as occasional impulses but as a settled character, deliberately ’put on’ like clothing. It looks like bearing with others when they’re difficult, forgiving as one who has been forgiven. And over all of this, love — described as the bond that holds the rest together. The image is of a person whose interior life is being shaped into something beautiful, with visible relational effects.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses the image of clothing — ‘clothe yourselves’ — for these qualities. What does that image suggest about whether this character is passive or active, natural or intentionally adopted?

elements.question: How does that vision compare with the person you are now? How does it compare with the person your addiction was making you?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is a personal reflection question, but the pattern is consistent: addiction tends to produce the opposite of every quality Paul lists. It makes people self-absorbed rather than merciful, impatient rather than patient, harsh rather than gentle, unable to forgive because they’re consumed by their own need. The contrast between the person addiction was making you and the person Paul describes here is itself a form of motivation — not guilt, but a compelling vision of what you were made for and what you’re being restored toward.
elements.questionHint: Look at the list Paul gives — mercy, kindness, humility, gentleness, patience, forgiveness — and then think about what addiction tends to produce in a person over time. What does the contrast tell you about what you were made for?

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people of his own, so that you may proclaim the virtues of the one who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light. <sup>10</sup>You once were not a people, but now you are God’s people. You were shown no mercy, but now you have received mercy.

elements.question: What is the identity and calling of those who belong to Christ?
elements.sampleAnswer: Those who belong to Christ are: a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people of his own. Each of these describes a collective identity — they belong to one another as they belong to God. The calling that flows from this identity is to proclaim the virtues of the one who called them out of darkness into marvellous light. The identity is gift; the calling is witness.
elements.questionHint: Peter gives four distinct identity descriptions, each of them collective rather than individual. What does the plural, corporate nature of all four suggest about whether Christian identity can be fully held or expressed in isolation?

elements.question: How does that identity sit alongside the identity that addiction tried to give you?
elements.sampleAnswer: Addiction offers an identity built around the substance or behaviour — ‘I’m someone who needs this’, ‘I’m someone who can’t stop’, ‘I’m someone who has this secret.’ It’s an identity shaped by compulsion, shame, and diminishment. Peter’s description is its opposite: chosen, royal, holy, God’s own — an identity shaped by belonging, dignity, and purpose. The contrast isn’t just motivational; it’s theological. The question is which identity is actually true, and which one we’re living from.
elements.questionHint: Peter says ’you once were not a people, but now you are God’s people’ — the identity has changed. What does that ’now’ imply about the identity addiction offered, and which one has the stronger claim on who you actually are?

elements.text:
Throughout this series we have seen that God offers ’more’ than addiction – more truly, more lastingly, more freely. But it’s worth asking ourselves: do I see and feel that God is more joyful than sin? Not in theory. In experience.

C.S. Lewis wrote that our problem is not that we desire too much, but that we desire too little – that we are far too easily pleased, settling for the cheap version when the real thing is available. The person caught in addiction is not someone whose desires are too strong. They are someone whose vision is too small. The appetite is right; the object is wrong.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>You make known to me the path of life; you will fill me with joy in your presence, with eternal pleasures at your right hand.

elements.question: What does it say about the joy available in God’s presence?
elements.sampleAnswer: The psalm says that in God’s presence there is fullness of joy — not partial, not conditional, but full. And at his right hand are eternal pleasures. The joy is real and experiential (‘fill me with joy’), not merely doctrinal. And it is in his presence — which means the joy is relational, not transactional. It comes from being with God, not from receiving something from him at arm’s length.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist says ’fill me with joy’ — not ’give me some joy’ or ’remind me that joy is available.’ What does the word ’fill’ suggest about the quality and quantity of what God’s presence offers?

elements.question: What does it mean that these pleasures are described as ’eternal’? Not just lasting longer than sin’s pleasures – but of an entirely different order, sourced in the inexhaustible life of God?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘Eternal’ doesn’t just mean ’a very long time’ — it means sourced in and sustained by the life of God himself, which is inexhaustible. Sin’s pleasures diminish and require escalation; they borrow against something that runs out. God’s pleasures are eternal because God doesn’t run out. They are of a qualitatively different order — not just more of the same thing, but pleasures that flow from infinite being. This is the comparison that makes addiction’s offer look not just inferior but simply small.
elements.questionHint: Compare ’eternal pleasures’ with what addiction offers — which requires increasing doses to maintain the same effect. What does the ’eternal’ quality of God’s pleasures imply about whether they diminish over time or can be exhausted?

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>The thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy; I have come so that they may have life, and may have it abundantly.

elements.question: What does Jesus say he came to bring?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says he came to bring life — and not merely life, but life abundantly. The contrast is with the thief, whose agenda is to steal, kill, and destroy. Jesus offers the opposite of that: life in its fullest expression. ’Abundantly’ translates a Greek word that implies excess, overflowing, more than sufficient — a life that surpasses what is merely adequate.
elements.questionHint: Jesus doesn’t just say ’life’ — he says life ’abundantly.’ What does the word ’abundantly’ add to the promise, and how does it sit alongside the contrast with the thief who ’steals, kills, and destroys’?

elements.question: How does that description of the life Jesus offers compare with the life that addiction promises – and delivers?
elements.sampleAnswer: Addiction promises life — relief, pleasure, escape, connection — but delivers the thief’s agenda: it steals (time, relationships, health, integrity), kills (slowly, in various ways), and destroys (what is built, what is loved). The language Jesus uses for the thief is a precise description of what addiction actually does, beneath its promises. The contrast is not between two similar things but between the real thing and a counterfeit — one that always takes more than it gives.
elements.questionHint: Jesus describes the thief’s work as stealing, killing, and destroying — three active, destructive verbs. Looking back honestly at what addiction has done, how many of those three words apply to what it has taken from you?

elements.question: What would it look like to take C.S. Lewis’s diagnosis seriously in your own life – to treat your desire for joy not as a problem to be managed but as a capacity to be filled with something worthy of it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Taking Lewis’s diagnosis seriously means reframing the desire itself as good — not a weakness to be suppressed, but a capacity that was made for something larger. In practice, it means asking: what am I actually longing for when I reach for the behaviour? And then asking: where is the real version of that? It means investing in the practices that actually fill the capacity — worship, community, prayer, beauty, service — rather than simply trying to starve the desire. The goal is not a smaller appetite, but a better object.
elements.questionHint: Lewis says we’re ’too easily pleased’ — meaning we settle for a small thing when a larger one is available. What would it mean to take your desire for joy seriously enough to refuse to settle for the small thing — and actively pursue the larger one instead?

elements.question:
• Have you heard this phrase before?
  • What do you think it means for the Christian life?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The ’already and not yet’ is theological shorthand for a real tension that every Christian lives in. Christ has come. Sin’s power has been broken. The Spirit has been given. The Kingdom of God has genuinely arrived. That’s the ’already.’

And yet: we still struggle. We still suffer. We still sin. The Kingdom is not yet fully here. Creation itself is still groaning. That’s the ’not yet.’

Both are true simultaneously – and getting them wrong in either direction causes real damage. Overemphasising the ’already’ produces unrealistic expectations and despair when change is slow. Overemphasising the ’not yet’ produces passivity and an excuse for not engaging seriously with growth. The Christian life is lived in the tension between the two.

elements.text:
We need to be clear about a tension that will not be resolved this side of eternity. The Kingdom of God has come in Christ. Sin’s power has been broken. The Spirit has been given. New creation has begun. These are accomplished facts, and they are the ground of genuine hope and genuine change.

And yet the Kingdom is not fully here. We still struggle. We still suffer. The ’already’ gives us real resources and real victories. The ’not yet’ explains why the struggle is still hard, and why patience is not optional.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>For I consider that our present sufferings cannot even be compared to the coming glory that will be revealed to us.

elements.question: What does Paul say about the relationship between present suffering and future glory?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says the present sufferings cannot even be compared to the coming glory — not that they’re outweighed, but that the comparison itself doesn’t hold. The glory is of such a different and greater order that the scale doesn’t match. This doesn’t minimise suffering; Paul doesn’t pretend it’s small. But it does insist that suffering is not the final word, and that what is coming is incomparably greater than what is currently being endured.
elements.questionHint: Paul says present sufferings ’cannot even be compared’ to future glory — not that glory is bigger, but that the comparison doesn’t work. What does the breakdown of the comparison tell you about the relative scale of what’s coming?

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>For the creation eagerly waits for the revelation of the sons of God. <sup>20</sup>For the creation was subjected to futility — not willingly but because of God who subjected it — in hope <sup>21</sup>that the creation itself will also be set free from the bondage of decay into the glorious freedom of God’s children.

elements.question: And what does the whole creation’s groaning tell us about the nature of the hope we are waiting for?
elements.sampleAnswer: Creation’s groaning tells us that the hope is cosmic in scale — not just personal improvement but the liberation of the whole created order from bondage to decay. The freedom coming is described as ’glorious’ and as belonging to God’s children. The hope we’re waiting for is nothing less than the full renewal of everything that has been broken — a comprehensive restoration that makes the current struggle part of a much larger story moving toward a definite and magnificent end.
elements.questionHint: Paul says creation itself is waiting and groaning — not just individual believers. What does the cosmic scale of the waiting tell you about the scale of what the hope is for?

elements.question: What does it mean that even creation is caught in this tension – between what is and what is coming? How does that reframe the experience of ongoing struggle?
elements.sampleAnswer: It means that the experience of tension between what is and what is coming is not a malfunction — it’s the condition of the whole created order until the end. Creation itself groans in anticipation. This reframes ongoing struggle: rather than evidence that something has gone wrong with your faith, it’s evidence that you’re living in the same world that creation itself is groaning through. The struggle is real, but it has a direction and a destination — and you’re not outside that story, you’re inside it.
elements.questionHint: If even creation is in a state of groaning and waiting, what does that suggest about whether the struggle you experience in the ’not yet’ is evidence of personal spiritual failure — or simply evidence that you’re living in the same tension the whole created order is living in?

elements.text:
A Word to Those Still in the Middle

There will be some who have reached this final session still feeling stuck. You are not a failure. You are not evidence that the Gospel does not work. You are in the middle of a story whose end is certain but whose path is hard – and the fact that you are still engaging, and still wanting to be free, is itself evidence of the Spirit’s work in you. Do not despise that.

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>For I am sure of this very thing, that the one who began a good work in you will perfect it until the day of Christ Jesus.

elements.question: How confident can we be that God will complete what he began?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says he is ’sure of this very thing’ — not hopeful, not optimistic, but certain. The confidence is grounded entirely in God’s character and commitment: the one who began the work will perfect it. The guarantee is not our faithfulness but God’s — he is the one who started it, and his starting it is the assurance that he will finish it. The day of Christ Jesus is named as the completion point, meaning the work is ongoing until then — but its completion is not in question.
elements.questionHint: Paul says he is ’sure’ — a very strong word. What is the basis of his certainty — is it the Philippians’ track record, or something about God? And what difference does that basis make to how secure the confidence is?

elements.question: What is the basis of that confidence – is it our faithfulness, or God’s?
elements.sampleAnswer: The basis is entirely God’s faithfulness, not ours. Paul doesn’t say ’I’m confident because of how you’re doing’ — he says ’the one who began a good work in you will perfect it.’ The guarantee is located in God: his having started the work is the reason to be confident it will be finished. Our faithfulness matters and is expected — but it is not the ground of the confidence. That ground is God himself.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’the one who began a good work in you will perfect it’ — not ’you who have begun a good work will perfect it.’ Who is the subject of the sentence, and why does that matter enormously for someone whose own faithfulness feels unreliable?

elements.text:
A Word to Those Walking in Freedom

There will also be some who have found genuine freedom – whether recently won or long established. Receive it with gratitude. This is a gift, not an achievement. Hold it with vigilance. Freedom is not immunity. And offer it with generosity – you now have something to give. The person who has been freed from addiction is not simply a beneficiary of the Gospel. They are equipped to be one of its most compelling witnesses.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>Blessed is the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, <sup>4</sup>who comforts us in all our troubles so that we may be able to comfort those experiencing any trouble with the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God.

elements.question: What are we equipped to offer to others, precisely because of what we have received?
elements.sampleAnswer: We are equipped to offer comfort — the same comfort with which we ourselves have been comforted by God. The logic is circular in the best way: what we have received, we are now able to give. This means that suffering and struggle, rather than simply being liabilities, become the source of our usefulness to others. The person who has been comforted by God through difficulty has something specific and credible to offer someone in the same difficulty — not theory, but the lived comfort of God.
elements.questionHint: Paul says we comfort others ’with the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God.’ What does the specific source of the comfort we offer — God’s own comfort, passed through our experience — add to what we’re able to give others that generic advice cannot provide?

elements.question: What does it tell us about God’s purposes that he takes our suffering – including the suffering of addiction and recovery – and makes it useful for others?
elements.sampleAnswer: It tells us that God’s purposes are redemptive all the way down — nothing is wasted, including the worst of what we’ve been through. The suffering of addiction and recovery is not simply a problem to be overcome and then left behind. It becomes, in God’s economy, the material from which a specific kind of ministry is made. This is itself a profound comfort: the thing that felt like nothing but loss turns out to have been preparing you to serve someone else in exactly that kind of loss.
elements.questionHint: Paul says God comforts us ’so that’ we can comfort others — there’s a purpose in the receiving of comfort. What does the phrase ’so that’ tell you about whether suffering in God’s economy is ever purposeless?

elements.text:
Coming Home

The title of this series – Find Rest for your Soul – comes from one of the most familiar and most personal of all Jesus’ invitations.

elements.passageText: <sup>28</sup>"Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. <sup>29</sup>Take my yoke on you and learn from me, because I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. <sup>30</sup>For my yoke is easy to bear, and my load is not hard to carry."

elements.question: What does Jesus promise to those who come to him?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus promises rest — specifically, rest for the soul. He offers this to those who are weary and burdened: not the strong, but those who are worn out from carrying what they’re carrying. He describes himself as gentle and humble in heart — which means the rest he offers comes through relationship with someone who won’t add to the burden. And the yoke he gives is described as easy, his load as not hard to carry.
elements.questionHint: Jesus addresses ’all you who are weary and burdened’ — he doesn’t say ’all you who have got things sorted.’ What does his choice of audience tell you about whether you need to arrive in a better condition before the invitation applies to you?

elements.question: Jesus speaks of rest – but not the rest of inactivity or escape. What is the difference between the rest he offers and the escape that addiction provides?
elements.sampleAnswer: Addiction’s escape is a temporary exit from reality — it removes you from what is hard without actually changing it. When the escape ends, the burden is still there, and often heavier. Jesus’ rest is different: it’s relational, not chemical; it comes through taking on his yoke, not dropping all burdens; and it’s specifically rest for the soul — the deepest kind. It doesn’t remove you from life; it gives you a different way to carry it, because you’re carrying it with him and under his gentleness. The burden doesn’t disappear, but its weight changes.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’take my yoke on you’ — a yoke is not the absence of work, it’s a way of sharing a load. How does that image of shared labour differ from addiction’s promise of escape, and what does it imply about how Jesus’ rest actually works?

elements.text:
Throughout this series, we have seen what that rest looks like from many different angles. It is the rest of the person who no longer needs to perform for approval, because they know they are justified. It is the rest of the person who no longer needs to medicate their pain, because they have found a God who is present in it. It is the rest of the person who no longer needs to punish themselves, because the Great High Priest has sat down. It is the rest of the person who has stopped fleeing into private worlds of fantasy and escape, and has found, in the community of God’s people and in the presence of God himself, somewhere better to be.

This is what Jesus is offering. Not a programme. Not a set of principles. Not a better coping strategy. He is offering himself – and the rest that comes from knowing him.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Emma arrived at the series thinking it wasn’t for her, and left with a bigger vision of God than she had arrived with. Looking back over the series, which session or insight has most shifted your understanding of your struggle, or of God?

Don’t stop with freedom from addiction as the goal; point toward conformity to Christ as the true destination. Does that larger goal feel motivating or overwhelming? What would it mean to hold it as a genuine horizon?

C.S. Lewis says our problem is not that we desire too much but that we desire too little – that we are too easily pleased. Does that diagnosis feel accurate to you? What would it look like to take your desire for joy seriously, and direct it toward its true object?
The chapter speaks to two groups – those still in the middle, and those walking in freedom. Which group do you identify with today? And what does the chapter say specifically to you?

Matthew 11:28-30 speaks of rest for the soul. Looking back over the whole series – the false promises, the true promises, the theological foundations, the practical realities – what would it mean, personally for you, to find that rest?

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Find Rest for your Soul
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A ten-session Bible study exploring the root of addictive behavior – and finding a better joy.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any chapter.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ‘How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: People who recognise a pattern they can’t break
blocks.body: If there is something you keep returning to — something you’ve tried to stop, perhaps many times, with complete sincerity — this study is written with you in mind. It doesn’t assume you’ve hit rock bottom, and it doesn’t require you to use the word ’addiction’ about yourself. It simply takes seriously the experience of wanting to change and finding that wanting isn’t enough.

blocks.title: People who don’t think this applies to them
blocks.body: You may have opened this study for someone else’s sake — a friend, a family member, someone you’re walking alongside. But the framework this study uses is broader than the stereotypes. It describes a heart that reaches for the wrong things to meet real needs. By that account, the pattern is far more universal than any clinical definition suggests. Give it a fair hearing.

blocks.title: Group leaders, pastors, and mentors
blocks.body: If you are leading others through this material — in a small group, a one-to-one setting, or a pastoral context — the Leaders’ Notes at the end of each chapter are written specifically for you. They cover key teaching points, pastoral sensitivities, and things to watch for in group dynamics. Work through each chapter personally before you lead it.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Work through each session at your own pace, read the Bible passages carefully, write your answers to the questions, and give the reflection questions at the end real time. There is no right speed — some sessions may move quickly, others may stop you in your tracks. That’s the point.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study also works well in a small group setting — though the subject matter calls for a group culture of genuine safety and honesty. The most rewarding approach is usually for each person to work through the chapter individually first, write their answers privately, and then come together to discuss. This means the conversation starts from a place of real reflection rather than first impressions.

blocks.title: A note on honesty
blocks.body: This material deals with things people rarely say out loud. Whether you are working alone or in a group, you will get most from it by engaging honestly — not performing the right answers, but writing what you actually think and feel. The questions are designed to prompt genuine reflection, not to test your theological knowledge.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
It can make a real difference to have someone walking alongside you through this study — a group leader, a pastor, or a trusted friend who is further along. Perhaps they are the one who recommended it to you. The subject matter is serious, and having someone who knows your situation can help you apply what you are learning rather than keeping it safely abstract.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for a much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each chapter has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions are tied to specific passages — asking you to look carefully at what the text actually says. The reflection questions at the end are more personal — asking what the chapter means for your own situation specifically. Both matter. The Bible questions build the foundation; the reflection questions are where the study does its real work.

blocks.title: Common Misunderstanding boxes
blocks.body: Throughout the study you will encounter darker-coloured cards headed Common Misunderstanding. These address words — like addiction, idolatry, or willpower — that are often misunderstood in ways that affect how we diagnose the problem and look for a solution. Think of your own answer first, then tap Find out to see a short clarification.

blocks.title: The opening illustrations
blocks.body: Each chapter begins with a short story — a named person in a recognisable situation. These are composite illustrations, not real individuals. They are designed to make the dynamics of each chapter concrete and human before the theological analysis begins. You may find yourself in some of them more than others.

intro: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter – both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have worked through the questions personally before they help others doing the same.

Create a safe space for honesty. The questions in this study are designed to prompt real reflection, not just correct answers. Your job is to make the group feel safe enough to say what they actually think, not what they think a Christian is supposed to say.
Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.

chapters.title: The Heart Behind Addiction
chapters.keyPoints:
The session establishes the worship framework as the organising lens for the whole series. Addictive behaviour is not primarily a habit or willpower problem; it is a worship problem – something has taken God’s place at the centre of the heart.

Draw out the contrast between conventional explanations (triggers, biology, history, the devil) and the biblical account (James 1:13-14, Mark 7:21-23). Each contains truth but locates the problem outside the self.

Introduce the false promises framework: addictions work by making real promises about real needs. The needs are God-given; the problem is where we go to meet them. This thread runs through all ten sessions.
chapters.pastorals:
Some in the group may be carrying active addiction. The opening illustration (David) is designed to be broadly recognisable without triggering premature confession. Don’t push for disclosure in a first session.

The worship framework can feel confronting to people who have been using therapeutic or medical language about their struggle. Honour the truth in those frameworks while showing why the biblical account goes deeper.

The ‘there is hope’ closing section is important. Don’t end the session on the diagnosis without pointing forward to the promise.
chapters.watch: Watch for people who recognise the pattern intellectually but are reluctant to apply it personally. The individual reflection exercise is designed for this – encourage people to take it seriously.

chapters.title: Respect & Rescue: ‘You are Enough’
chapters.keyPoints:
Two versions of the same hunger: the Respect dynamic (drivenness, performance, fear of being found inadequate) and the Rescue dynamic (searching for unconditional acceptance). The gospel answers with justification (a permanent, not-earned verdict) and adoption (welcomed as a child, not merely acquitted).

The fear of the Lord section is important and often missed: the antidote to the fear of man is not the absence of all fear, but a better and more liberating one. When God’s verdict is the one that counts – and that verdict is entirely favourable in Christ – the tyranny of others’ opinions loses its grip.
chapters.pastorals:
Sarah’s story (busyness as addiction) will land with high-achieving people who do not think of themselves as having an addiction. Be ready for a moment of recognition and the discomfort that follows.

The Rescue dynamic resonates most with people carrying deep shame. The language of counterfeit grace – ‘receives us as we are and leaves us exactly as we are’ – is important for them. Handle gently.
chapters.watch: Watch for the elder-brother type – the person who is clearly performing for approval but cannot yet see it. The Luke 15 prodigal story, used later in the series, will be important for them.

chapters.title: Relationship: ‘You are Wanted’
chapters.keyPoints:
The longing for intimacy is God-given and good. The problem is seeking it in places that simulate intimacy without delivering it – and that make the isolation worse.

The idol of control section is key: many addictive behaviours offer relational experience without vulnerability – intimacy on entirely safe terms. The gospel answer: a relationship in which we are fully known and fully loved (Romans 8:38-39), embodied and extended in the community of the Church (James 5:16).
chapters.pastorals:
Be careful not to let the discussion narrow to pornography – the relationship dynamic operates across many different behaviours.

James 5:16 (confession to another person) may challenge people who have never practised this. Don’t push for premature disclosure, but take time on why confessing to another person matters – not as a mechanism of forgiveness but as a healing act of humility.
chapters.watch: Watch for people who find the idea of being ‘fully known’ terrifying rather than comforting. That terror is itself data about where the addiction is operating. Leave space for it.

chapters.title: Relief & Rhythm
chapters.keyPoints:
Relief: addiction as anaesthetic for pain. The key move is from escape to lament. Both turn away from pressure, but escape turns away from God too; lament turns toward God in the pressure. The Psalms model lament, not escape.

Rhythm: addiction as structure and identity for people whose lives lack purpose or belonging. Removing the behaviour without replacing the rhythm leaves a vacuum. The gospel offers a new identity (1 Peter 2:9-10) and a new rhythm through the practices of the Christian life.
chapters.pastorals:
Anne’s story involves bereavement. Be sensitive to anyone carrying grief – the anaesthetic dynamic will be very close to home, and they may feel judged rather than helped. Make clear this material is not designed to add guilt to grief.

The lament material may be unfamiliar to many evangelicals. You may need to explain and model it.
chapters.watch: Watch for people carrying pain they have never brought to God – who have been using behaviour to manage it instead. This session may open a significant conversation. Follow up individually.

chapters.title: Refuge: ‘You are Safe Here’
chapters.keyPoints:
The longing for refuge is legitimate – God commands sabbath and Jesus himself sought regular withdrawal. The problem is seeking refuge in a place that offers the feeling of safety without its reality.

Key distinction: escape (turns away from pressure and from God) vs genuine rest (turns toward God in the pressure, casting the weight rather than hiding from it). Recovery requires building real sabbath, not just removing the escape.
chapters.pastorals:
The chapter names people in high-responsibility roles as particularly vulnerable – this may apply to leaders in your group. Handle with self-awareness.

Be alert to people redirecting to ‘worse’ addictions to avoid examining their own socially acceptable escapes (TV, reading, social media).
chapters.watch: Watch for people who have never genuinely rested – always busy, always productive. For them the invitation to sabbath may feel threatening. It may be the most important thing in this session for them.

chapters.title: Reward: ‘You Deserve This’
chapters.keyPoints:
Two versions: entitlement (I’ve earned this) and boredom (easier pleasure is more immediately accessible). Both reflect a failure of faith – a vision of God too small to compete with the counterfeit.

The Moses example (Hebrews 11:24-26) is the heart of the session. Moses does not suppress desire; he redirects it toward a truer and more lasting object. Faith does not work against desire; it satisfies it more deeply.
chapters.pastorals:
The entitlement dynamic can feel accusatory. James and Claire are designed to be sympathetic – the question is not whether the reward seems reasonable but whether it is real.

Watch for quiet resentment at God from those who have made genuine sacrifices and feel the return has not come. The elder-brother dynamic begins here.
chapters.watch: Watch for people who find Psalm 16:11 (eternal pleasures at God’s right hand) unconvincing or distant. That gap is exactly where the false promise still holds power. Name it honestly.

chapters.title: Revenge & Redemption
chapters.keyPoints:
Revenge: addiction as protest, as taking back what feels owed, as acting out a grievance. The elder brother (Luke 15:29-32) was standing outside a feast always open to him, blind to the vastness of what he already had.

Penance: addiction as self-atonement, because ordinary forgiveness feels insufficient. Hebrews 10:12 is the answer: the priest sat down. The work is finished. There is nothing to add. Self-atonement, however soaked in shame, contains a hidden pride – it refuses the sufficiency of Christ’s work.

The guilt/shame distinction: guilt is resolved by forgiveness; shame requires a deeper word – not merely acquittal but adoption.
chapters.pastorals:
The Revenge dynamic may surface real anger at God – driven underground because it felt dangerous to name. The Psalms give permission. Create space.

Rachel’s story (using a hated behaviour as self-punishment) is not uncommon but rarely discussed. Some may recognise themselves. Significant pastoral territory – follow up individually.
chapters.watch: Watch for the person who has never been able to receive forgiveness – for whom the Gospel feels true for others but not for them. They need the priest-has-sat-down truth most urgently.

chapters.title: The Gospel Offers More: Foundations for Change
chapters.keyPoints:
Why doesn’t understanding the framework automatically produce change? Because the will follows desire, and desire is not changed by argument alone.

Three foundations: (1) What the Gospel actually is – not thin forgiveness but reconciliation, adoption, union with Christ. (2) Faith as the mechanism – working through desire, not against it; satisfying the thirst for joy more deeply than sin can. (3) The Holy Spirit as agent of transformation, working through the means of grace.

The non-linearity of change must be named honestly: relapse is common, progress is uneven, the response to relapse is repentance not despair.
chapters.pastorals:
The opening scenario (understanding everything, still stuck) creates identification before the teaching. Many will recognise themselves. This session is not designed to add guilt to frustration but to give a more accurate diagnosis.

People who have been working hard at the means of grace and still feel stuck need honesty about the timeframe and encouragement about what God is doing unseen.
chapters.watch: Watch for those using ‘results are God’s’ as permission for passivity. The means-of-grace section directly addresses this – use it.

chapters.title: Walk in the Light: Community, Accountability, & the Long Haul
chapters.keyPoints:
Theological foundations must become concrete structures. Accountability defined specifically: a named person who knows the real shape of the struggle, asks hard questions, and responds to failure with ‘let’s bring this to Christ’. Moralistic accountability drives behaviour underground; grace-based accountability creates safety for honesty.

Practical structures (filters, environmental management, restructuring dangerous hours) are wisdom, not spiritual weakness. There is no virtue in unnecessary exposure.
chapters.pastorals:
Some may have no one who knows the truth. Be ready for people to take a step during or after this session. Follow up.

The relapse material needs honesty: silence has driven many into isolation and despair. The Gospel’s answer is repentance and return, not despair and self-atonement.
chapters.watch: Watch for those taking ‘results are God’s’ as a reason for passivity about their discipleship structures. The session directly addresses this – use it.

chapters.title: Freed by a Better Joy
chapters.keyPoints: A gathering-up and a sending-out. The central claim: there is a joy so full, so true, and so lasting that the counterfeits eventually lose not just their power but their appeal. The question pressed is not ‘have you stopped?’ but ‘what are you for?’ Freedom from is always in service of freedom for.
chapters.pastorals:
Some will end this series more free than when they started. Others will feel they have understood much but changed little. Both need this session, but for different reasons: the first need vision; the second need patience and permission to press on.

End with genuine warmth. Offer to follow up individually with anyone for whom the series has opened something significant.
chapters.watch: Watch for anyone ending the series more aware of their struggle but without clear next steps. Make sure they leave with a specific person to talk to.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).

studyAiData.aiMentorPrompt: You are a warm, thoughtful mentor for a Bible study series called ‘Find Rest for your Soul: Escaping addictive behaviour by finding a better joy. A ten-session Bible study exploring the heart behind addiction — and how the Gospel offers more than any false promise can deliver. Working through Scripture, each session combines close reading with personal reflection.
